A  CATALOGUE  OF  THE   PRINTED  BOOKS. 

MANUSCRIPTS,  AUTOGRAPH  LETTERS. 

AND    ENGRAVINGS.    COLLECTED 

BY    HENRY    HUTH.    WITH 

COLLATIONS     AND 

BIBLIOGRAPHICAL 

DESCRIPTIONS, 


? 


"  L'etude  de  la  bibliographie,  si  aride  en  apparence  pour  qui 
ne  la  considere  que  superficiellement,  est  loin,  pour  qui  I'examine 
de  plus  pr^s,  d'etre  depourvue  d'un  certain  charme ;  elle  offre  a 
I'esprit  observateur  bien  des  fails  curieux,  bien  des  anecdotes 
piquantes,  bien  des  rapprochements  singuliers.  Voila  pourquoi 
sans  doute  plusieurs  hommes  de  lettres  distingues,  des  poetes 
meme,  se  sont  livres  a  cette  etude  avec  autant  d'ardeur  que  de 
succes. " — Brunet.

UBALDINI,  Petrucci.  [Liber  precum.]  lAboitt  1580.]  Sm.  8vo. 

MS.  on  paper  written  in  a  beautiful  Italian  hand  with  gilt  capitals.  In  the 
original  but  worn  binding. 

This  volume  appears  from  the  initials  E.  R.  surmounted  by  a  crown,  which  are 
illuminated  on  the  first  two  leaves,  to  have  belonged  to  Queen  Elizabeth,  and  was 
probably  presented  to  her  by  the  author,  who  was  an  Italian  Protestant  refugee. 
He  was  the  author  of  "  Le  Vite  delle  donne  illustri  del  regno  d'Inghilterra  e  del 
regno  di  Scotia  &c.    G.  Wolf.     1591."  4to,  and  some  other  pieces. 

UBEDA,  Francisco  DE.  Libro  de  entretenimiento,  de  la  picara 
Ivstina,  en  el  qual  debaxo  de  graciosos  discursos,  se  encierran  proue- 
chosos  auisos.  En  Brvccllas,  En  casa  de  Oliuero  Brnnello,  en  la  Ftiente 
de  oro.     Ailo  M.D.C.  VIII.     Sm.  8vo. 

Title  and  preliminaries,  8  leaves;  A — Ff  4  in  eights,  the  last  leaf  blank.  Brunei 
must  be  mistaken  in  describing  the  title  to  this  book  as  engraved  on  wood.  In  this 
copy  at  least  it  is  letterpress.  From  the  library  of  Bishop  Burnet,  with  his  book- 
plate at  the  back  of  the  title. 

UBERTI,  Luc.  Ant.  Libro  Dabaco  che  insegna  a  fare  ogni  ragione 
mercadatile ;  i  a  ptegare  le  terre  c6  larte  di  la  geometria :  1  a  lire 
nobilissime  ragione  straordinarie  c5  la  Tariffa  come  respodeno  li  pen 
1  monede  de  molte  terre  del  modo  con  la  inclita  citta  di  Vinegia.  [At 
the  end.]  Opus  lucha  atonio  de  uberti  fe  1  uinetia.  S^Sens'  alcuna  data.~\ 
Sm.  8vo.    Black  letter.     Woodcuts.    BR.  M. 

A — K  in  eights.  The  volume  is  described  by  Brunet  (vol.  i.,  p.  i)  as  consisting 
of  only  78  leaves,  but  a  complete  copy  should  certainly  contain  80  leaves.  Many 
pages  are  entirely  xylographic.

UNCASING  OF  MACHIAVEL.  The  Vncasing  of  Machivils

A  satire  in  verse.  The  Answer  is  explanatory  of  the  satirical  meaning  of  the 
first  portion. 

UNFORTUNATE  JACK.  The  Second  Part  of  Unfortunate 
Jack. 

Jack  comes  once  upon  the  Stage, 

And  shews  the  People  of  the  Age, 

His  Fortunes  and  his  discontent, 

And  finally  his  banishment. 

And  whatsoever  of  him  I  hear, 

I  purpose  to  you  to  declare, 

Being  full  of  honest  mirth  and  wit, 

The  merriest  Book  that  e'er  was  writ. 
London  :  Printed  by  and  for  C.  B.  and  sold  by  C.  Bates  at  the  Sun  and 
Bible  in  Gilt-spur-strect.    [About  1675.]    Sm.  8vo.    G.  M. 
A — B  4  in  eights.     In  verse.     Woodcut  on  title. 

The  first  part  of  this  History  appeared  under  the  title  of  "  The  Unfortunate 
Son  ;  Or,  A  Kind  Wife  is  worth  Gold."  A  copy,  the  only  one  yet  noticed,  is  in  the 
Pepysian  library.  In  the  same  collection  occurs  a  copy  of  the  second  part,  sm.  8vo., 
1 68 1.  But  "a  Pamphlett  called  the  vnfortunate  Sonne "  was  licensed  to  Richard 
Harper  on  the  loth  August,  1635.     See  Arber,  iv.,  318.

URSPRUNG—UTENIIOVIUS.  1505 

A — I  in  fours,  the  first  and  last  leaves  blank. 

These  Epigrams  were  originally  published  in  1641,  and  the  present  edition  is 
nothing  more  than  the  unsold  copies  of  that  impression  with  a  new  title-page.  The 
volume  is  dedicated  to  the  Marquis  of  Hamilton  in  an  Epistle,  with  some  of  the 
terms  of  which  Mr.  Collier  ("  Bibliogr.  Catalogue,"  ii.,  460)  very  properly  finds  fault. 
The  author,  Sir  Thomas  Urchard,  or  Urquhart,  of  Cromarty,  is  well  known  as  the 
earliest  translator  of  Rabelais  into  English. 

In  the  present  copy  is  the  usual  full  length  portrait,  by  Glover,  with  an  English 
couplet  beneath  it,  signed  "W.  S.  ;"  but  an  infinitely  rarer  engraving  (also  by 
Glover)  is  inserted,  which  represents  a  full  length  figure  of  the  author  seated  on 
Parnassus,  surrounded  by  the  Muses,  two  of  whom  are  about  to  crown  him  with 
garlands.  The  winged  horse  Pegasus  hovers  over  his  head.  The  impression  of 
the  usual  full  length  portrait  has  some  inscriptions  which  do  not  appear  on  it  as 
issued  with  the  edition  of  1641.  From  Dr.  Bliss's  collection.  A  MS.  note  states 
that  he  had  never  heard  of  a  second  impression  of  the  extra  frontispiece  inserted  in 
this  copy. 

There  is  also  in  the  library  a  fine  tall  copy  of  the  Epigrams  of  the  issue  of  1646 
(from  the  collections  of  Bindley  and  Skegg),  with  the  print  in  the  first  state,  dated 
1641,  and  also  a  duplicate  of  the  plate  as  altered,  inserted  since  Bindley's  time. 

URSPRUNG  vnd  Vrsach  diser  Auffrur  Tciitscher  Nation.  Das 
Lied  mag  man  singen,  in  Briider  Veiten  thon.  Getruckt  zu  Aiigspurg 
durch  Valentin  Othmar.    [Cy'.]   4to. 

Six  leaves.

I506  VADE  MECUM—VADIANUS. 


V. 

VADE  MECUM  FOR  MALT-WORMS.  A  Vade  Mecum  for 
Malt- Worms  :  or,  a  Guide  to  Good  Fellows :  being  A  Description  of  the 
Manners  and  Customs  of  the  most  Eminent  Fublick  Houses  in  and 
about  the  Cities  of  London  and  Westminster.  With  A  hint  on  the 
Props  (or  Principal  Customers)  of  each  House.  In  a  Method  so  plain, 
that  any  Thirsty  Person  (of  the  meanest  Capacity)  may  easily  find  the 
nearest  Way  from  one  House  to  another.  Illustrated  with  proper  cuts. 
Dedicated  to  the  Brewers.  London  :  Printed  and  sold  by  T.  Bickertou 
at  tJie  Crowne  m  Paternoster-Row.    [1720.]    8vo.   bl.  M. 

A — G  in  eights. 

The  first  edition  of  the  first  part.     From  Mr.  Daniel's  library. 

A  Vade  Mecum  for  Malt-worms  :  or,  a  Guide  to  Good-Fel- 
lows :  .  .  .  The  Second  Edition.  Illustrated  with  proper  Cuts.  Dedi- 
cated to  the  Brewers.  London :  Printed  and  Sold  by  T.  Bickertou  at 
the  Crown  in  Pater nostcr-Row.  \Septeinb.  1720.]  (ii.)  A  Guide  for  Malt- 
Worms.  The  Second  Part  .  .  .  Done  by  several  Hands.  Illustrated 
with  proper  Cuts.  Sold  by  T.  Bickerton  in  Paternoster-Row.  Price  6d. 
wliere  the  First  Part  may  be  had  at  the  same  Price.  \_Septemb.  1720.] 
In  one  vol.    8vo.    The  edges  iincut.    R.  M. 

Part  I  :  A — G  in  fours,  besides  a  half  title.     Part  2  :  A — F  in  fours. 

From  the  collections  of  Mr.  Tyrrell  and  Sir  W.  Tite,  and  the  only  complete  copy 
of  both  parts  known.  It  was  from  this  copy  that  M.  Berjeau  made  his  reprint. 
The  dates  between  brackets  given  above  are  inserted  on  each  title  in  the  hand- 
writing of  Narcissus  Luttrell,  by  whom  the  two  tracts  were  doubtless  purchased 
at  the  period  of  pubHcation,  as  in  the  same  hand  we  have  the  price  given,  i.e. 
5(/.  for  each  portion. 

The  copy  referred  to  by  Sir  W.  Tite,  in  an  autograph  note  on  the  flyleaf,  as 
being  in  the  possession  of  a  country  clergyman,  was  only  an  imperfect  one  of  the 
first  part. 

"  Every  leaf  (amounting  to  upwards  of  one  hundred)  is  decorated  by  a  woodcut 
of  the  sign  of  some  well-known  tippling  establishment,  many  of  which  are  known  by 
the  same  sign  at  the  present  day,  while  underneath  the  qualities  of  the  liquors  are 
rehearsed,  and  the  names  of  the  guzzlers  or  frequenters  rendered,  their  drinking 
capabilities  being  faithfully  recorded  in  verse  ;  the  names  given  to  many  of  the 
London  drinks  have  fallen  into  oblivion  or  disuse,  while  numerous  houses,  mentioned 
as  being  well-frequented,  no  longer  exist." — Note  in  Sir  W.  Tite's  Catalogue.

M.D.XV.    Viennae.  4to. 

A — D  in  fours. 

"  First  edition,  of  the  very  highest  rarity  and  geographical  importance.  Unknown 
to  Harrisse,  who  says,  under  15 15,  "  We  find  under  this  date  in  Maittaire  ("  An- 
nales,"  vol.  ii.,  pt.  i,  p.  267);  Rudolphus  Agricola  junioris  ad  Joachimum  Vadianum 
Epistola  de  locorum  nonnuUorum  obscuritate  cum  Joach.  Vadiani  Epistola 
responsoria.  4to.,  but  we  are  unable  to  state  whether  the  Epistle  of  Vadianus 
mentioned  in  that  work  is  the  same  which  entitles  the  various  editions  of  Pomponius 
Mela  by  Vadianus  to  a  place  in  this  compilation."  With  the  exception  of  the 
Letters  of  Columbus  and  Vespucci,  and  the  first  Decade  of  Peter  Martyr  of  151 1, 
we  know  of  no  other  single  publication  up  to  1515  comparable  with  this  in  point  of 
historical  and  geographical  interest,  so  far  as  the  new  discoveries  affect  the 
geography  of  the  ancients  and  are  interpreted  by  it.  We  neither  forget  nor  under- 
value the  Ptolemy  of  150S  (with  its  extraordinary  map  by  Johan  Ruysch,  and  "The 
New  Description  of  the  World"  by  Beneventanus),  nor  the  editions  of  151 1  and 
1 5 13,  nor  the  geographical  work  of  Schoner  this  same  year.  This  little  book  of 
Vadianus  was  suggested  and  edited  by  his  friend  Rudolfus  Agricola,  who  addresses 
his  Dedicatory  Letter  to  Caspar  Ursinus.  Then  follows  Agricola's  Letter  to  Vadianus 
(omitted  in  the  Pomponius  Mela  of  15 18,  where  this  letter  of  Vadianus  is  reprinted 
in  full)  in  which,  after  some  pleasant  gossip,  Agricola  asks  him  to  explain  a  passage 
in  Pliny  about  the  Pygmies,  a  line  in  Persius — 

"  Sic  costa  longo  subduximus  Apenino  ; " 

and  thirdly,  to  give  his  opinion  about  the  Antipodes,  which,  he  says,  are  denied  by 
Lactantius,  Firmianus,  and  Augustine  ;  while  Pliny,  Pomponius,  Strabo,  Macrobius, 
and  many  others  do  not  deny  them,  although  they  speak  differently  about  them. 
He  adds  that  Laurentius  Corvinus,  his  beloved  preceptor,  agrees  with  the  Modems, 
but  he  himself  does  not  know  what  to  do  between  the  Fathers  and  the  learned 
authorities.  He  then  says  :  "  It  has  not  escaped  me  with  what  diligence  you 
have  promised  the  books  of  Pomponius  Mela  de  situ  orbis  to  the  studious  next 
Summer.^'  Agricola's  Letter  is  dated  September,  15 14.  In  a  postscript,  he  asks 
questions  about  Lake  Acronius,  a  passage  in  Lucan,  iS;c. 

In  his  answer  to  Agricola's  Letter,  Vadianus,  after  replying  to  the  first  two 
queries,  has  a  very  long  dissertation  upon  the  Antipodes,  whose  existence  he  demon- 
strates in  a  variety  of  ways,  telling  Agricola  that  he  ought  to  be  as  certain  that  there 
are  Antipodes  as  that  he  has  fingers  and  nails.  He  enters  at  length  on  the  question  of 
the  relative  latitude  and  longitude  of  Antipodes  in  different  parts,  &c.  He  demolishes 
the  old  objection  of  people  walking  with  their  heads  downwards  by  saying  that  every- 
thing tends  to  the  centre  of  gravity  [and  this  two  centuries  before  the  fall  of  New- 
ton's apple,  and  the  new  birth  of  the  doctrine  of  gravitation.]  He  refutes  Augustine 
and  Lactantius  at  length.  He  says  that  to  get  to  Catagara  its  Antipodes  will  be  in 
Western  Africa,  near  the  Sinus  Hesperius,  but  that  the  inhabitants  of  America  have 
their  Antipodes  much  more  to  this  side.     He  also  says  he  will  write  more  about  the 


i5o8  VADIANUS—V^NIUS. 

Periaci  in  his  Annotations  {Pranotamentis)  to  Pomponius.  Throughout  the  treatise 
he  seems  well  acquainted  with  the  progress  of  discovery  by  the  Portuguese  and 
Spanish,  and  is  acquainted  with  all  the  books  printed  relative  to  them.  He  adopts 
without  hesitation  the  suggestion  of  the  Gymnasium  of  St.  Did,  and  calls  the 
Mundus  Novics  (that  is  Brazil)  America,  without  explanation  or  qualification.  It 
is  not  a  little  singular  that  this  original  special  edition  is  not  noticed  by  any  geo- 
graphers or  bibliographers  who  have  written  about  America,  though  Humboldt, 
D'Avezac,  and  many  others  have  used  with  effect  the  reprint  of  it  at  the  end  of  the 
Pomponius  Mela,  1518."— j1/^.  note  by  Mr.  H.  Stevens.

now  known  as  South  America.  There  are  in  the  book  also  two  large  woodcut 
initial  letters,  representing  Elisha,  and  Christ  clearing  the  Temple,  which  are  evi- 
dently from  designs  by  Holbein.

In  the  printer's  preface,  addressed  "  To  the  Reader,"  we  are  told  "  The  History 
here  written,  was  translated  out  of  French  into  English  above  lOo  years  ago,  by  one 
Henry  Watson,  and  since  that  time  it  hath  by  him  been  Corrected,  and  put  into  a 
more  plysant  stile,  and  so  followed  on  to  the  Presse  till  this  present  Edition." 

In  Lowndes  it  is  said  that  four  leaves  of  an  edition  printed  by  Wynkyn  de  Worde 
are  in  the  Duke  of  Devonshire's  library,  but  of  the  identity  of  this  edition  there  is 
no  certainty.  ' 

In  the  "Bibliotheca  Heber.,"  pt.  ix.,  Nos.  31  ii  and  31 12,  are  two  editions 
printed  by  W.  Coplande,  with  variations  in  the  colophon,  and  of  the  second  of 
these  another  copy  was  sold  in  Mr.  Corser's  library,  pt.  7,  No.  369.  The  next 
edition  on  record  is  that  entered  in  the  St3.tioners'  Register,  Arber's  "  Transcript," 
ii.,  p.  210,  "  8  August!  [1586]  Thomas  Purfoote.  Receaved  of  him  for  printinge  the 
oldi:  Booke  of  Valentine  and  Orson  vi''.  Alwaies  provided  that  y<^  cumpanie 
shall  haue  them  at  his  handes."  We  know  of  no  other  edition  till  1637,  and  then 
followed  that  described  above.

VALTAN,  Petrus  Ludovicus.  Ad  honorem  Trinitatis  im- 
mense Patrem  et  Filii  et  Spiritus  Sancti  Unius  Dei.  Petri  Ludovici 
Valte  Andegavensis  Archidiaconi.  Super  qualibet  dictione  Symboli 
Apostolici.  Opusculum  quoddam  incipit.  Quod  Sacre  Theologie 
Parisii  presentatum  :  fidei  Catholice  consonum  approbatum  est.  Sm. 
Folio. 

MS.  on  twenty-three  leaves  of  vellum. 

This  volume  contains,  as  will  be  seen  from  the  foregoing  title,  a  commentary  on 
the  Apostles'  Creed.  It  is  an  admirable  example  both  of  caligraphy  and  illumina- 
tion. Each  word  of  the  Creed  is  written  in  blue  on  a  beautifully  designed  scroll  and 
beneath  each  scroll  are  six  lines  of  Latin  verse,  beginning  always  with  the  word  on 
the  scroll  above.     The  first  verse  runs  : — 

"  Credo  fide  militanti 

Vera  certa  et  constant! 

In  te  unum  summum  deum 

Orans  ut  animum  meum 

In  hac  fide  tuearis 

Pro  salute." 


VALTELLINE—VALTURIUS.  1513 

The  first  page  is  occupied  witli  an  exquisite  miniature  of  St.  Peter,  seated  at  a 
desk  writing  his  Gospel,  within  a  beautifully  designed  architectural  border.  The 
miniatures  of  the  other  Apostles  arc  arranged  throughout  the  book  in  the  following 
unusual  order  :  St.  Andrew,  St.  James  the  Great,  St.  John,  St.  Thomas,  St.  James 
the  Less,  St.  Philip,  St.  Bartholomew,  St.  Matthew,  St.  Simon,  St.  Jude,  St. 
Matthias. 

In  the  year  1500  this  beautiful  volume  was  presented  by  the  author  to  Isabella, 
Queen  of  Spain,  to  whom  the  author  addresses  the  following  preface  : — 

"  Excellentissime  potentissime  et  serenissime  Principi  Elisabeth  dei  gratia  Regine 
Castelle  Aragonu  Sicilie  Granate  &c.  Petrus  Ludovicus  de  valtan,  patria  cathalanus, 
andegauesis  archidiaconus,  christianissimi  Regis  P'rancie  impresentia  hie  legatus. 
Cum  humillima  atque  debita  obedientia  Salutem  Didici  Regina  excellentissinia  inter 
cetera  memoratu  digna  que  Regiis  tuis  moribus  atque  nomini  plurimum  venera- 
tionis  acquirunt  ;  te  latine  lingue  peritiam  (quo  altius  et  terebrantius  que  fidei  sunt 
valeas  adipisci)  no  diu  est,  fuisse  consecutam.  Et  huius  rei  gratia,  in  latinis 
operibus  lectitandis  baud  parum  sepenumero  delectari  quod  profecto  non  minus 
credendum  mihi  quam  admirabile  visum  est.  Noui  enim  his  diebus  inter  agendum 
et  tua  cum  Serenitate  super  negociis  ipsius  legationis  communicandu  qua  bonitate, 
qua  fide,  quo  sermone  cuius  tandem  ingenii  claritate  decoreris. 

"  Quamobrem  non  miretur  precor  Tua  Maiestas  :  nee  cuique  omnium  indecens 
quoquo  pacto  probetur  :  Si  presens  opusculum  de  apicibus  suffie  Trinitatis  elabo- 
ratum,  Tibi  q  q  mulieri  presentatum  iri  decreverim  Scio  Maiestatem  tuam  in 
ipso  tuo  genere  muliebri  :  et  viris  equipollere  litteratis  et  omnia  que  ad  fidei  re- 
ligione  spectat,  maxima  cum  devotione  rimari.  Vale  Regina  Serenissima  mu- 
lierum  nostri  temporis  omnium  precipua.  Ex  amenissimo  suburbano  Granate.  xiii". 
kaleii  Decembres  anno  dominice  natiuitatis  et  sacri  iubilei  Millesimo  Quin- 
gentesimo." 

VALTELLINE.  Alia  Maesta  Christianissima.  II  clero,  et  cattolici 
di  Valtellina.    \_Senz'  alcuna  data.']    4to.    R.  M. 

A — D  iii  and  A — D  ii,  besides  the  second  title. 

Two  memorials  for  the  extirpation  of"  Heretics  "  (Waldenses)  in  the  Valtelline. 
They  are  unnoticed  by  Muston  ("  Bibliographie  des  Vaudoises  ")  or  other  biblio- 
graphers.    They  were  printed  about  1623. 

[VALTURIUS  (ROBERTUS)  de  re  militari  libri  XII.  ad  Sigism. 
Pandulfum  Malatestam,  edente  P.  Ramusio.]  [Colophon.]  Johannes 
ex  uerona  oritindus :  Nicolai  cyrugiq  medici  filius :  Art  is  impressoriq 
magister :  hunc  de  re  miliiari  librnm  elegatdissiuiuin :  litteris  & 
figuratis  signis  sua  in  patria  primus  iinprgssit.  An.  AI.CCCCLXXII. 
Folio.     Woodcuts.    R.  M. 

This  volume  consists  of  262  leaves,  including  4  leaves  of  table  and  3  blank 
leaves,  two  of  which  follow  the  table,  and  the  other  forms  folio  176.  It  is  impossible 
to  give  a  more  exact  collation,  as  the  book  is  not  only  without  folios,  signatures,  or 
catchwords,  but  a  great  many  leaves  consist  of  single  woodcuts,  and  in  others  the 
text  is  complete  at  the  end  of  the  page,  and  does  not  run  on  to  the  next.  The 
only  means  of  exact  collation  therefore  is  to  compare  one  copy  with  another.  The 
engravings  are  extremely  curious,  and  the  figures,  where  they  occur,  are  drawn  with 
very  great  spirit.  They  are  ascribed  to  Matteo  Pasti  of  Verona,  see  Nagler, 
"  Kvinstler- Lexicon,"  vol.  ii.

VARIGNANUS—VARTHEMA.  15 15 

Title  and  preliminaries,  8  leaves,  the  last  blank  ;  folios  1-178  ;  table,  4  leaves. 

Though  this  author  is  unnoticed  by  any  other  bibliographer,  a  long  list  of  his 
writings  is  given  by  Antonio,  who  describes  this  book  as  printed  at  Madrid  in  1626, 
but  as  the  approbation  and  licence  to  print  are  dated  January  20,  1628,  it  appears 
probable  that  this  is  the  first  edition.

VAUGHAN,  Sir  William.  The  Golden  Fleece  Diuided  into 
three  Parts,  Vnder  which  are  discouered  the  Errours  of  Religion,  the 
Vices  and  Decayes  of  the  Kingdome,  and  lastly  the  wayes  to  get 
wealth,  and  to  restore  Trading  so  much  complayned  of  Transported 
from  Cambrioll  Colchos,  out  of  the  Southermost  Part  of  the  Hand, 
commonly  called  the  Newfoundland.  By  Orpheus  Junior,  For  the 
generall  and  perpetuall  Good  of  Great  Britaine.  London,  Printed  for 
Francis  Williams,  and  are  to  bee  sold  at  his  Shop  at  the  signe  of  the 
Globe,  oner  against  the  Roy  all  Exchange.    1626.   4to.    O.  M. 

a — c  in  fours;  A,  2  leaves  ;  B — V  3  in  fours.  Part  2  :  Aa — Nn  in  fours,  and 
a  leaf  of  Oo.  Part  3  :  Aaa — Mmm  in  fours.  There  are  no  separate  titles  to  parts 
2  and  3.  With  a  folding  map  of  "  Newfound  land  described  by  Captaine  John 
Mason." 

The  author  dedicates  his  work  in  English  and  Latin  verse  to  King  Charles.  In 
this  dedication  he  says,  "  The  Mvses  and  the  Graces,  by  the  hands  of  Orpheus 
Junior,  doe  here  present  this  Treatise  of  the  Golden  Fleece  at  the  Feet  of  the  most 
Noble,  Mightie,  and  hopeful!  King  of  Great  Britaine;"  and  he  concludes  his 
address  "To  the  Indifferent  Readers"  with  the  words  :  "—And  in  the  Third  Part 
I  will  discouer  the  certainty  of  the  Golden  Fleece,  which  shall  restore  vs  to  all 
worldly  Happinesse."

[VERSTEGAN,  R.]   Theatrvm  Crudelitatum  Haereticorum  Nostri 

Temporis.  A  ntverpice,  ApvdA  driamim  Huberti.  A  nnoM.D.LXXX  VII. 

4to.    Plates. 

Pp.  95,  including  the  title. 

Odes  in  Imitation  of  the  Seaven  Penitential  Psalmes,  with  sun- 

drie  other  Poems  and  Ditties  tending  to  deuotion  and  pietie.  [Printed 
at  Antwerp  by  Robert  Brnney^   Anno  Domini.  M.  D.  CI.    8vo.    R.  M. 

A — H  4  in  eights. 

Dedicated  "To  the  Vertvovs  Ladies  and  Gentlewomen  Readers  of  these  Ditties." 
Here  the  author  says  that  his  original  intention  was  not  to  commit  his  poems  to 
the  press,  but  that  having  shown  them  to  a  friend,  and  having  been  urged  to  make 
them  public,  he  had  not  only  done  so,  but  had  made  additions  to  the  original  col- 
lection ;  and  he  asks  his  lady  readers  to  procure  some  skilful  musician  to  set  them 
to  proper  tunes. 

The  only  other  perfect  copies  known  are  three  :  in  the  Bodleian,  at  Britwell 
(formerly  Heber's),  and  Mr.  Addington's,  purchased  at  Corser's  sale,  Mr.  Corser 
having  obtained  it  at  Pickering's  sale  in  1854,  The  Bibliotheca  Anglo-Poetica 
copy  was  not  complete. 

There  can  be  no  doubt  that  the  book  was  executed  by  the  same  printer  who 
issued  Verstegan's  "  Restitution  of  Decayed  Intelligence"  in  1605  ;  the  typogra- 
phical ornaments  arc  exactly  similar.

First  edition.  This  collection  of  voyages  and  the  translations  of  it  into  German 
Latin  and  French  is  placed  under  Vesputius,  because  it  is  so  placed  by  Brunet,  and 
the  real  editor  or  compiler  of  the  volume  appears  to  be  unknown.  It  contains  only 
a  portion  of  the  voyages  of  Vesputius,  but  includes  those  of  several  other  early 
navigators.

Brunei  has  given  a  collation  of  these  pieces,  which  is  correct  except  as  to  the 
first,  to  which  he  assigns  only  fifty-seven  leaves  instead  of  fifty-eight.  The  copy  he 
saw  probably  wanted  the  first  or  dedicatory  leaf,  which  is  blank  on  the  recto. 
There  being  no  signatures  or  catchwords  its  absence  would  not  easily  be  detected.

VICARS,  John.  Mischeefes  Mysterie :  or.  Treasons  Master- 
peece,  The  Powder-plot.  Inuented  by  hellish  Malice,  prevented  by 
heauenly  Mercy  :  truely  related.  And  from  the  Latine  of  the  learned 
and  reuerend  Doctour  Herring  translated,  and  very  much  dilated.  By 
lohn  Vicars.  London,  Printed  by  E.  Griffin,  dzvelling  in  the  Little  Olde 
Bayly  neere  the  signe  of  the  Kings  head.    1617.   4to. 

IT,  4  leaves  ;  A — P  in  fours.  In  two  parts,  with  separate  titles,  but  continuous 
signatures.     In  stanzas  of  six  lines. 

This  is  an  expansion  of  Herring's  "Pietas  Pontificia,"  printed  in  Latin  verse  in 
1606  and  1609.  It  is  dedicated  by  Vicars  to  Sir  John  Lemmon,  Lord  Mayor,  Sir 
W.  Craven,  President  of  Christ's  Hospital,  the  Treasurer,  Mr.  Richard  Heath,  and 
all  the  Governors  of  that  Institution.  There  are  commendatory  verses  by  Thomas 
Salisbury,  Joshua  Sylvester,  N.B.  (Nicholas  Breton.?),  &c.  On  the  title  to  the  first 
part  is  a  woodcut  which  represents  an  eagle  [Lord  Monteagle]  bringing  a  letter  in 
its  beak  to  King  James,  seated  on  his  throne  ;  the  title  to  the  second  part  has  a 
cut  representing  the  discovery  of"  Infernall  Fauks." 

Babel's  Balm  :   or  the    Honey-combe   of  Romes   Religion. 

With  A  ncate  draining  and  straining-out  of  the  rammish  Honey  thereof. 
Sung  in  tenne  most  elegant  Elegies  in  Latine,  by  that  most  worthy 
Christian  Satyrist,  Master  George  Good-winne.  And  Translated  into 
tenne  English  Satyres,  by  the  Muses  most  vnworthy  Eccho,  lohn 
Vicars.  A  vgvstine.  Pride  hath  an  appetite  of  Vnitie  and  Omnipotcncy. 
Imprinted  at  London  by  George  Purslowe  for  Nathanaell  Browne,  and 
are  to  be  sold  at  his  Shop,  at  the  vpper  end  of  the  long  Walke  necre 
Little  S.  Bartholomews.    1624.   4to. 


1530  VICARS. 

C,  4  leaves,  title  on  C  2  ;  A — R  2  in  fours  ;  C— Q  in  fours. 

Dedicated  to  William,  Earl  of  Pembroke,  in  verse.  Goodwin's  dedication  in 
prose  to  Sir  Robert  Nanton  is  retained  and  translated.  An  acrostic  on  Goodwin's 
name  follows,  then  some  more  verses  by  Vicars,  and  some  complimentary  lines  by 
Thomas  Salisbury.

knowledgment of  Parliamentary  Mercies  to  our  English-Nation. 
Wherein  is  contained  a  breife  enumeration  of  all,  or  the  most  of  Gods 
free  favours  and  choise  Blessings  multiplied  on  us  since  this  Parliament 
first  began.  By  lohn  Vicars.  London,  Printed  for  T/ionias  Under/nil, 
and  are  to  be  sold  at  the  Bible  in  Wood-street.  M.DCXLI.  4to. 
Four  leaves.     In  verse.     With  marginal  notes. 

A  Looking-Glasse  for  Malignants  :    or,   Gods  hand  against 

God-haters.  Containing  A  most  terrible  yet  true  relation  of  the  many 
and  fearefull  personall  examples  (in  these  present  times,  since  the  yeere 
1640.)  of  Gods  most  evident  and  immediate  wrath  against  our  malevo- 
lent Malignants.  Together  with  a  Caveat  for  Cowards  and  unworthy, 
(either  timorous  or  treacherous)  Newters.  Collected  For  Gods  honour, 
and  the  ungodlies  horrour,  by  John  Vicars.  [Quotations.]  London, 
Printed  for  John  Rothzvell,  at  the  signe  of  the  Sunne  in  Pauls  Church- 
yard.   1643.    4to.    The  edges  uncut. 

A — F  in  fours,  title  on  A  2. 

Dedicated  to  Sir  John  Wollastone,  Lord  Mayor  of  London,  and  to  Lady 
WoUastone. 

Magnalia    Dei    Anglicana.     Or,    England's    Parliamentary 

Chronicle.  Containing  a  full  and  faithful!  Series,  and  Exact  Narration 
of  all  the  most  memorable  Parliamentary  Mercies,  and  mighty  (if  not 
miraculous)  Deliverances,  great  and  glorious  Victories,  and  admirable 
Successes,  of  the  Counsels  and  Armies  of  this  present  Parliament, 
both  by  Sea  and  by  Land  .  .  .  Collected  cheifly  for  the  high  Honour 
of  our  Wonder  working  God  ;  and  for  the  unexpressible  Comfort  of  all 
Cordiall  English  Parliamentarians.  By  the  most  unworthy  Admirer  of 
Them,  John  Vicars.  [Quotation  from  Gen.  xlix.  22-4.]  Imprinted  at 
London  for  J.  Rotlnvell  .  .  .  and  T ho.  Under  hill  .  .  .   1646.    4to.    R.

volume  as  above.  Part  3  ("  God's  Arke,"  &c.) :  A — Ss  3  in  fours,  title  on  A  2.

VICARS— VICE  LLUS.  1531 

A — D  in  fours,  including  the  engraved  title.  There  are  several  copper-plates  on 
the  letterpress. 

The  last  event  mentioned  in  the  text  is  of  the  5th  April,  1645.  At  the  end 
occurs  :  "  Collected  by  John  Vicars." 

VICARS,  John.  England'.s  Worthies.  Under  whom  all  the  Civill 
and  Bloudy  Warres  since  Anno  1642,  to  Anno  1647,  are  related. 
Wherein  are  described  the  severall  Battails,  Encounters,  and  Assaults 
of  Cities,  Townes,  and  Castles  at  severall  times  and  Places  ;  so  that  the 
Reader  may  behold  the  time,  yeare,  and  event  of  every  Battle,  Skir- 
mish and  Assault.  Wherein  London-Apprentices  had  not  the  least 
share.  As  Also,  Severall  Victories,  by  Sea  by  the  Noble  Admirall, 
Robert  Earle  of  Warwick.  [Quotations  from  Heb.  ii.  and  Psal.  xliv.  3.] 
London,  Printed  for  J.  RotJnvell  at  the  Sun  and  Fountain  in  Pauls 
Churchyard,  1647.    Sm.  8vo. 

A — G  7  in  eights.  A  duplicate  leaf,  A  2,  is  inserted  in  this  copy  with  a  brilliant 
impression  of  the  portrait  of  the  Earl  of  Essex  and  a  variation  in  the  inscription. 

This  volume  contains  eighteen  portraits,  some  of  which  are  by  W.  Hollar,  all  on 
the  letterpress.     From  Sir  W.  Tite's  collection.

VIES  DES  PHILOSOPHES.    4to. 

MS.  of  French  execution,  written  on  sixty-eight  leaves  of  vellum.  Probably 
executed  about  the  end  of  the  fifteenth  century.  It  contains  the  Hves  of  twenty  of 
the  Greek  philosophers,  beginning  with  Solon  and  ending  with  Theophrastus.  At 
the  beginning  of  each  life  is  a  conventional  portrait  of  the  subject  of  the  biography, 
well  executed  in  gold  and  colours. 

At  the  end  of  the  book  is  the  inscription,  "  Ce  lyure  est  amoy,  Gaillarde,"  and 
on  the  fly-leaf,  "  Ce  presant  liure  est  de  berthelemy  Pancatier."  It  was  subse- 
quently in  the  collection  of  Sir  W.  Tite.

feliciter  Amen.    \_Sine  ulla  notd.^   4to.   R.

VIRGILIUS  MARO,  Publius.    Opera.    Folio,    o.  M. 

MS.  of  Italian  execution  on  213  leaves  of  vellum,  beautifully  written  in  a  semi- 
gothic  hand.  The  first  page  has  an  interlaced  illuminated  border,  and  there  are 
numerous  capitals  illuminated  in  the  same  style. 

[Opera,  cum  aliis  opusculis.]    [Colophon.]    Presens  hec  Virgilii 

impressio  poete  clarissimi  in  alma  iirbe  Roma  facta  est. per  Vdal- 

ricuni  Galium  &  Simonem  de  Liica.  Anno  domini  M.CCCC.LXXiii. 
Die  uero.  HIT.  misis  Noicembris.    Folio.    BR.  M. 

This  third  Roman  edition  is  said  by  Brunet  to  be  equal  in  rarity  to  the  two 
which  preceded  it,  and  to  be  of  great  importance  in  that  a  great  number  of  the  pas- 
sages wanting  in  the  former  editions  are  here  inserted.  The  collation  given  by  M. 
Brunet  is  not  quite  correct,  as  he  makes  only  265  leaves,  including  the  two  which 
are  blank,  whereas  this  copy  contains  267.  The  discrepancy  appears  to  be  in  the 
last  part,  which  contains  the  13th  book  of  the  vEneid,  the  Priapeia,  Catalecta,  &c., 
and  a  leaf  of  register,  which  M.  Brunet  makes  to  consist  of  only  51  leaves  ;  but  it 
has  really  53,  and  should  have  that  number  according  to  the  register  at  the  end  of 
tlie  book. 

[Virgilii  Opera.]    [Colophon.]   Impressum  parmce  opera  &  im- 

pensis  Andrea  Portilice  Anno  Domini  M.CCCCLXXIX.  Quarto  Idus

*,  4  leaves,  with  title-page,  dedication,  and  "A  generall  somme";  A — U  2  in

Dedicated  "  To  the  ryght  honorable  and  my  verye  good  Lorde  Syr  Nicholas 
Bacon  knyght,  Lorde  Keeper  of  the  great  Seale  of  Englande,"  by  William  Wight- 
man,  who  dates  from  London,  the  6th  of  July,  1562.  This  dedication  contains  the 
following  interesting  information  :  "  Whitest  God  gaue  lyfe  and  health  to  Thomas 
Phaer  Doctour  of  Phisike,  I  had  some  moore  frendly  familiaritie  with  him  then 
moste  men  had.  In  whych  respect  he  did  before  his  last  departynge  downe  from 
hence  into  Penbroke  shire  of  speciall  trust  leaue  in  my  handes  the  eyght  and  nynth 
bookes  of  Virgilles  Eneidos,  by  him  translated  into  Englyshe  verse.  And  promised 
to  vse  all  hys  possible  diligence  for  the  finishing  of  the  other  three  bookes  then 
vtterly  vnbegonne  :  Declarynge  moreouer  vnto  me  that  hys  verye  mynde  and  pur- 
pose was  not  onely  to  prynt  the  former  part  agayne  for  reformation  of  some  faultes 
overslypt  vpon  the  first  impression,  but  also  hauying  finished  the  same  to  dedicate 
the  whole  worke  vnto  your  Lordship  ....  Albeit,  it  pleased  God  to  preuent  hym 
by  death  ....  I  do  herewith  present  vnto  your  honour  the  first  seuen  bookes 


1538  VIRGILIUS. 

newly  prynted,  the  two  latter  bookes  as  I  had  them  with  his  owne  hande  roughly 
■written,  not  throughly  corrected,  and  so  much  of  the  tenth  booke  as  vpon  diligent 
searche  emongst  his  secrete  writynges  at  hys  house  in  kilgarron  foreste  in  Penbroke 
shire  my  selfe  coulde  fynde  or  come  by.  Marie  it  should  appcare  by  the  two  verses 
in  the  ende  of  this  booke  by  hym  translated  upon  his  death  bed  the  very  day  before 
he  dyed,  which  he  sent  vnto  me  subscribed  with  his  left  hand  (the  vse  of  the  right 
hande  beyng  taken  awaye,  through  the  hurte  ^vhereof  he  dyed)  that  he  had  gone  so 
much  further  as  those  verses  be  in  Virgilles  tenth  booke  "

translate,  and  sende  to  William  Wightman  Receyuour  of  South  Wales,"  and  they 
are  subscribed,  "  Thomas  Phaer  olim  tuus  nunc  dei."

oratour  7  poete/  Intytuled  Eneydos.  hath  be  translated  oute  of  latyn 
in  to  comyn  langage.  In  whiche  may  alle  valyaunt  prynces  and  other 
nobles  see  many  valorous  fayttes  of  armes.  And  also  this  present 
boke  is  necessarye  to  all  cytezens  Thabytaunts  in  tovvnes  and  castelles/ 

[Colophon.]     Here  fynysskcth  the  boke  of  Encydosj  compyled 

by  Vyrgylej  ivhiche  liatlie  be  translated  oute  of  latyne  in  to  frenskej  And 
oute  of  frenshe  reduced  in  to  Englysshe  by  me  Wyllrh  Ca.vtonj  the  x.vif. 
daye  of  Juyn.  the  yere  of  our  lorde.  M.  iiij  C.l.vxxx.  The  fythe  [sic] 
yere  of  the  Regne  of  kyttge  Henry  the  seiienth.    Folio.   Black  letter,   o.  M. 

Preface,  2  leaves  ;  table,  3  leaves  ;  B — L  7  in  eights. 

The  first  four  leaves  are  inlaid,  and  the  fifth  is  in  facsimile.  From  the  collec- 
tions of  Mr.  Hanrott  and  Mr.  Lancelot  Holland. 

This  work  is  a  literal  translation  from  the  French  book  described  above,  which 
was  no  doubt  the  "  lytyl  booke  in  frenshe,  named  Eneydos,"  which  Caxton  describes 
in  his  preface  as  the  original  of  his  work.  Mr.  Blades  was  unacquainted  with  the 
original,  and  suggests  that  Caxton  translated  from  a  MS.  no  longer  known,  but 
Brunet  remarks, "  C'est  probablement  d'apr^s  cette  paraphrase  anonyme  que  Caxton 
a  donnd  I'Eneide  en  anglais."

The  edition  appears  to  be  altogether  undescribed  by  bibhographers.

Aa,  8  leaves  ;  a — o  in  eights  ;  p,  6  leaves  ;  q — x,  in  eights  ;  y,  10  leaves  ;  z — tt 
in  eights;  w — xx  in  sixes.  The  last  leaf,  which  has  on  the  recto  the  words  "Vitas 
patrum"  over  a  large  woodcut,  and  on  the  verso  Caxton's  device,  is  here  in  facsimile. 

A  magnificent  specimen  of  old  English  typography,  and  considered  one  of  the 
masterpieces  of  W.  de  Worde's  press.

VIVES,  LUDOVICUS.  An  Introdvction  to  wysedome,  made  by 
Ludovicus  Vines,  and  translated  into  Englyshe  by  Rycharde  Mory- 
sine.  [Colophon.]  Londini  in  cedibus  Tliome  Bertheleti  typis  impress. 
Cum  priiiilegio  ad  imprimenditm  solum.  Anno.  M.D.XL.  Sm.  8vo. 
Black  letter. 

A — L  in  eights  ;  A  8  blank,  and  A  7  verso,  with  the  printer's  device. 

Dedicated  by  the  translator,  Sir  Richard  Moresine,  or  Morysine,  "  To  the  ryght 
worshypful  master  Gregory  Crumwell,  sonne  to  the  ryght  honorable  Lord  Crumwell, 
lorde  priuie  scale,"  in  an  epistle  occupying  several  pages.  He  observes  :  "  If  ye 
make  these  preceptes  yours,  by  vse  of  ihcm,  speakynge  as  they  teache  you,  work- 
ynge  as  they  aduise  your,  who  shall  have  more  cause  to  reioise  of  it,  than  you  youre- 
selfe  1  wherin  can  you  more  please  my  lord  your  fathers  mind  ?  what  thinge  can 
more  satisfye  his  desire .''  what  greater  comforte  can  come  to  his  lordeshyppe,  then 
to  se  you  moste  lyke  him  in  condicions .?  what  greater  worshyppe  to  you,  then  to 
treade  in  his  steppes  ?  .  .  ."  As  the  Earl  of  Essex  was  disgraced  and  executed  this 
very  year,  possibly  the  present  volume  was  suppressed,  as  no  record  of  any  other 
copy  of  this  edition  of  1540  appears  to  exist.  Herbert  cites  an  edition  by  the  same 
printer  in  1544,  which  he  had  not  seen  ;  but  a  copy  of  it  is  in  the  British  Museum.

A— t  in  eights,  the  first  leaf  blank;  aa— pp  in  eights;  qq,  10  leaves.  Thirty-two 
lines  to  a  full  page. 

This  is  by  Graesse  described  as  "  Imprimd  vers  147 1  chez  J.  Zainer  \  Uhii,"  but 
the  fact  of  the  book  having  signatures  would  indicate  a  year  or  two  later.  It  is 
believed  to  be  the  earliest  printed  German  and  Latin  dictionary.

Engraved  title  and  preliminaries,  3  leaves  ;  pp.  1-26  ;  then  follow  sigs.  A  and  B, 
4  leaves  each  ;  C,  6  leaves  ;  D — I  in  fours  ;  K,  5  leaves. 

After  p.  26  is  a  fine  portrait  of  the  author,  inscribed,  "  Preclari  &  Vera  Diuini 
Poete  Domini  loannis  Van-der  Noot  Patrity  Antuerpiensis  Effigies.  1573."  There 
are  eighteen  tine  engravings  on  the  text  besides  the  portrait  and  engraved  title. 
Some  of  the  plates  have  the  monogram  D.V.C.  [Dirck  Volkertsz  Coornhaert]. 

A  Theatre  wherein  be  represented  as  wel  the  miseries  & 

calamities  that  follow  the  voluptuous  Worldlings,  As  also  the  greate 
ioyes  and  plesures  which  the  faithfull  do  enioy.  An  Argument  both 
profitable  and  delectable,  to  all  that  sincerely  loue  the  word  of  God. 
Deuised  by  S.  lohn  vander  Noodt.  Scene  and  allowed  according  to 
the  order  appointed.  Imprinted  at  Lo7idon  by  Henry  Bynneman. 
Anno  Doinitti.  i^6g.  Cvm  Privilegio.  Sm.  8vo.  Black  letter.  Wood- 
cuts.   BL.  M. 

A — S  2  in  eights. 

Dedicated  by  Vondemoodt  to  Queen  Elizabeth.  See  Collier's  "  Bibliogr.  Cata- 
logue," ii.,  374-5.  This  volume  is  of  special  interest,  as  containing  the  earliest 
printed  verses  of  Edmund  Spenser.  An  edition  of  the  book  in  French  was  printed 
by  John  Day  in  1 568,  which  is  much  rarer  than  the  English  version,  but  the  Flemish 
was  the  original  of  both.

VOS—VOX.  1547 

The  last  edition  of  the  Golden  Legend.  There  is  no  title,  the  recto  of  the  first 
leaf  being  blank,  and  the  verso  occupied  by  a  large  woodcut  of  an  assembly  of  saints 
and  martyrs. 

From  the  library  of  Mr.  Maskell,  with  his  book-plate. 

VOS,  Martin  de.  A  series  of  17  engravings  representing  the 
Passion  and  sufferings  of  Christ,  engraved  by  Anton  Wierx  and 
Crispin  de  Pas.  Each  plate  is  surrounded  by  an  ornamental  border  of 
fine  design.    [About  1600.]    4to.

1 548  VO  YA  G E—  VO  YA  GE S. 

A — D  in  fours,  and  a  leaf  of  E.  This  exceedingly  rare  tract  was  evidently 
printed  abroad  ;  it  is  of  considerable  historical  interest  and  curiosity,  as  it  contains 
numerous  allusions  to  celebrated  characters  of  the  time.  The  author  was  clearly  a 
Royalist,  though  he  does  not  disclose  his  identity  ;  but  he  animadverts  in  severe 
terms  on  the  behaviour  of  the  men  of  the  other  party.

VOYAGES  OF  THE  DUTCH  EAST  INDIA  COMPANY. 
Begin  ende  Voortgangh/  van  de  Vereenighde  Nederlantsche  Geoctroy- 
eerde  Oost-Indische  Compagnie.  Vervatende  De  voornaemste  Rey-

Alles  Nevens  de  beschrijvinghen  der  Rijcken/  Eylanden/  Havenen/ 
Revieren/  Stroomen/  Rheeden/  Winden/  Diepten  en  Ondiepten; 
Mitsgaders  Religien/  Manieren/  Aerdt/  Politic  ende  Regeeringhe  der

thien  Voyagien/  den  meeren-deelen  voor  desen  noyt  in  'tlicht  geweest. 
Gedruckt  in  den  Jaere  1646.    2  vols.  Oblong  4to.  Large  paper.   Plates. 

A  full  collation  of  this  important  collection  of  Voyages  will  be  found  in  Muller, 
"  M(5m.  sur  les  Voyages  Nderlandais,"  pp.  g-13. 

The  first  edition  was  published  in  1644,  and  this  of  1646  varies  only  in  a  few 
particulars  which  M.  Muller  points  out.  It  is  known  as  the  collection  of  Isaac 
Commelin,  and  was  published  by  Jan  Jansz.,  though  no  name  of  either  editor  or  pub- 
lisher appears  on  it.

Dedicated  to  James,  Marquis  of  Hamilton,  by  William  Bowyer,  who  describes 
himself  as  a  soldier,  and  who  appears  to  have  been  the  editor  of  the  play.  In  the 
course  of  the  dedication,  however,  he  does  not  refer  at  all  to  the  writer,  whose 
initials  are  on  the  title-page.

third,  late  King  of  France,  to  be  both  commendable,  admirable,  and 
meritorious.  Wherein  the  saide  Apology  is  faithfully  translated, 
directly  answered,  and  fully  satisfied.  Let  God  be  Bulge  bctivixt  thee 
and  me.  Genes.  16.  At  London  Printed  for  Thomas  Woodcock,  and

Dedicated  by  R.  W.  to  Sir  W.  Butler,  Knight.  All  the  persons  concerned  in 
this  crime  were  Englishmen.  This  tract,  as  well  as  Ascham's  Discourse  on  Govern- 
ment, 1648,  is  unnoticed  by  Lowndes. 

W.,  T.  The  Optick  Glasse  of  Hvmors.  Or  The  touchstone  of  a 
golden  temperature,  or  the  Philosophers  stone  to  make  a  golden 
temper.  Wherein  the  foure  complections.  Sanguine,  Cholericke, 
Phlegmaticke,  Melancholicke  are  succinctly  painted  forth,  and  their 
externall  intimates  laide  open  to  the  purblind  eye  of  ignorance  it 
selfe,  by  which  euery  one  may  iudge  of  what  complection  he  is,  and 
considerably  learne  what  is  most  sutable  to  his  nature.  Lately 
pend  by  T.  W.  Master  of  Artes.  .  .  .  London.  Lniprinted  by  lo/nt 
Windet  for  Martin  Gierke,  and  are  to  be  sold  at  his  shop  without 
Aldcrsgate,  1607.    Sm.  8vo.    BL.  M. 

U,  8  leaves  ;  A,  4  leaves  ;  B — M  in  eights.  The  last  two  leaves  occupied  by 
the  errata.  Dedicated  "  To  the  right  worshipful  wise  and  learned  Knight,  Sir  Jvs- 
tinian  Lewin,"  by  T.  W.,  who  dates  "  from  my  study  in  St.  John's  x.  Calend.  of 
March." 

Lowndes  quotes  Dr.  Farmer's  "Essay  on  the  Learning  of  Shakespeare"  as 
pointing  to  "The  Optick  Glasse  of  Hvmors"  as  the  source  from  which  Shakespeare 
derived  the  saying  of  Shylock :  "  And  others  when  a  Bagpipe  sings  i'  th'  nose  can- 
not contain  their  urine  for  affection  ;"  whereas  what  Farmer  says  is,  that  the  story  is 
found  in  Scaliger's  "  Exercitations  against  Cardan,"  and  was  afterwards  printed  in 
Le  Loyer's  "  Treatise  of  Spectres,"  whence  Shakespeare  must  have  taken  it.  If  this 
were  so,  it  must  have  been  from  the  original  French,  as  the  English  version  did  not 
appear  till  1605.  All  that  Farmer  says  of  this  book  is :  "  The  passage  from  Scaliger 
is  likewise  to  be  met  with  in  '  The  Optick  Glasse  of  Hvmors,'  written  I  believe  by 
T.  Wombwell."     By  Lowndes  it  is  attributed  to  T.  Walkington.

nomians,  Familists.and  Libertines  That  Infected  the  Churches  Of  New- 
England  :  And  how  they  were  Confuted  by  The  Assembly  of  Ministers 
there  :  y\s  also  of  the  Magistrates  proceedings  in  Court  against  them.

ultimately  he  consented  to  make  some  additions.

WALDENSES— WALKER.  1553 

WALDENSES.  Matchlesse  Ciueltie,  declared  At  large  in  the 
ensuing  History  of  the  Waldcnses  :  Apparently  manifesting  unto 
the  World  the  horrible  Persecutions  which  they  have  suffered  by  the 
Papists,  for  the  space  of  four  hundred  and  fifty  years.  .  .  .  Likewise, 
Hereunto  is  added  an  exact  Narrative  of  the  late  Bloody  and  Barba- 
rous Massacres.  .  .  .  committed  on  many  thousands  of  the  Protestants, 
dwelling  in  the  Valleys  of  Piedmont.  .  .  .  Published  by  command  of 
his  Highness,  the  Lord  Protector.  London,  Printed  for  Ediuard 
Brezvster.  .  .  .  1655.    4to. 

A— K  2  in  fours  ;  Aa — Ss  in  fours  ;  Aaa — Sss  in  fours  ;  Aaaa — Nnnn  in  fours  ; 
the  Narrative,  *,  4  leaves  ;  A — G  2  in  fours. 

The  first  portion  bears  no  name  of  author  or  translator  ;  the  Narrative  at  the 
end  purports  to  have  been  collected  by  desire  of  the  Protector  by  J.  B.  Stouppe. 

WALKER,  Clement.  Relations  and  Observations,  Historical 
and  Politick,  upon  the  Parliament  begun  Anno  Dom.  1640.  Divided 
into  II.  Books:  i.  The  Mystery  of  the  two  Junto's,  Presbyterian  and 
Independent.  2.  The  History  of  Independency,  &c.  Together  with 
An  Appendix,  touching  the  Proceedings  of  the  Independent  Faction 
in  Scotland.  [Quotations.]  Printed  in  t/ie  Year  164&.  4to.  Frontis- 
piece.   R. 

Although  only  two  parts  are  mentioned  in  the  first  title,  there  were  four  parts  to 
the  work,  the  third  printed  in  1651,  and  the  fourth  in  1660:  they  are  all  bound  up 
together  here.  Part  i,  A — Bb  in  fours,  besides  the  frontispiece.  Part  2,  with  a 
new  title,  "  Anarchia  Anglicana,"  1649  ;  preliminaries,  6  leaves,  and  B — LI  in  fours, 
last  leaf  blank.  Part  3,  with  a  new  title,  "  The  High  Court  of  Justice,"  &c.,  1651  ; 
A — I  in  fours.  Part  4,  "  The  History  of  Independency,  The  Fourth  and  Last 
Part,"  with  a  new  title,  dated  1660  ;  A — R  2  in  fours.  Lowndes's  collation  varies 
from  that  of  the  present  copy  (see  p.  2810.) 

Walker  published  parts  1-3  under  the  name  of  Theodorus  Verax,  and  part  4 
under  the  fictitious  initials  T.  M. 

WALKER,  Sir  Edward.  Historical  Discourses  upon  Several 
Occasions  :  viz.  i.  The  happy  Progress  and  Success  of  the  Arms  of 
K.  Charles  I.  .  .  .  from  the  30th  of  March  to  the  23rd  of  November, 
1644.    Written  by  his   Majesty's  especial  Command,  and   corrected, 

almost  every  page,  with  his  own  Hand Together  with  perfect 

copies  of  all  the  Votes,  Letters,  .  .  .  London.  .  .  .  1705.  Folio. 
Large  paper. 

With  an  equestrian  portrait  after  Hollar  on  page  2  verso,  and  a  large  print  of

First  edition.     Printed  without  the  author's  permission.  It  includes  the  Speeches. 

Poems,   &c.   Written   by   Mr.   Ed.  Waller  of  Beckonsficld, 

Esquire  ;  lately  a  Member  of  the  Honourable  House  of  Commons. 
All  the  Lyrick  Poems  in  this  Booke  were  set  by  Mr.  Henry  Lawes 
Gent,  of  the  Kings  Chappell,  and  are  of  his  Majesties  Private  Musick. 
Printed  and  Published  according  to  Order.  London,  Printed  by  T.  W. 
for  Humphrey  Mosley,  at  the  Princes  Arms  in  Pauls  Church-yard. 
1645.    Sm.  8vo. 

A,  4  leaves  ;  B — G  in  eights  ;  I,  4  leaves. 

In  the  " Advertisement  to  the  Reader"  it  is  said:  "This  parcell  of  exquisit 
Poems,  have  pass'd  up  and  downe  through  many  hands  amongst  persons  of  the 
best  quality,  in  loose  imperfect  Manuscripts,  and  there  is  lately  obtruded  to  the 
world  an  adulterate  Copy,  surniptitiously  and  illegally  imprinted,  to  the  derogation 
of  the  Author  and  the  abuse  of  the  Buyer.  But  in  this  Booke  they  appcare  in 
their  pure  originals  and  true  genuine  colours."  An  examination  of  the  two  editions, 
however,  shows  that  this  edition  is  a  literal  reprint  of  that  which  is  spoken  of  in  such 


1556  WALLER. 

strong  terms  of  disapprobation.  The  misprints  and  corruptions  of  the  text  are 
exactly  the  same  in  both.  It  will  be  observed  that  the  first  is  "  Printed  for  Thomas 
Walkley,"  and  the  second  ''by  T.  W."  The  only  real  difference  between  the  two 
books  is  that  the  Epistle  "To  my  Lady,"  and  the  mendacious  advertisement  to  the 
reader  occupy  three  leaves,  which  are  inserted  after  the  title,  and  the  speeches  con- 
tained in  the  first  issue  are  here  omitted,  while  two  leaves  of  poems  and  two  leaves  of 
Table  are  added  at  the  end.  It  appears  from  a  contemporary  folio  MS.  of  Waller's 
Poems  with  corrections  in  his  autograph,  now  in  the  possession  of  Mr.  Waller,  of 
Beaconsfield,  that  the  Epistle  addressed  here  and  in  the  other  printed  editions 
simply  "To  my  Lady,"  and  applying  not  to  the  volume,  in  which  it  is  inserted, but  to 
a  MS.  copy  sent  to  the  patroness,  was  designed  for  Lady  Sophia  Bertie,  for  in  the 
MS.  the  superscription  is:  "To  my  Lady  Sophia  Bartee,"  to  which  is  added  in  a 
contemporary  hand,  "  the  Earl  of  Linsey's  daughter."  The  Lady  Isabella  mentioned 
by  Waller  was  Lady  Isabella  Thynne.  But  the  MS.  described  has  a  still  higher 
interest  as  possessing  an  inedited  dedication,  which  the  poet  intended  to  have 
addressed  to  Queen  Henrietta  Maria,  but  which  was  suppressed  in  consequence 
of  the  Civil  War.     See  "  Notes  and  Queries,"  3rd  .S.  vii.,  435,  and  ix.,  193.

Printed  by  L  N.  for  Hu.  Mosley.  .  .  .   1645.    Sm.  8vo.    R.  M. 

A,  4  leaves  ;  B — N  4  in  eights  ;  O — P  2  in  eights. 

This  is  the  first  genuine  edition  with  much  more  correct  text.  The  speeches 
now  appear  again.     The  advertisement  regarding  the  spurious  copy  is  repeated. 

The  present  copy,  from  the  libraries  of  Mr.  Jolley  and  Mr.  J.  Delaware  Lewis,  has 
been  called  "  large  paper,"  but  this  seems  to  be  doubtful.  The  volume  is  printed 
in  a  larger  and  handsomer  type  than  the  earlier  issue,  and  instead  of  fifty-four 
occupies  ninety-six  leaves,  exclusively  of  the  Speeches.

The  Stationer,  in  his  Address  to  the  Reader,  says  that  this  letter  was  intended

lary," folio,  1674 — but  that  some  few  copies  had  crept  abroad,  he  knew  not  how, 
by  themselves.  He  therefore  reprinted  the  Prefatory  Letter  separately  in  8vo.  to 
supply  the  demand  for  it,  requiring  all  the  folio  stock  to  bind  up  with  the  History.

WARD,  Samuel.  Deo  trin-vni  Britanniae  bis  ultoro.  In  memo- 
riam  Classis  invincibilis  subversae  submers^u  Proditionis  ncfandae 
detectse  dissectae.  To  God,  In  memorye  of  his  double  deliverance 
from  the  invincible  Navie  and  the  unmatcheable  powder  Treason. 
1588.  1605.  Invented  by  Samuell  Ward  preacher  of  Ipswich.  Im- 
printed at  Amsterdam  Anno  \6z\.    A  large  Broadside. 

In  the  centre  is  an  engraving  of  the  Pope  and  his  adherents  in  council,  with  a 
view  of  the  Parliament  house  on  the  right-hand  side,  and  Faux  advancing  with  his 
lantern  towards  the  cellar,  while  on  the  left  hand  we  see  the  Spanish  Armada 
drawn  up  in  the  form  of  a  horse-shoe.  At  the  foot  are  three  sets  of  verses,  a  Latin 
hexameter  and  pentameter,  and  a  quatrain  in  English,  each  descriptive  of  a  portion 
of  the  plate.  Above,  in  the  clouds,  are  cherubim  shedding  rays  of  light  on  the 
deeds  of  darkness  projected  against  England  by  her  Romish  enemies.

In  the  Grenville  Catalogue  it  is  stated  that  only  six  copies  were  printed  on  large 
paper,  but  that  is  certainly  an  error,  as  is  also  the  opinion  expressed  that  the  plates 
of  Costume  are  always  on  small  paper.  They  are  here  the  same  size  as  the  book 
with  one  exception.

A  work  of  prose  fiction,  of  which  there  is  an  earlier  and  less  correct  impression, 
attributed  to  15S5,  the  book  having  been  licensed  in  1584.  The  first  edition  was 
dedicated  to  Sir  George  Casey,  Lord  Hunsdon,  and  the  author  here  retains  the 
dedication,  or,  as  he  expresses  it,  "most  humbly  continueth  the  recommended 
patronage  of  his  reformed  Syrinx."   Both  editions  are  of  the  greatest  rarity,  the  only 


WARNING— WARREN.  1565 

other  copy  known  of  this,  being  that  which  was  formerly  in  the  Roxburghe  and 
White  Knight  collections,  and  is  now  in  the  British  Museum. 

Warner's  "Syrinx"  is  described  by  Warton  as  "a  novel,  or  rather  a  suite  of 
stories,  much  in  the  style  of  the  adventures  in  Heliodorus's  Ethiopia  Romance."

It  has  been  strenuously  asserted  by  some  writers  th.it  Shakespeare  must  have 
had  some  hand  in  the  composition  of  this  remarkable  oldplay,  but  the  proposition  has 
been  as  strenuously  denied  by  others.  The  style  of  certain  passages  is  of  course  all 
that  can  be  adduced  in  favour  of  the  idea.

representative.

only  other  copy  known  is  that  at  Britwell,  formerly  Mr.  Heber's.

Knights,  and  Pen7  collections."— ^/i".  Notf  by  Mr.  jolley  (Catalogue,  part  4,  p.  106). 

The  author  in  "  The  ProKme  "  speaks  of  this  as  his  "  first  effort,"  but  in  Arber's 
"Transcript,"  v.  II.  153b,  Nov.  7,  1578,  we  find  licensed  to  Richard  Jones,"  A  pithie 
and  pleausaunt  discourse  Dialoguewyse  betwene  a  welthie  citizen  and  a  miserable 
souldiour  briefelye  touchinge  the  commodyties  and  discommodyties  both  of  warre 
and  peace  by  W.  Warren."  No  copy  of  this  book  is  known  to  exist.  If  it  were  in 
prose,  it  is  probable  that  the  author  speaks  of  "The  Nurserie  of  Names"  as  his 
"  first  effort "  in  verse.

to  Walter  Hammond,  24th  March,  1631-2.

WATT,  Robert.  Bibliotheca  Critannica.  Edinburgh  .  .  .  1824.

[WATTON,  John.]    [Speculum  Christiani.]  Hie  incipit  liber  de 

speculo  xani Prohemium.  IVIagna  differecia  e  int^  |)dicac6m  & 

doctrinam MS.  of  the  fifteenth  century  on  vellum.    4to. 

This  work  is  attributed  to  John  Watton,  an  English  priest,  who  lived  in  the 
latter  part  of  the  fifteenth  century. 

The  body  of  the  book  is  in  Latin,  and  begins  with  a  statement  concerning  the 
duty  of  teaching  religious  truths,  citing  the  Lambeth  Constitution  of  Archbishop 
Peckham  in  1281,  by  which  priests  were  ordered  to  expound  at  certain  intervals  the 
articles  of  faith,  the  precepts  of  charity,  the  seven  works  of  piety,  the  seven  deadly 
sins,  &c.  In  the  course  of  his  work  the  author  gives  a  rhyming  version  of  the 
Decalogue,  verses  in  which  the  seven  deadly  sins  express  themselves,  a  kind  of 
sermon  on  these  sins  in  prose,  followed  by  various  other  metrical  counsels,  and  a 
hymn  to  the  Virgin,  all  in  English.  In  this  hymn  a  couplet  is  found  in  the  MS.,  one 
half  only  of  which  appears  in  the  printed  book.  After  the  words,  "  Crete  the  with 
Avys  five,"  near  the  end,  the  MS.  proceeds,  "  A  paternoster  and  a  crede,"  which  is 
necessary  to  rhyme  with  "  To  helpe  me  lady  at  my  nede."  On  the  other  hand  this 
MS.  omits  the  last  couplet,  which  is  found  in  Machlinia's  edition. 

Occasionally  the  verses  differ  materially  from  the  printed  copy,  as  the  following: — 
MS.  Machlinia. 

I  chyde  and  fyght  and  malas  faste  I  chide  I  flite  I  manace  faste 

All  my  foe  men  I  will  downe  caste  Alle  my  fo  men  I  doun  caste 

Mercy  on  hem  I  will  non  haue  Mercy  on  hem  wille  I  not  haue 

But  veniance  strong  so  god  me  saue  But  vengeaunce  strong  so  wood  I  raue

Esq.,  and  has  commendatory  verses  by  J.  C,  S.  N.,and  D.  S.  The  second  part  is

years  before,  that  he  had  not  designed  it  for  publication,  but  that  he  was  forced

Two  New  Playes :  viz.    A  Cure  for  a  Cuckold  :  A  Comedy. 

The  Thracian  Wonder:  A  Comical  Historj'.  As  it  hath  been  several 
times  Acted  with  great  Applause.  Written  by  John  Webster  and 
William  Rowley.  Placerc  Cupio.  London  :  Printed  by  Tho.  Johnson, 
and  are  to  be  sold  by  Francis  Kirkman  .  .  .   1661.    4to. 

A,  2  leaves  ;  B — H  in  fours,  for  each  play,  besides  the  general  title. 

In  the  Address  to  the  Reader  before  the  first  play,  Kirkman  mentions  that 
this  was  the  earliest  of  a  series  of  three  plays,  which  he  intended  to  produce  in 
the  course  of  the  term,  the  second  being  the  other  drama  in  the  volume,  and 
the  third,  "  Gammer  Gurton's  Needle."  He  says  "It  was  not  long  since  I  was  only 
a  Book-Reader,  and  not  a  bookseller,  which  Quality  ...  I  have  now  lately  taken 
on  me."  He  further  says  that  he  had  been  a  collector  of  old  plays  for  some 
years,  and  that  he  had  some  700  by  him  ;  and  in  the  preface  to  the  "  Thracian 
Wonder"  he  informs  us  that  he  had  several  MS.  plays  by  worthy  authors  in  his 
possession. 

First  editions  of  both  pieces.  No  other  copy  with  a  general  title-page  is  noticed. 
The  authorship  of  Webster  and  Rowley  has  been  questioned. 

WEBSTER,  John.  The  Displaying  of  supposed  Witchcraft. 
Wherein  is  affirmed  that  there  are  many  sorts  of  Deceivers  and  Im- 
postors. And  Divers  persons  under  a  passive  Delusion  of  Melancholy 
and  Fancy.  But  that  there  is  a  Corporeal  League  made  betwixt  the 
Devil  and  the  Witch,  Or  that  he  sucks  on  the  Witches  body,  has 
Carnal  Copulation,  or  that  Witches  are  turned  into  Cats,  Dogs,  raise 
Tempests,  or  the  like,  is  utterly  denied  and  disproved London,

1 574  WEERDT—  WEE  VER. 

"  For  originality  of  ideas  and  construction  in  part  writing  .  .  .  Weelkes  in  my 
opinion  leaves  all  other  composers  of  his  time  far  behind,"  says  Mr.  Oliphant  in 
the  "  Musa  Madrigalesca  ;"  and,  especially  in  relation  to  the  "Ayeres  ;"  he  further 
states,  "  Some  of  them  I  really  think  are  the  cleverest  specimens  of  three-part 
writing  that  are  to  be  met  with." 

The  twenty-sixth  piece  in  the  collection  is  a  Song  for  six  voices,  "  A  remem- 
brance of  my  friend  M.  Thomas  Morley." 

There  is  in  a  satirical  piece  (No.  19)  a  curious  allusion  to  hackney  coaches, 
which,  as  it  is  known,  had  then  been  some  time  already  in  use  :^ 
"  —  for  he  that  doth  the  Cochman  prayse, 
May  safely  use  the  Coch." 
There  is  also  a  song  in  praise  of  Tobacco.

"  Mdm.,"  p.  102,  &c.,  and  MuUer,  p.  21,  where  a  full  collation  is  given.  After  D  2 
is  another  title-page,  for  the  treatise  of  Jan  Outghersz,  as  described  by  Muller,  who

This  copy  is  in  beautiful  condition  and  entirely  uncut.  It  is  the  identical  one

WEEVER,  John.  The  Mirror  of  Martyrs,  or.  The  life  and  death 
of  that  thrice  valiant  Capitaine  and  most  godly  Martyre  Sir  lohn  Old- 
castle  knight  Lord  Cobhani.  Printed  by  V.  S.  for  William  Wood. 
1 60 1.    Sm.  sqr.  8vo.     The  edges  uncut.    O.  M. 

A — F  3  in  eights.     In  verse. 

Weever,  whose  name  is  better  known  as  the  author  of  the  "  Funeral  Monu- 
ments "  than  by  his  poetical  pieces,  says  in  his  dedication  to  his  friend,  William 
Covell,  that  this  work  had  lain  two  years  in  the  corner  of  his  study,  where  he 
kept  his  waste  paper.  There  is  a  second  inscription  (in  verse)  "  To  the  Author's 
most  honored  friend  Richard  Ualton  of  Pilling."  The  address  to  Dalton  is,  like 
the  poem  itself,  in  6-line  stanzas. 

The  present  copy  with  uncut  leaves  was  obtained  at  the  sale  of  the  Wolferston 
collection.  Copies  are  in  the  Bodleian  and  Pcpysian  libraries.  A  fourth,  wanting 
the  title,  was  sold  among  Mr.  Caldecott's  books  in  1833.     No  others  are  known. 


WEEVER—WEIGELIUS.  1575 

WEEVER,  John.  An  Agnvs  Dei.  Printed  by  V.  S.  for  Nicholas 
Lyng.     1606.    A  thumb-book,  measuring  about  i|  in.  in  height. 

This  diminutive  poeticil  volume  contains  a  history  of  Christ  from  the  birth  of 
the  Virgin.     It  collates  in  eights,  A — Q.     The  present  is  the  only  copy  known.     It 
is  in  the  original  vellum  binding  with  a  ribbon  attached,  as  first  published.     The 
dedication  runs  :  "  To  Prince  Henry.     Your  humble  servant.    lo  :  Weever." 
It  shows  that  the  poem  had  been  written  some  years  before  it  was  printed. 
"  Thou  machlesse  issue  of  a  mighty  King, 
To  whose  green  years  &  iudgemct  grave,  I  bring 
These  holy  numbers  of  my  heav'nly  muse, 
Which  my  late  Empresse  daigned  to  peruse  : 
The  like  acceptance  humblie  I  intreate. 
My  Booke  is  litle,  but  my  love  is  great." 

'■  lo:  Weever!' 
On  the  flyleaf  is  the  autograph  of  "Elizabeth  Baynton.  1622."

Folios  i.-cxxxvii.,  including  title,  followed  by  ten  un-numbered  leaves. 

There  are  fifteen  woodcuts  in  this  volume,  including  that  on  the  title.  On  the 
last  appears  the  monogram  H.  S.  as  figured  by  Nagler,  Monogrammisten,  No.  1449. 
This  book  appears  to  have  been  unknown  to  him,  though  he  quotes  two  other  works 
printed  at  Leipsic  about  the  same  period,  with  woodcuts  in  which  the  monogram 
occurs.  He  goes  carefully  through  the  probable  claimants  to  the  monogram,  but 
is  only  able  to  come  to  the  conclusion  that  the  name  of  the  artist  is  unknown.

WENLOCK,  John.  To  the  most  Illustrious,  High  and  Mighty

Wenlock  of  Langham,  in  the  County  of  Essex    Esquire,   an  Vtter 


1578  WENT  WORTH— WEST. 

Barrister  of  near  Forty  years  continuance,  in  that  Honourable  Society 
of  Lincolns-Inne.  Being  first  a  Supplicatory  Preface  and  Discourse  to 
his  Majesty  and  then  humbly  shewing  the  great  and  dangerous 
Troubles  and  intollerable  Oppressions  of  Himself  and  His  Family,  and 
the  true  occasion  thereof,  in  the  wofull  Times  of  these  late  most  un- 
happy Distractions Hereunto  are  annexed  certain  Poems,  and 

other  Treatises,  composed  and  written  by  the  Author  upon  several 
Occasions,  concerning  the  late  most  horrid  and  distracted  Times,  and 
never  before  published.  [Quotations.]  Loudon,  Printed  by  T.  Childe 
and  L.  Parry,  for  the  Ant/ior.  .  .  .   1662.  4to. 

A — V  in  fours,  besides  the  title-page  and  a  leaf  before  it  with  verses.  Dedicated 
to  Charles  II. 

In  the  leaf  which  precedes  the  title,  Wenlock  says  that  he  had  intended  to 
prefix  his  portrait  and  coat  of  arms,  but  that  the  expense  prevented  him  from  doing 
so.  He  prints,  however,  the  verses  which  he  had  purposed  to  place  beneath  the 
engraving.

WHETSTONE,  George.  An  Heptameron  of  Ciuill  Discourses. 
Containing  :  The  Christmasse  Exercise  of  sundrie  well  Courted  Gentle- 
men and  Gentlewomen.  In  whose  bchauiours,  the  better  sort,  may  see, 
a  represetation  of  their  own  Vertues :  And  the  Inferiour,  may  learne 
such  Rules  of  Ciuil  Gouernmet,  as  wil  rase  out  the  Blemish  of  their 


1584  WHETSTONE. 

basenesse  :  Wherin  is  Renowned,  the  Vertues,  of  a  most  Honourable 
and  braue  mynded  Gentleman.  And  herein,  also,  [as  it  were  in  a 
Mirrour]  the  Vnmaried  may  see  the  Defectes  whiche  Eclipse  the

diousnesse  from  the  Reader  :  and  garnished  with  Morall  Noates  to 
make  it  profitable,  to  the  Regarder.  The  Reporte,  of  George  Whet- 
stone. Gent.  Formce,  nulla  fides.  At  London.  Printed  by  Richard 
lones,  at  the  Si^/ie  of  the  Rose  and  the  Crowne,  neare  Holburne  Bridge,

A,  4  leaves  ;  IT,  2  leaves  ;  B — Z  in  fours.     In  prose  and  verse. 

Dedicated  to  Sir  Christopher  Hatton.  This  work  was  reissued  in  1593  under  a 
different  title  :  "  Aurelia.     The  Paragon  of  Pleasure,"  &c. 

From  the  collections  of  Mr.  J.  Dunn  Gardner  and  Sir  W.  Tite. 

"We  are  sent  to  Cinthio  for  the  Plot  of '  Measure  for  Measure,'  and  Shake- 
speare's judgement  hath  been  attacked  for  some  deviations  from  him  in  the  conduct 
of  it:  when  probably  all  he  knew  of  the  matter  was  from  Madam  Isabella  in  the 
'  Heptameron '  of  Whetstone." — Farmer  on  Shakespeare. 

Mr.  Hazlitt  states  that  there  was  another  issue  of  this  work,  also  dated  Feb.  3rd, 
1582,  "with  a  very  different  title,"  but  the  difference  appears  to  be  made  only  by 
omitting  a  portion  of  the  text  of  the  title  in  each  of  the  versions  he  gives  of  it. 

WHETSTONE,  George.  The  Honorable  Repvtation  of  a  Soul- 
dier.  With  a  Morall  Report,  of  the  Vertues,  Offices,  and  (by  abuse) 
the  Disgrace  of  his  profession.  Drawne  out  of  the  lines,  documents, 
and  disciplines,  of  the  most  renowned  Romaine,  Grecian,  and  other 
famous  Martialistes.  By  George  Whetstone,  Gent.  Alalgre  de  Fortune.' 
Imprinted  at  London,  by  Richard  lones  :  divelling  neere  vnto  Holburne 
Bridge.   1585.    4to.    Black  letter. 

A — F  2  in  fours.  Dedicated  "  To  the  most  worthy  Gent.  Sir  William  Russell, 
Knight."  The  Epistle  dedicatorie,  which  is  in  prose,  is  followed  by  five  stanzas  of 
six  lines  directed  "  To  the  Right  Valiant  Gentlemen  &  Souldiers,  that  are,  or  shall 
be  Anned  vnder  the  Ensigne  of  Sainct  George." 

We  learn  from  the  Epistle,  that  "the  English  Mirror,"  not  printed  till  1586, 
was  already  written,  "But  because,  the  booke  is  of  some  volume,"  says  Whetstone, 
"  I  cannot  haue  it  speedily  printed  :  and  for  that  occasio  now  (at  this  present) 
maketh  passage  for  this  Subiect,  being  a  member  or  small  parcell  of  the  fore- 
recited  booke."  He  tells  us,  a  little  farther  on,  a  story  w-hich  was  intended  by 
him  to  illustrate  the  arrogance  of  the  Spaniards  at  that  time — eight  years  before 
the  Armada.  He  says  that  being  in  the  company  of  a  Gentleman  of  Picardy,  a 
Monsieur  Dobart,  in  a  garrison  town  in  the  Duchy  of  Milan,  with  another  English- 
man, in  the  year  1580,  a  Spaniard  came  in  when  Dobart  asked  him  what  might 
be  the  cause  that  the  King  of  Spain  had  raised  so  great  an  army.  "Ah  Sir," 
returned  the  other,  "  the  time  nowe  draweth  neare,  that  we  shall  ha\e  the  spoile  of 
rich  England,  that  we  shall  embrace  their  fairc  wiues,  and  make  hauocke  of  their 
log  gathered  riches."  The  story  goes  on  to  relate  how  the  Spaniard,  being  challenged 
to  fight,  disappeared  before  morning. 


WHETSTONE.  1585

presenting the  Ordinaiinces,  Policies,  and  Diligence,  of  the  Noble  Em- 
peroLir,  Alexander  (surnamcd  Severvs)  to  suppresse  and  chastise  the 
notorious  Vices  nourished  in  Rome,  by  the  superfluous  number  of 
Dicing-houses,  Tauerns  and  Common  Stewes,  Suffred  and  cheerished 
by  his  bcastlye  Predecessour,  Hclyogablvs.  With  sundrie  graue  Ora- 
tions, by  the  said  noble  Emperor,  concerning  Reformation.  And 
herevnto  is  added,  A  Touchstone  for  the  Time  Containyng  :  many 
pcrillous  Mischiefes,  bred  in  the  Bowels  of  the  Citie  of  London  :  By 
the  Infection  of  some  of  thease  Sanctuaries  of  Iniquitie.  By  George

by  Mr.  Collier  in  his  "  Bibliographical  Catalogue."

A — G  2  in  fours. 

A  reissue  in  Februar>',  15S6-7,  with  the  title  and  last  leaf  reprinted.  The  two 
issues  agree  so  far  as  the  middle  of  G  2  recto  ;  but  then,  in  the  place  of  a  paragraph 
which  refers  to  Mary  Queen  of  Scots  as  still  living,  another  is  inserted,  mentioning 
her  execution,  which  had  taken  place  in  the  meantime.  This  piece  was  edited,  during 
the  absence  of  Whetstone  in  the  country,  by  his  friend  Thomas  Churchyard,  who 
signs  with  his  initials  a  short  address  to  the  reader  on  the  back  of  the  title. 

See  Hunter's  "  New  Illustrations  of  Shakespeare,"  1845,  i.,  222,  Note.

WHITNEY,  Geffrey.  A  ChoiceofEmblemes,  and  other  Devises. 
For  the  moste  parte  gathered  out  of  sundrie  writers,  Englished  and 
Moralized.  And  Divers  newly  devised.  By  Geffrey  Whitney.  A 
worke  adorned  with  varietie  of  matter,  both  pleasant  and  profitable : 
wherein  those  that  please,  maye  finde  to  fit  their  fancies :  Bicause 
herein,  by  the  office  of  the  eie,  and  the  eare,  the  minde  may  reape 
dooble  delighte  throughe  holsome  preceptes,  shadowed  with  pleasant 
deuises :  both  fit  for  the  vertuous,  to  their  incoraging :  and  for  the 
wicked,  for  their  admonishing  and  amendment.  To  the  Reader. 
Peruse  with  heede,  then  frendlie  iudge,  and  blaming  rash  refraine :  So 
maist  thou  reade  vnto  thy  good,  and  shalt  requite  my  paine.  Imprinted 
at  Ley  den,  In  the  house  of  Christopher  Plantyn,  by  Francis  Raphclengius.

Dedicated  "To  My  Honoured  Friend  John  Hyde  Esq." 

It  appears  from  the  preface  that  Whitney  selected  Hyde  as  the  patron  of  his 

9G 


1 590  WHOL  E—  WICKL I  FEE. 

book  on  account  of  the  kindness  of  the  latter  in  allowing  him  to  fish  at  Winckhurst. 
He  relates  that  while  he  was  there,  in  company  with  an  old  gentleman,  a  Captain 
Comer,  who  was  enjoying  the  sport,  his  companion  had  left  his  rod  for  a  moment, 
when  a  fish  came  and  took  away  rod  and  all.  But  Comer  stripped,  jumped  in,  and 
brought  out  both  rod  and  fish,  "  with  much  content  to  regain  his  rod."

WIDOW  OF  WATLING-STREET.  The  first  part  of  the  faire

The  author  was  minister  of  Maldon,  Massachusetts  (1631-1705).  It  was  pro- 
bably first  printed  at  Boston,  though  no  copy  of  the  original  edition  is  extant.  The 
first  London  edition  is  1666.     The  poem  was  frequently  reprinted  in  New  England.

WILLIAMS.  1595 

A,  2  leaves  ;  n — I  in  fours  ;  "  The  Discovery  of  Silkworms,  &c. ,"  with  a  new 
title  ;  A— L  in  fours,  first  and  last  leaves  blank,  with  woodcuts. 

This  is  a  second  issue  of  the  "  Virgo  Triuinphans,"  omitting  the  addresses  of 
Williams  "To  the  Conservers  and  Enlargers  of  the  Liberties  of  the  Nation,  &c." 
and  to  the  Reader,  with  the  Errata ;  and  adding  what  was  not  in  the  former  issue, 
an  address  "  To  the  worthy  Gentlemen,  Adventurers,  and  Planters  in  Virginia," 
subscribed  with  Williams's  initials. 

In  the  next  year  a  third  title  was  given  to  the  book  as  follows  :  "Virginia  in 
America,  Richly  Valued  :  More  especially  the  Southerne  Parts.  With  the  Tendure 
of  the  Vine,  and  Silke-worme,  making  Wines  and  Silks  there  :  and  framing  of 
Saw-mils,  to  cut  Timber  for  Building.  Shewing  also  divers  other  meanes  of 
raysing  infinite  profits  to  Adventurers  and  Planters.  Together  with  A  compleat  Map 
of  the  Country,  from  35.  to  41.  Degrees  of  Latitude  discovered,  and  the  West  Sea. 
London,  Printed  for  John  Stephenson  .  .  .  M.DC.LI." 

This  copy  has  the  title  to  the  issue  of  165 1  inserted,  and  the  map  by  Goddard 
in  two  states ;  i.  On  thin  paper,  without  the  oval  portrait  of  Sir  Francis  Drake, 
and  without  some  of  the  names  of  places  which  are  added  in  the  other.     The 

imprint  is  :  John  Farrer  Esq.  Collegit.    Are  sold  by  J.  Stephenson 1651. 

2.  On  thicker  paper,  with  the  portrait  of  Drake  at  the  top  towards  the  left  hand 
corner,  many  names  of  places  filled  in,  and  the  imprint  varied,  Domina  Virginia 
Farrer  Collegit,  &c.

With  a  preface  by  Sir  John  Hayward.  Sir  Peter  Manwoode,  in  the  dedication  to

long  time  by  him  in  MS.  The  author  himself  had  published  in  his  lifetime  "  A

WILSON,  George.  The  Commendation  of  Cockes,  and  Cock- 
fighting.  Wherein  is  shewed,  that  Cocke-fighting  was  before  the 
comming  of  Christ.  London,  Printed  for  Henrie  Tomes,  and  are  to  be 
sold  at  his  Shop  oner  against  Graies  Inne  gate  in  Holburne,  1607.  4to. 
Black  Letter.    O.  M. 

A — D  in  fours,  title  on  A  2. 

Dedicated  to  Sir  Henry  Bedingfield,  in  the  county  of  Norfolk,  Knight,  in  an 
epistle  in  which  Wilson  states  that  this  treatise  had  been  long  since  promised  by 
him  to  friends  in  London,  Boston,  and  elsewhere.  It  appears  that  Sir  Henry  was 
a  fellow-lover  of  the  sport.  On  the  title  is  a  cut  of  two  cocks  preparing  to  close. 
Mr.  Collier  ("  Bibl.  Cat.,"  ii.,  528)  states  that  "  Wilson's  tract  was  several  times 
reprinted."   According  to  Lowndes  the  latest  edition  was  in  1655. 

WILSON,  John.  Cheerfull  Ayres  or  Ballads  First  composed  for 
one  single  Voice  and  since  set  for  three  Voices  by  John  Wilson 
Dr.  in  Mvsick  Professor  of  the  same  in  the  Vniversity  of  Oxford.  Ox- 
ford. Printed  by  W.  Hall,  for  Ric.  Davis.  Anno  Dom.  M  DC  LX. 
Oblong  4to. 

A — U  1  in  fours.     The  Cantus  Primus. 

It  is  stated  in  the  Preface  that,  this  being  the  first  essay  in  the  way  of  printing 
music  at  Oxford,  certain  mistakes  (for  which  there  is  a  table  of  errata)  had  been 
committed  by  the  printer,  chiefly  in  "omission  of  Moods."  It  also  informs  us 
that  this  part  was  intended  to  form  "  a  compleate  Book  of  it  selfe,  carrying  the 
principall  Ayre  to  Sing  alone  with  a  through  Base."  The  two  other  parts  were 
Cantus  Secundus  and  Bassus.  Some  of  the  music  had  been  composed  by  others, 
but  the  whole  is  here  reset  by  Wilson.

the  Holy  Scriptures  in  English  In  the  collection  of  Lea  Wilson 
Esq^    London  MDCCCXLV.     4to.

WILSON— WINCHESTER.  1599 

A  passage  quoted  by  Wilson  from  Udall's  "Ralph  Roister  Doistcr"  is  the 
earliest  indication  of  the  existence  of  that  drama,  so  nearly  lost  to  us,  and  fixes  ap- 
proximately the  date  of  its  composition,  if  not  the  original  performance  of  it,  as 
anterior  to  1551— at  least  fifteen  years  before  it  was  entered  at  Stationers'  Hall.

were  better  be  a  Carter,  then  a  Scholar,  for  worldly  profite."

WINGAR,  Henry.  Light  Extinguish'd.  A  Poem  Intitul'd  the 
Sight's  Retreat :  or.  The  True  Light  at  last  made  Manifest.  By 
H.W.Gent.    Printed  for  tJie  AutJwr.    \Abo\t  \6%o?}^    8vo. 

A — C  in  fours. 

This  copy  is  dedicated  to  the  Countess  of  Dalkeith,  but  the  volume  has  the 
appearance  of  having  been  printed  with  the  name  of  the  patron  or  patroness  left 
blank,  with  a  view  to  subsequent  insertion,  as  it  is  in  a  different  type  and  ink.  Such 
was  the  case  with  Anderson's  "  Court  Convert"  and  some  of  Jordan's  publications. 
Wingar  speaks  of  his  work  as  the  "offspring  of  an  obscure  Muse,"  and  of  himself 
as  "altogether  benighted,  and  his  Candle  extinguish'd";  and  he  humbly  takes 
leave,  upon  a  just  consideration  of  Lady  Dalkeith's  character,  to  light  his  candle 
again  at  her  illuminating  altar. 

A  copy  is  in  the  British  Musum  ;  but  no  other,  besides  the  present,  has  been 
noticed.

WIRRE—WISE.  1601 

offnen  vnd  burgerlichc  helm  vnd  schiltcn,  auch  abbildiig  viler  vilcr-

in  druck  verfcrtigt.  Wie  sie  erstlich  vom  Hcinrich  Wirings  in  kupfifcr 
gestochc  angcfangen,  vnd  volgentz  vom  Joh:  Hogenb.  volledct  wordcn 
sint.  Zii  Colle  trtickts  Joh.  Bussemechcr.  10.  y.]  Sm.  oblong  8vo. 
BR.  M. 

The  above  title  is  engraved  on  copper  within  an  ornamental  border.  It  is  followed 
by  a  second  title  in  Latin,  also  within  an  engraved  border,  which  reads, "  Monumenta 
Amicorum  Libellus  elegans,  omnium  statuum  galeas  et  clypeos  varios  pra;figurans, 
quos  pro  sua  quisque  conditione  adimplere  deinceps  paruo  labore  valebit  :  inteiectis 
sparsim  facetis  nonnullis  et  elegantibus  diuersoru  habituum  iconibus.  Nunc  primum 
in  gratiam  generosK  nobilitatis  ac  studiosx  Juuentutis  in  lucem  editus  sculptoris 
Hen.  Wi.  et  Jo.  Hogenbergio.  Impressore  yoaii  Bitsscmachcro."  \s.  ai\  The  work 
consists  in  all  of  forty-five  plates  besides  the  two  engraved  titles.  Seventeen  of  these 
plates  represent  various  subjects,  mostly  satirical  or  humorous  in  their  character,  and 
twenty-eight  are  coats-of-arms  with  scroll-work  round  them,  and  some  of  them  with 
full-length  figures  for  supporters.  There  are  no  numbers  on  any  of  the  plates.  No 
bibliographer  appears  to  have  known  the  book,  and  the  artist  is  mentioned  only  by 
Nagler  as  the  engraver  of  a  single  plate,  of  which  he  says,  •'  Dieses  blatt  ist  sicher 
von  Hieronymus  Wierx,  oder  Copie  nach  demselben  mit  falschem  Namen."  The 
only  other  copy  known  of  the  volume  is  in  the  South  Kensington  Museum,  but  that 
contains  only  twenty-four  plates,  and  has  only  the  German  title.
