This  is  not  a  reprint  of  any  single  work  of  earlier  date,  but  appears  to  be  made 
up  from  two  or  three  sources.     It  contains  a  portion  of  "  Wit  Restor'd,"  1658. 

The  present  copy,  in  the  original  binding,  with  the  Sheldon  book-plate  and  the 
crest  stamped  on  each  side  of  the  cover,  has  Ritson's  autograph,  and  also  that 
of  Mr.  E.  V.  Utterson.  It  once  belonged  to  Mr.  Allen,  whose  curious  Ubrary  was 
dispersed  in  1799.

diah  Blagrave,  at  the  Bear  in  St.  Pauls  Chiirch-Yard,  1682.   8vo.

all  the  same.

A,  4  leaves  ;  B — Mm  4  in  eights,  Mm  4  having  the  errata. 

Following  the  title  is  an  epistle  or  address,  "  To  my  most  esteemed  and  ap- 
proued  louing  friend,  Maister  I.  B.  I  wish  all  happiness,"  from  which  it  appears 
that  the  writer,  [Nicholas  Ling  the  Stationer,]  was  encouraged  by  John  Boden- 
ham  to  undertake  this  volume  of  extracts  and  precepts  as  a  sort  of  sequel  to 
"  Wit's  Commonwealth."  Herbert  speaks  of  the  book  as  having  been  originally 
published  in  1598  ;  but  he  does  not  appear  to  have  seen  any  copy  of  it  himself 
with  that  date,  and  it  is  not  described  by  any  other  bibliographer.  Neither  on  the 
title  nor  in  the  introductory  matter  is  there  any  indication  of  an  earher  issue  than 
this  of  1599.

WITCHES.  The  Lawes  against  Witches  and  Conivration.  And 
Some  brief  Notes  and  Observations  for  the  Discovery  of  Witches.

WITCHES.  A  True  and  Impartial  Relation  of  the  Informations 
against  Three  Witches,  viz.  Temperance  Lloyd,  Mary  Trembles,  and 
Susanna  Edwards.  Who  were  Indicted,  Arraigned,  and  Convicted  at 
the  Assizes  holden  for  the  County  of  Devon  at  the  Castle  of  Exon, 
Aug.  14,  1682.  With  their  several  Confessions,  taken  before  Thomas 
Gift  Mayor  and  John  Davie  Alderman  of  Biddiford  in  the  said  County, 
where  they  were  Inhabitants.  As  also  their  Speeches,  Confessions, 
and  Behaviour  at  the  time  and  place  of  Execution  on  the  Twenty  fifth 
of  the  said  Month.  London  :  Printed  by  Freeman  Collins,  and  are  to 
be  sold  by  T.  Benskin  in  St.  Brides  Church-yard,  and  C.  Yea  Bookseller  itt 
Exon.    1682.    4to. 

A,  2  leaves  ;  B — F  in  fours. 

The  Preface  to  the  Reader  is  very  curious.  It  speaks  of  the  account  as  written 
by  "  a  faithful  and  able  Hand,"  but  the  name  of  the  author  is  not  given.  It  seems 
to  have  been  considered  that  the  narrative  would  prove  of  additional  interest  from 
the  fact  that  "we  have  had  no  Conviction  or  Execution  of  any  Witches  for  many 
years  past."  Those  who  do  not  believe  in  witches  are  invited  to  consult  King 
Alfred,  Holy  Writ,  King  James's  "  Demonology,"  and  "  Sadducismus  Trium- 
phatus." 

The  Tryal,  Condemnation,  and  Execution  of  Three  Witches, 

viz.  Temperance  Floyd,  Mary  Floyd,  and  Svsanna  Edwards.  Who 
were  Arraigned  at  Exeter  on  the  i8th  of  August,   1682.    And  being 

prov'd  Guilty  of  Witch-Craft,  were  Condem'd  to  be  Hang'd 

Also,  how  they  squeezed  one  Hannah  Thomas  to  death  in  their  Arms; 
How  they  also  caused  several  ships  to  be  cast  away  .  .  .  With  many 
Wonderful  Things,  worth  your  Reading.  Printed  for  J.  Deacon,  at  the 
sign  of  the  Rainbow,  a  little  beyond  St.  Andrews  Church,  in  Holborn.

The  only  copy  known.  See  the  privately  printed  "Book  of  Prefaces,"  1S74, 
p.  13,  where  the  Prologue  is  given  entire.  It  is  addressed  to  .Sir  Thomas  Chaloner, 
and  it  is  quite  clear  from  the  terms  of  it,  that  the  book  was  then  printed  for  the  first 

9  I 


i6o6  WITHER. 

time,  and  that  the  editions  spoken  of  by  Ames  and  Herbert  as  printed  by  Caxton 
and  Wynkyn  de  Worde,  never  existed.  Till  this  copy  was  discovered  in  1870,  the 
edition  was  altogether  uncertain,  though  it  is  doubtfully  mentioned  by  Herbert  at 
p.  460  ;  and  at  p.  465  he  speaks  of  it  as  printed  by  Berthelet  in  1559,  and  says  that 
a  copy  was  in  Dr.  Lort's  library.

From  the  collection  of  Mr.  J.  D.  Lewis.  The  copy  was  bound  from  the  original 
sheep  cover,  and  has  many  uncut  leaves. 

It  is  said  that  as  many  as  seven  editions  of  this  work,  for  which  the  author  was 
committed  to  the  Marshalsea  prison,  were  issued  in  1613. 

It  has  been  thought  that  there  may  have  been  an  edition  of  these  Essays  and 
Satires  prior  to  1613,  and  one  of  161 1  has  been  hypothetically  cited.  The  book  was 
not,  however,  registered  at  Stationers'  Hall  till  i6th  January-,  161 3-14.

WITHER.  1607 

which  we  learn  that  the  title  of  the  volume  was  conferred  on  it  by  the  publisher, 
that  it  would  have  come  out  sooner  had  not  Withcr's  reluctance  to  go  to  press 
retarded  it  ;  and  further,  that  the  poet  had  dispersed  among  his  friends  certain 
other  lighter  productions,  of  which  he  did  not  desire  to  witness  the  appearance  in 
print. 

This  volume  is  bound  up  with  "Abuses  Stript  and  Whipt,"  1615,  and  "A  Satyre," 
1615.    In  the  old  vellum  cover.

Dedicated  to  Prince  Charles  in  an  Epistle,  where  the  writer  describes  this  as  a 
"  Frontispice  to  a  greater  building  " — his  version  of  the  Psalms,  not  printed  till 
1632.  In  the  Preface,  which  follows,  he  announces  the  work  which  he  printed  in 
1620,  and  which  is  described  below. 

At  the  end  occur — i.  "A  Soliloquy  :  or  the  Authors  Preparation  of  himselfe 
vnto  the  Studie  and  Vse  of  the  Psalter ;"  2.  "A  Hymne  of  Thankesgiving  after  Sick- 
nesse  .  .  .  ." 

It  has  been  said  that  a  portrait  of  Wither  by  Delaram  should  accompany  this 
volume,  but  it  is  doubtful  whether  that  print  really  belongs  to  it.

By  Geo:  Wither,  Of  the  Societie  of  Lincolnes  Inne.  But  his  delight  is 
in  the  Law  of  the  Lord,  &c.  Psal.  I,  vers.  2.  London,  Printed  by  Ediv.

A — M  in  eights,  the  first  and  last  leaves  blank. 

Dedicated  "To  the  Noble  Young  Gentle-man  S'  lohn  Smith,  Knight  ;  onely 
Sonne  to  the  honourable  Knight,  S'  Thomas  Smith,  Gouernour  of  the  East-India 
Company,  &c."  Here  Wither  acknowledges  the  kindnesses  which  he  had  received 
at  the  hands  of  Sir  Thomas  Smith  ;  and  in  the  Notice  to  the  Reader  he  observes  : 
"  I  know,  that  many  of  you,  e're  this  time,  expected  the  first  Decade  of  the  Psalmes, 
according  to  the  promise,  in  my  Preparation.  And  therefore,  when  you  behold 
here,  but  a  tenth  part  of  it,  I  shall  be  thought  to  come  too  much  short  of  what  I 
intended  .  .  ."  He  adds  further  on  :  "  Discouragements,  and  hinderances,  I  have 
had  many,  since  I  began  to  meddle  with  the  Psalmes." 

This  work  was  registered  by  John  Harison,  30  March,  1620,  as  '■  The  way  to 
Blessednes,  Discouered  in  a  sixfold  exercise,  lic.''

Curo.    London  printed  for  lolin  Marriott  i62\.    Sm.  8vo.

Wither  himself  informs  us  that  as  many  as  30,000  copies  of  this  tract  were

Faire-Virtve,  the  Mistresse  of  Phil'arete.    Written  by  Him- 


selfe.  [Quotation  from  Catullus.]  London,  Printed  for  loIin  Gris- 
mond.   1622.    Sm.  8vo. 

A — P  in  eights,  first  leaf  blank. 

The  Stationer,  in  his  Notice  to  the  Reader,  states  that  this  was  "one  of  the 
Author's  first  Poems,  and  was  composed  many  years  agone,  and  vnknown  gotten 
out  of  his  custodie  by  an  acquaintance  of  his."  It  was  annexed  to  the  edition  of 
the  "  Juveniha"  in  1633,  together  with  the  "  Miscellany  of  Epigrams,"  which  occupy 
the  last  two  sheets. 

"  Fair  Virtue  "  is  mentioned  in  "Abuses  Stript  and  Whipt,"  1613,  and  was  pro- 
bably Wither's  earliest  work,  though  not  printed  till  1622.

title-page,  dated  1626,  was  added  to  the  unsold  copies.

ordinary  engraved  one.  The  former,  which  is  of  the  rarest  occurrence,  reads  as

poet's  miscellaneous  works  is  known  ;  the  title  given  above  was  probably  cancelled.

about  1625,  but  had  been  commenced  some  time  before,  and  continued  at  the 
request  of  James  I. 

This  is  considered  one  of  the  rarest  of  Wither's  productions.  Before  his  death, 
the  poet  prepared  a  revised  copy,  which  still  e.xists  in  MS. 


i6io  WITHER. 

WITHER,  George.  A  Collection  of  Emblemes,  Ancient  and 
Moderne :  Quickened  With  Metrical!  Illvstrations,  both  Morall  and 
Divine :  And  disposed  into  Lotteries,  That  Instruction,  and  Good 
Counsell,  may  bee  furthered  by  an  Honest  and  Pleasant  Recreation. 
By  George  Wither.  The  First  Booke.  London,  Printed  by  A.  M. 
for  Richard  Royston,  and  are  to  be  sold  at  his  Shop  in  Ivie-Lane. 
MDCXXXV.    Folio.   Plates.    R.  M. 

Frontispiece  by  W.  Marshall,  and  metrical  "  Preposition  "  to  the  same,  2  leaves  ; 
printed  title,  i  leaf;  "A  Writ  of  Prevention,"  i  leaf;  Dedication  to  Charles  I.,  2 
leaves  ;  A — S  in  fours,  except  K,  which  has  only  two  leaves  ;  (*),  2  leaves  ;  no 
sig.  T  ;  V — Dd  in  fours  ;  (*),  2  leaves  ;  Ee — Oo  in  fours,  the  last  leaf  occupied  by 
a  woodcut  of  a  dial. 

The  second,  third,  and  fourth  Books  of  the  Emblems  have  each  a  separate  title, 
but  the  signatures  are  continuous.  This  fine  and  large  copy  (iii  x  7^)  was 
obtained  in  Holland  many  years  ago,  and  was  then  in  the  old  vellum  cover.  It 
wants,  as  usual,  the  pointers  to  the  dial. 

The  plates  were  originally  engraved  for  the  Emblems  of  RoUenhagius  printed  at 
Cologne  and  Arnheim  in  1613-16,  which  is  described  in  the  catalogue.  The  plates 
having  been  purchased  by  a  London  publisher,  Wither  was  employed  to  write 
illustrative  verses  to  them. 

Halelviah   or,    Britans  Second  Remembrancer,  bringing  to 


Remembrance  (in  praisefull  and  Pcenitentiall  Hymns,  Spirituall 
Songs,  and  Morall-Odes)  Meditations,  advancing  the  glory  of  God,  in 
the  practise  of  Pietie  and  Vertue  ;  and  applyed  to  easie  Tunes,  to  be 
Sung  in  Families,  &c.  Composed  in  a  three-fold  Volume,  by  George 
Wither.  The  first,  contains  Hymns  Occasionall.  The  Second, 
Hymns-Temporary.  The  third.  Hymns  Personall.  That  all  Persons, 
according  to  their  Degrees,  and  Qualities,  may  at  all  Times,  and  upon 
all  eminent  Occasions,  be  remembred  to  praise  God ;  and  to  be  mind- 
full  of  their  Duties. 

One  woe  is  past,  the  second,  passing  on  ; 
Beware  the  third,  if  this,  in  vain  be  gone. 

London,  Printed  by  I.  L.  for  Andrew  Hebb,  at  the  Bell  in  Paids  Church- 
yard. 1641.    Sm.  8vo. 

A — Y  in  twelves,  the  last  two  leaves  blank. 

Dedicated  to  the  Parliament.  This  is  one  of  the  rarest  of  Wither's  publications. 
Not  more  than  three  perfect  copies  are  known.  The  present,  which  is  probably  by 
far  the  finest,  was  formerly  in  the  Bridgwater  collection,  and  has  the  arms  of  that 
family  on  the  sides.  At  page  408  the  four  concluding  lines  of  Hymn  XXVH.  have 
been  struck  through  with  a  pen  by  the  first  Earl  of  Bridgwater,  who,  in  allusion  to 
the  death  of  his  wife,  has  substituted  the  following  in  his  own  handwriting  :— 

"  And  let  not  me  ere  wed  againe, 
But  in  a  single  state  remaine  ; 
Lord,  to  thine  honour  let  it  be, 
And  satisfaction  unto  me." 


WITHER.  1611 

On  the  title-page  and  at  the  end  occurs  tlie  autograph  of  T.  Park,  and  on  the 
flyleaf  is  this  note  in  his  hand  ;  "  This  singular  copy  of  a  liook  most  singularly 
rare,  must  have  been  huddled  into  some  large  lot  at  the  Duke  of  Bridgwater's  sale 
in  Aug.  1800.  I  purchased  it  about  three  months  afterward  at  a  book-shop  near 
Holborn.  The  only  second  copy  I  have  traced  was  obtained  from  Herbert  Ijy  Mr. 
A.  Dalrymple,  who  says  that '  many  fine  things  are  interspersed  in  it,  perhaps  no- 
where to  be  surpassed.'  See  his  '  Extracts,'  1785,  p.  15."  It  is  added  in  pencil  : 
"  Mr.  Heber  since  bot.  a  copy  at  Brand's  sale." 

The  copy  mentioned  as  belonging  to  Heber,  and  which  is  now  in  the  collection 
of  T.  Gaisford,  Esq.,  has  an  old  M.S.  note  on  the  flyleaf  which  states  it  to  have  been 
a  presentation  copy  from  the  author.  Dalrymple's  copy  is  now  in  the  British 
Museum.  The  volume  here  described  was  sold  as  a  Bridgwater  duplicate,  but 
there  is  no  copy  now  in  that  collection.

Vox  Pacifica  :  A  voice  tending  to  The  Pacification  of  God's 


wrath  ;  and  offering  those  Propositions,  or  Conditions,  by  the  accepta- 
tion, and  performance  whereof,  in  some  good  measure,  a  firme  and 
continuing  Peace  may  be  obtained.  It  is  directed  to  the  King,  Parlia- 
ments, and  People  of  these  Islands  :  By  Geo.  Wither  Esquire,  (a 
Commander  in  this  War)  heretofore  their  unheeded  Remembrancer 
of  Plagues  and  Deliverances  past ;  and  their  timely  Forewarner  of 
the  Judgments  now  come.  He  hath  disposed  it  into  six  Books,  or 
Canto's,  whereof  foure  onely  are  contained  in  this  Volume  ;  and  the 
other  deferred  to  be  hereafter  published,  as  there  shall  be  Cause. 
London,  Printed  by  Robert  Austin,  in  the  Old-Baily.  cli.  loc  xlv.    8vo.

deliverances  upon  the  Appeals  of  the  Parliaments,  and  Royall  Forces

Scotland,  and  for  the  late  Surrender  of  Edinburgh  Castle,  &c.  Humbly

cern  ;  but,  was  first  intended  only,  for  two  or  three  of  the  Authors

WITHER,  George.  Salt  upon  Salt :  Made  out  of  certain  In- 
genious Verses  and  the  Death  of  His  Highness  Ensuing.  By  which

Salt  seasons  all  things,  saving  onely  those 
Which  must  feed  Fishes,  Maggots,  Dogs  and  Crows. 
Read  it,  and  heed  it.  For  you  need  it ; 
And  so  God  speed  it.

WITHER,  George.  A  Triple  Paradox :  Affixed  to  a  Covnter- 
Mure  Raised  against  the  Furious  Batteries  of  Restraint,  Slander  and 
Poverty,  The  three  Grand  Engines  of  The  World,  the  Flesh  and  the 
Devil.  By  Major  George  Wither.  Who,  now  beleaguered  by  their 
Forces,  throws  out  unto  them  this  Defiance.  The  said  Paradox  main- 
tains these  Particulars,  That  Confinement  is  more  safe  then  Liberty, 
Slander  more  advantageous  than  Praise,  Poverty  more  profitable  than 
Riches.  Nttsqiiaiii,  non  potest  esse  virtiiti  locus. — Seneca.  London, 
Printed  for  the  Author.    1661.    8vo. 

A — K  in  fours.     In  verse.

Inhabitants  of  the  Isles  of  Great  Brittain  :  And  especially  to  those  that 
have  Hypocritically  pretended  to  Justice,  Mercy,  Honesty  and  Reli- 
gion ;  (as  also  to  them  that  have  lived  in  open  Prophanessand  Impiety) 
summoning  them  to  Repentance,  by  denouncing  God's  Judgements, 
and  declaring  his  Mercy,  offered  in  the  Everlasting  Gospel.  Warrant-

WITHER,  George.  Tuba-Pacifica.  Seasonable  Praecautions, 
whereby  is  sounded  forth  a  Retreat  from  the  War  intended  between 
England  and  the  United-Provinces  of  Lower  Germany.  By  Geo  : 
Wither,  a  Lover  of  Peace,  and  heartily  well-affected  toward  both 
Nations.  NtJitis  Cautela  non  nocet.  [Quotation  from  Matth.  v.  9.] 
AiDio.  1664.  Imprinted  for  the  Author,  and  is  to  be  disposed  of,  rather 
for  Love  then  Money.    Sm.  8vo. 

A — D  in  fours.    In  verse. 

Three  Private  Meditations,  Which  being,  for  the  most  Part,  of 


Publick  Concernment,  are  therefore  Published  by  their  Author,  Geo. 
Wither.  The  First,  is,  a  Private  Thanksgiving,  consisting  of  three 
Hymns,  whereby  God  is  magnified  for  his  Mercy  vouchsafed  in  the 
late  Ingagement  between  the  English  and  the  Dutch,  in  June  1665, 
Composed  after  celebrating  the  Publick  Thanksgiving  commanded  by 
the  King.  The  Second,  is  a  Sacrifice  of  Praise  and  Prayer,  by  him 
offered  to  Almighty  God,  for  His  providential  respect,  to  Him,  his  Wife 
and  Children,  during  his  Imprisonment  in  the  disgraceful  Goal  of 
Newgate,  when  left  destitute  of  all  ordinary  means  of  subsistance,  by 
being  deprived  both  of  his  Estate  and  Liberty.  The  Third,  Intituled 
Nil  Ultra,  is  a  Soliloquium,  wherein  this  Author  expresses  the  impro- 
bability of  an  effectual  proceeding  further,  to  prevent  the  Sins  and 
Plagues  increasing,  by  ought  which  he  can  offer  to  consideration.  Re- 
printed in  the  Year,  1666.    8vo.    R.  M.

A — Ee  in  fours.     With  a  portrait  of  Wither,  "  ^tat.  su£e  79." 
This  is  a  selection  from  some  of  Wither's  previous  publications.     At  p.  47  he 
mentions  his  "  Motto"  as  printed  in  1618,  but  no  such  edition  is  now  known.     He 
speaks  of  some  of  his  works  as  even  then  difficult  to  procure,  particularly  his  first 
"  Remembrancer,"  1628,  which  is  now  a  common  book. 

Majesty  in  Misery,  Or  an  Imploration  to  the  King  of  Kings  ;

1 68 1.    Folio. 

A  single  leaf.     In  verse. 

This  small  piece  of  poetry,  although  it  purports,  as  vi'e  see,  to  have  proceeded 
from  the  king's  pen,  is  usually  ascribed  to  Wither.  It  is  inserted  in  the  later 
editions  of  the  "  Eikon  Basilike,"  and  in  the  edition  of  Wither's  Emblems,  Svo.,

i6i8  WITHER— WITTY. 

WITHER,  George.  Divine  Potms  (by  way  of  Paraphrase)  on  the 
Ten  Commandments.  Illustrated  with  Twelve  Copper  Plates,  shew- 
ing how  Personal  Punishments  has  been  inflicted  on  the  Transgressors 
of  these  Commandments,  as  is  Recorded  in  the  Holy  Scripture. 
Never  before  printed.  Also  a  Metrical  Paraphrase  upon  the  Creed  and 
the  Lords  Prayer.  Written  by  George  Wither  Esq;  Author  of  Brittains 
Remembrancer.  [Quotation  from  Psal.  cxix.  5.]  London  Printed  by 
T.  S.  And  are  to  be  sold  by  R.  Janeiuay.  .  .  .   1688.    8vo.    R. 

A — I  3  in  eights. 

It  appears  from  the  dedication  of  Wither's  daughter  and  only  surviving  child, 
"  To  all  such  as  have  formerly  been  Friends  to  the  Author,"  that  the  original  plates 
mentioned  in  the  author's  Preface  were  lost,  and  that  others  were  made  for  it  after 
his  death.  A  MS.  note  in  the  copy  here  described  says  that  the  plates  which 
appear  in  the  volume  are  similar  to  those  which  occur  in  a  4to.  tract  by  Jenner, 
called  "  Punishments  inflicted  on  the  breakers  of  the  Ten  Commandments,"  1650. 
The  lady,  who  describes  herself  as  Wither's  only  living  representative,  signs  herself 
"  E.  B."  and  dates  "  From  my  lodgings  at  Mr.  Snowdens  a  Printers,"  in  Great  Carter 
Lane.  The  copies  of  the  present  work  dated  1697  and  1728  are  merely  new  title- 
pages  printed  for  the  unsold  copies  of  the  edition  of  1688.

most  Celebrated  Conundrums  Now  in  Vogue  Among  People  of  High 

Taste.  London:  Printed  for  W.  Owen,  near  Temple-Bar.  MDCCL.  8vo.

WITTENBERG.  Dye  zaigung  des  hochlobwirdigen  hailigthums 
der  Stifft  kirchen  aller  hailigen  zu  wittenburg.  [Colophon.]  Gcdruckt

vnd  neiin.   4to.     Woodcuts.    BR.  M. 

a — 1  in  fours. 

This  beautiful  and  rare  volume  contains  118  wood  engravings  by  Lucas  Cranach 
of  the  relics,  reliquaries,  and  treasures  of  the  Church  of  All  Saints  at  Wittenberg. 
On  the  title  is  a  copperplate  engraving  with  Cranach's  monogram  dated  15 10.  It 
contains  two  portraits,  which,  according  to  Bartsch,  vol.  vii.,  p.  276,  represent  Albert 
the  Bold  and  his  son  Henry  the  Pious,  Dukes  of  Saxony,  but  according  to  Passavant, 
vol.  iv.,  p.  6,  Frederick  111.  and  his  brother  John  I.  are  the  persons  represented. 
Neither  Bartsch  nor  Passavant  appears  to  have  been  aware  that  this  engraving 
belonged  to  the  title-page  of  a  book.  Graesse  informs  us  that  three  copies  of  this 
choice  volume  are  known  upon  vellum,  one  at  Berlin,  a  second  at  Munich,  and  a 
third  in  private  hands  in  Silesia.

WOLCOTT,  Roger.  Poetical  Meditations,  being  the  Improve- 
ment of  some  Vacant  Hours.  By  Roger  Wolcott,  Esq.;  with  a  Preface 
by  the  Reverend  Mr.  Bulkley  of  Colchester.  New-London  :  Printed 
and  Sold  by  T.  Green,  1725.    Sm.  Svo.    R.  M. 

a — d  6  in  eights,  including  a  half-title  ;  B — E  in  eights  ;  F — G  2  in  half-sheets. 

A  little  volume  of  extreme  rarity.  The  Preface  by  Bulkley  consists  of  an  elabo- 
rate essay  on  Government,  dated  from  Colchester  [N.E.]  December  24,  1724.  The 
work  itself  opens  with  a  metrical  dedication  by  the  author  to  Mr.  Timothy  Edwards, 
dated  from  Windsor  [N.E.]  January  4,  1722-3.  Pp.  1-18  are  occupied  by  religious 
poems,  but  on  p.  19  occurs,  in  verse:  "A  Brief  Account  of  the  Agency  of  the 
Honourable  John  Winthrop,  Esq.  ;  in  the  Court  of  King  Charles  the  Second,  Anno. 
Dom.  1662.  When  he  obtained  for  the  Colony  of  Connecticut  His  Majesty's  Gra- 
cious Charter."    This  occupies  the  remainder  of  the  volume. 

WOLFFEN,  Caspar.  Kalender  oder  Laaszbiichli  sampt  der 
Schreybtafel,  Massen  vnd  Jarmarckten  vff  das  M.D.  vnd  LXVII.  Jar. 
Gestelt  vff  den  Meridianum  der  vralten  loblichen  statt  Ziirych,  durch 
Caspar  Wolffen  der  Artznyen  Doctor  daselbst.  [Colophon.]  Getruckt 
zil  ZilrycJi  in  der  Froschow,by  Christoffel  Froschozver.  [1567.]    4to. 

Sixteen  leaves. 

This  very  curious  almanac  and  diary  was  quite  unknown  till  the  discovery  of 
this  copy  by  Mr.  C.  J.  Stewart  in  the  covers  of  another  book. 

In  Jocher's  Gelehrte  Lexicon  it  is  stated  that  the  author  was  a  native  of  Zurich 
and,  in  1566,  succeeded  Conrad  Gesner  as  Professor  of  Medicine  in  the  University 
of  that  town.     He  was  also  the  editor  of  several  learned  works.

i620  WOLFI  US— WOMEN. 

A — I  in  eights,  the  last  leaf  blank. 

This  volume  contains  fifty-four  woodcuts,  each  of  which  occupies  a  page,  and 
opposite  to  each  is  a  descriptive  account  of  it,  exactly  in  the  manner  of  the  Block- 
books.  It  appears  very  probable  that  there  was  a  Block-book  edition  which  is  now 
lost  An  earlier  edition  is  recorded  by  Panzer,  "  Legenda  S.  Wolfgangi  Episcopi 
Ratisponensis  cum  officio  vesperarum  matutinarum  et  misse  de  eodem.  Impresstim 
in  opido  Burgdorf.  1475.  Folio."  20  leaves.  But  it  is  not  mentioned  whether  it 
has  any  woodcuts,  and  very  little  appears  to  be  known  about  it,  even  the  identity  of 
the  place  of  printing  being  disputed.

WONDERFUL— WOOD.  1621 

WONDERFUL  DELIVERANCE.  The  Wonderful  Deliverance 
at  Sea  Or,  The  miraculous  Preservation  of  several  Persons  belonging 
to  the  Ship  called  William  and  Dorothy,  lately  cast  away  in  a  Voyage 
to  New-Castle.  Who  by  a  most  gracious  Providence  were  kept  alive 
for  the  Space  of  Twelve  Days  and  Nights  without  any  Food,  in  a  Small 
Boat,  on  the  Main  Sea  :  In  all  which  time,  onely  one  Childe  Perished, 
being  Starved  to  Death  with  Cold  and  Hunger,  and  the  rest  were  at 
last  taken  up,  and  carried  into  Holland.  On  the  28th  of  November 
Last.  And  Several  of  them  are  now  safely  arrived  in  England.  Printed 
for  Benjamin  Pierce.    [Adoui  iG^^.]    4to. 

Four  leaves. 

The  date  of  this  exceedingly  rare  tract  has  been  cut  off  by  the  binder.

A — R  4  in  eights. 

The  portraits  do  not  occur  in  the  first  edition,  printed  in  1657.  They  are  mostly 
from  the  same  plates  as  those  in  the  series  which  is  described  in  this  catalogue 
under  "  Effigies,"  but  some  of  them  have  been  coarsely  re-engraved,  as  for  example 
that  of  Henry  IV.  The  impressions  are  necessarily  very  inferior  to  those  in  the 
"  Effigies."

The  address  to  the  Reader  is  subscribed  "  Philaretes,"  but  the  initials  "J.  H." 
are  at  the  foot  of  some  verses  which  foliow,  and  they  appear  to  be  those  of  the  writer 
of  the  tract.  The  author  of  "  The  Defence  of  Tobacco,"  written  in  reply  to  this 
work,  declares  himself  ignorant  of  the  name  of  his  opponent.

WORTLEY,  Sir  Francis.  Characters  and  Elegies.  By  Francis 
Wortley,  Knight  and  Baronet.  Printed  in  the  Yeere,  CLD.  IDC  XL  VL 
4to. 

A — K  2  in  fours,  and  a  leaf  of  errata  which  is  misplaced  between  pp.  32-3. 

This  copy,  in  the  original  gilt  vellum  binding  with  strings,  and  with  the  author's 
autograph  corrections  and  additions,  was  apparently  that  presented  to  Lady 
Abigail  Sherard,  and  came  from  the  library  of  Lord  Harborough.  On  the  flyleaf 
at  the  beginning  is  a  letter  in  the  handwriting  of  Sir  F.  Woitley  to  her  ladyship, 


IVOR  TLB  Y—  WOTTON.  1623 

and  nt  the  end  are  copied,  in  a  different  hand,  two  short  pieces,  entitled  "  Bri- 
tannicus  his  blessing,  and  Britannicus  liis  wellcome,"  printed  together,  at  Cambridge, 
4to.,  1646.  The  letter  has  been  printed  in  "  Notes  and  Queries."  Among  the 
persons  upon  whom  elegies  are  here  found  is  Francis  Quarles  the  poet.  The  author 
appears  to  have  been  much  vexed  at  the  numerous  errors  of  the  press.  At  the  end 
of  the  last  page  he  has  written  "  never  worse  printer",  and  at  the  head  of  the  errata 
he  says,  "  I  shall  desire  the  reader  of  these  Phansies  to  pardon  the  errors  of  the 
Presse  :  I  used  the  help  of  a  meane  Scholar  in  the  copy,  and  being  (as  I  am  a 
prisoner)  could  not  see  the  errors  but  in  the  book."

A  Declaration  from  York,  By  Sir  Francis  Wortley,  Knight 

and  Baronet.  In  vindication  of  himself  from  divers  aspersions  and 
rumours  concerning  the  drawing  of  his  sword,  and  other  actions^ 
wherein  he  desires  to  give  the  World  satisfaction.  London,  Printed  by 
A.  N.for  Thomas  Warren,  1642.    4to. 

Four  leaves. 

This  pamphlet  relates  to  the  desire  of  the  Parliamentary  party  to  remove  the 
large  magazine  of  arms  and  ammunition  stationed  at  Hull,  in  consequence  of  an 
apprehension  that  the  local  gentry  were  too  strongly  royalist  in  their  opinions,  and 
to  the  personal  resistance  said  to  have  been  offered  by  Wortley  to  the  Sheriff  and 
Committee  authorized  by  the  Parliament  to  act  in  the  business.

Title  and  Preface,  2  leaves  ;  A — G  in  twelves,  last  leaf  blank.     First  edition. 

In  an  address  from  the  Printer  to  the  Reader,  we  are  informed  that  these  letters 
were  published  from  the  original  in  Wotton's  autograph.  They  range  in  date  be- 
tween i6u  and  1638,  and  abound  in  historical  and  personal  interest.  At  p.  30 
is  an  account  of  the  Destruction  of  the  Globe  Theatre  by  fire  in  161 3  ;  we  have  at 
p.  140:  "A  Hymne  made  by  H.  W.  in  the  nights  of  a  great  sickness  abroad;" 
and  at  p.  155  occurs  a  curious  account  of  the  attempted  performance  of  Robert 
Tailour's  play,  "  The  Hog  hath  lost  his  Pearl,"  by  sixteen  London  apprentices.

Two  hundred  and  fifty  leaves,  without  catchwords,  pagination,  or  signatures. 
The  number  of  lines  in  the  page  varies  from  twenty-seven  to  thirty. 

This  work  has  no  regular  title,  and  the  contents  are  set  out  at  too  great  length 
to  give  them  in  full.  It  consists  of  eighteen  different  pieces,  amongst  which  occur 
the  history  of  Guiscard  and  Sigismunda,  the  banishment  of  Cosmo  de  Medicis  from 
Florence  ;  of  St.  Bernard  and  his  brother  ;  the  history  of  the  burning  of  Jerome  of 
Prague  and  John  Huss ;  Apuleius's  story  of  the  Golden  Ass,  &c.  The  first  six  leaves 
are  occupied  with  the  table  of  contents  and  the  dedicatory  epistle  to  Ulrich,  Duke 
of  Wurtemberg,  the  latter  being  dated  from  Stuttgart  April  18,  1478.  It  has  wood- 
cut initials  at  the  beginning  of  the  chapters  and  paragraphs.  It  is  attributed  by 
Hain  to  the  press  of  Conrad  Fyner  of  Esslingen.

del  sobre  dicho  capita  en  todas  las  prouincias  i  conquista  dela  nueua

a — e  vi  in  eights,  the  last  leaf  blank. 

The  only  copy  known  of  this  edition.  It  is  described  from  this  copy  by  Harrisse, 
B.  A.  v.,  pt.  2.  The  title  is  within  a  woodcut  border,  and  beneath  the  words,  "  Con- 
quista del  Peru"  is  a  rude  woodcut  of  a  town  with  vessels  before  it.  According  to 
Mr.  Harrisse  it  is  the  second  edition,  having  been  printed,  in  his  opinion,  by  Juan 
de  Junta  at  Seville,  a  little  earlier  than  the  folio  of  1547.  The  first  edition  was 
printed  at  Seville  in  1534. 

XEREZ,  Francisco  de.  Libro  primo  de  la  conquista  del  Perv 
&  prouincia  del  Cuzco  de  le  Indie  occidental!.  Con  gratia  &  priui- 
legio  per  anni  X.  [Colophon.]  Stampato  in  Viuegia  per  Maestro 
Stcphano  da  Sabio  del  MDXXX  V.    Nel  mese  di  Marzo.    4to. 

Title  and  preliminaries,  4  leaves  ;  a — fin  eights  ;  g,  10  leaves. 

On  the  second  leaf  of  the  volume  is  a  fuller  title  or  description  of  the  book, 
which  begins,  "  La  admirabile  ampla  et  vera  narratione  della  conquista  del  Perv  & 
prouintia  del  Cuzco  chiamata  la  noua  Castiglia,  conquistata  per  il  molto  magnifico 
&  valeroso  caualliero  Francesco  Picciaro  caualliero  della  citta  de  Trugillo  de 
Spagna  come  capitano  general  della  Cesarea  &:  catholica  maiesta  del  Imperador 
Carolo  qnto  Re  di  Spagna  &c.  mandata  a  sua  Maiesta  per  Francesco  de  Xerez 
secretario  del  supradetto  signor  capitano  generale."  &c. 

The  original  Spanish  version  of  this  book  was  printed  at  Seville,  in  folio,  in  1534, 
under  the  title  of  "  Verdadera  relacion  de  la  conquista  del  Peru,"  &c.  It  consists  of 
nineteen  leaves,  including  the  title.  Another  piece,  with  a  very  similar  title,  "  La 
conquista  del  Peru  llamada  la  nueva  Castilla,"  but  consisting  of  only  eight  leaves, 
was  printed  at  Seville  in  the  same  year.  Under  "  Xerez,"  Brunet  refers  to  "  Con- 
quista," but  under  that  head  he  gives  the  title  of  this  anonymous  work,  and  then 
describes  this  Italian  version  of  Xerez  as  though  it  were  a  translation  of  it,  while  he 
does  not  describe  the  original  of  Xerez'  work  at  all. 

Harrisse  (No.  200)  describes  this  book  as  being  in  the  Grenville  Catalogue, 
p.  536,  but  it  is  the  Spanish  edition  of  the  anonymous  "  Conquista  del  Peru, 
Sevilla.  1534,"  that  is  described  at  that  page.     This  book  is  described  at  p.  818. 

Conquista  del  Peru.    See  OviEDO.

ratione  fuerit,  nobis  non  adhuc  notum." — Bibliptheca  Hispaiia  Veius,  v.  2,  p.  339.

The  edition  is  described  by  Graesse,  but  he  erroneously  assigns  266  leaves  to  it, 
the  present  copy  being  unquestionably  perfect  and  complete,  with  224  leaves  (28 
sheets  of  S  leaves  each).  The  body  of  the  \iox\  ends  with  the  colophon  as  given 
above  on  the  recto  of  D  2.  On  the  verso  of  the  same  leaf  we  read  "Hie  nach 
volgend  Sant  Brigitten  gebettlyn  "  over  a  woodcut.  These  prayers  end  on  the 
verso  of  E  v.  On  E  vi.  begins  "Andechtig  lob  der  glyder  marie  "  over  a  woodcut 
of  the  Virgin  and  Child,  and  this  ends  the  volume  on  the  recto  of  Fviii.  This  last 
part  consists  of  prayers  addressed  to  the  different  parts  of  the  body  of  the  Virgin. 
"  Zu  den  Armen  Marie,"  "  Zu  den  briisten  marie,"  "  Zu  der  nasen  marie,"  S:c.

APPENDIX. 


A.,  T.  The  Massacre  of  Money.  Teruntio  sen  vitiosa  mice  non 
emitur.  London  Printed  by  Thomas  Creede  for  Thomas  Btishcll.  1602. 
4to.    R.  M. 

A — F  3  in  fours. 

A  poem  in  6-line  stanzas.  The  dedication  is  addressed:  "To  the  Worshipful!, 
vertuous,  and  most  worthy  gentlemen,  M.  William  and  M.  Thomas  Bedles  health 
and  their  hearts  content,"  and  is  signed  with  the  initials  T.  A. 

The  name  of  the  author  has  never  been  ascertained,  though  the  book  has  usually 
been  ascribed  to  Thomas  Achelly.  Mr.  Collier,  who  gives  a  very  full  account 
of  the  book  in  his  "  Bibliographical  Catalogue,"  remarks  that  it  is  ascribed  to  him 
on  no  very  sufficient  grounds. 

ADAMSON,  John.  The  Commonplace  book  of  John  Adamson, 
1665-6,  and  of  members  of  his  family,  being  a  miscellany  of  prose  and 
verse,  written  between  1665  and  1690.    Sm.  8vo. 

Manuscript  on  paper. 

ADAMUS  CARTHUSIENSIS.  Sermones  Ade  Cartusiensis  et 
aliorum.  Vita  S.  Hugonis  Eplscopi  Lincolniensis.  Vita  ejusdem  Ade. 
Etc.    Folio.    MS.  on  vellum. 

Two  hundred  and  twenty  folios,  written  in  the  earlier  part  of  the  fifteenth 
century,  in  double  columns,  with  rubrics  and  coloured  initials. 

At  the  beginning,  on  the  fly-leaf,  facing  folio  i,  is  this  inscription  :  "  Liber 
Domus  Salutationis  matris  dei  Ordinis  Cartusiensis  prope  london;"  and  at  the 
end,  after  the  table  of  contents,  is  written :  "  Liber  Domus  Ordinis  Cartusiensis 
prope  London." 

1.  "  Incipit  secunda  pars  sermonum  quos  Magister  dompnus  adam  ordinis  car- 
tusiensis monachus  composuit :  quos  et  nunc  propter  fastidium  lectoris  amouendum 
constat  esse  abbreuiatos."  After  twenty-three  sermons  is  this  colophon:  "  E.\- 
pliciunt  sermones  secunde  partis  magistri  ade  cartusiensis."     Ff.  i-ggb. 

2.  "  Sermo  beati  fulgencij  episcopi  :  de  natiuitate  domini."  Inc.  "  Cupientes 
aliquid."     Ff.  ggb-ioib. 

3.  "Sermo  beati  ma.\imi  episcopi:  de  natiuitate  domini."  Inc.  "Justissime 
fratres."     Ff.  ioib-103. 

4.  "  Sermo  beati  augustini  episcopi  :  de  sancto  stephano."  Inc.  "  Quum  in  deo." 
Ff.  103-106. 

J.  "  Sermo  venerabilis  bede  presb>'teri :  de  sancto  Johanne  euangelista."  Inc. 
"  Gaudeamus  fratres."     Ff.  106-111. 

6.  "  De  festo  sancte  Agnetis  secundo."    Inc.  "  Igitur  dum  parentes."    F.  1 1  lab. 

7.  "  De  Vita  Sancti   Hugonis."    After  this  title  (which  is  written  by  a  hand 


1636  APPENDIX. 

somewhat  later  than  that  of  the  first  scribe)  follows  :  "  Prologs  abbreuiacionis 
uite  beati  Hugonis,"  commencing,  "  Inspecta  serie  descripcionis  uite  beati  hugonis 
quam  composuit  frater  A.  de  quo  in  salutacione  ante  prologum  mencio  fit  expressa." 
This  last-mentioned  prologue  or  dedication  commences  on  the  following  page  : 
"  Dominis  et  amicis  in  Christo  carissimis,  R.  Priori  et  qui  cum  eo  sunt  Sanctis 
Withamensibus  monachis  seruorum  suorum  minimus  frater  A.  uite  que  nunc  est  et 
future  gaudia."  The  Life  is  comprised  in  five  books,  each  of  which  is  divided  into 
chapters.  The  first  chapter  of  book  i.  begins  thus  :  "  Hugo  genetricis  solacio 
.  .  .  orbatus  est."  Chapter  xxviii.  of  the  last  book  terminates  :  "  ad  gloriam 
dei  qui  viuit  et  gloriatur  super  omnia  deus  benedictus  in  secula  AMEN.  Explicit 
liber  abbreuiacionis  uite  beati  Hugonis."     Ff.  Ii2-2i3b. 

After  one  blank  column  and  one  blank  page  are  the  following  articles,  written 
in  long  lines,  not  much  later  than  the  foregoing  pieces  :— 

8.  "  De  vita  et  conuersacione  Magistri  Ade  cartusiensis  secundum  quod  habetur 
in  cronica  domus  de  Witham."  Inc.  "  Fuit  itaque  vir  iste  Magister  Adam  in  con- 
finio  anglie  et  scocie  oriundus."  The  Life  is  followed  by  a  list  of  so  many  of  his 
works  as  the  writer  could  remember,  commencing  with  his  Sermons,  in  two  volumes. 
Ff.  2i4b-2i8b. 

9.  "  De  priore  de  Witham  alberto  nomine."  Inc.  "  Eodem  tempore  Dominus 
Hugo  lincolniensis  episcopus."     F.  219. 

10.  "  Sermo  in  die  sancto  Pasche,"  on  the  text  "Alleluia."  Inc.  "Patres  re- 
uerendi  et  confratres  in  domino  carissimi."  Written  by  another  and  somewhat 
later  hand,  in  long  lines,  and  in  smaller  writing.     Ff.  219-220. 

11.  "  Sermo  deuoti  ac  religiosi  patris  domini  thome  kempis  ordinis  diui  augustini 
de  cruce  dominica  in  sacra  religione  portanda."  Inc.  "  Michi  Autem  absit  gloriari." 
This  is  written  in  long  lines,  by  a  scribe  of  the  fifteenth  century,  on  three  leaves, 
inserted  after  the  twentieth  sermon  of  Adam  the  Carthusian.     Ff.  8o*-82*. 

On  the  last  page  (f.  220b) :  "  Contenta  in  hoc  volumine,"  &c.  All  the  above- 
mentioned  articles  are  referred  to  except  those  numbered  6  and  n.  At  the  foot  is 
written  :  "  Liber  Domus  Ordinis  Cartusiensis  prope  London." 

Adam  the  Carthusian  is  mentioned  in  the  "  Bibliotheca  Carthusiana"  of  T. 
Petreius,  Colonicv,  1609,  8vo.,  as  well  as  by  Pits,  Vossius,  and  others,  who  generally 
state  that  he  flourished  A.D.  1340,  or  thereabouts.  From  the  account  of  him  con- 
tained in  the  present  MS.,  it  appears  that  he  lived  at  a  considerably  earlier  period, 
and  that  he  was  first  a  monk  and  then  Abbot  of  the  Prasmonstratensian  monastery 
of  Dryburgh  in  Scotland,  but  that  he  subsequently  joined  the  Carthusian  order,  and 
became  a  monk  of  Witham,  county  of  Somerset — their  first  house  in  England, 
which  was  at  that  time  under  the  special  care  of  St.  Hugh,  who  had  been  prior 
there.     This  prelate  deceased  a.d.  1200. 

The  Sermons  are  not  mentioned  by  Petreius,  nor,  so  far  as  appears,  by  any 
other  writer.  The  twenty-first  of  these  is  entitled  "  Sermo  in  die  sancti  hugonis 
episcopi  lincolniensis;"  and  it  is  observable  that  the  saint  is  spoken  of  historically, 
and  that  a  "  Life"  of  him  is  cited,  as  weU  as  the  "  Speculum"  of  Vincent  of  Beau- 
vais,  who  flourished  a.d.  1250. 

These  facts,  though  consistent  with  the  date  assigned  to  Adam  the  Carthusian 
by  Petreius  and  others,  viz.  1340,  do  not  agree  with  his  biography  as  recorded  in 
this  MS.  (artt.  8,  9).  Unless  there  were  two  Carthusian  writers  of  the  name  of 
Adam,  the  date  1340  must  be  an  erroneous  one.  The  sermon  on  St.  Hugh  would 
appear  to  have  been  interpolated  into  the  series. 

Petreius  refers  to  the  "Life  of  St.  Hugh,"  in  five  books,  and  cites  words  of  the 
author's  which  are  found  in  the  present  copy  ;  "  — que  de  uiro  beatissimo  lincolniensi 
presule  uidi  audiui,"  &c.,  f.  nib,  col.  2.  He  imagines  that  the  book  was  written, 
not  by  Adam  the  Carthusian,  but  by  an  archdeacon  of  Lincoln  who  was  familiarly 


APPENDIX.  1637 

acquainted  with  St.  Hugh — an  opinion  which  is  plainly  contrary  to  the  evidence 
furnished  by  this  MS. 

This  volume  is  in  the  original  monastic  binding',  consisting  of  massive  boards 
of  oak,  covered  with  white  leather,  and  with  the  ancient  chased  clasps  attached  to 
leathern  thongs.  It  is  a  valuable  and  interesting  relic  of  the  library  of  the  ancient 
Carthusian  monastery  of  London,  on  the  site  of  which  Thomas  Sutton  founded  the 
Charterhouse  Scliool  and  Almshouses. 

.(ESOP.  La  Vita  de  Esopo  Hystoriata.  Fabulo  de  Esopo  hys- 
toriate  \senz  alcuna  uota].    Sm.  8vo.    Woodcuts. 

"  La  Vita  ",  A— K  iv.  in  eights,  the  last  leaf  blank.     "  Fabule  ",  A— K  in  eights. 

The  title  to  the  "  Vita  de  Esopo  "  is  over  a  woodcut,  on  which  are  the  initials 
.  I. .  C.  These  initials  are  engraved  by  Nagler,  "  Monogrammisten  Lexikon,"  vol.  iii.. 
No.  2061.  He  is  unable  to  give  the  name  of  the  artist  or  wood-engraver  to  whom 
they  belong,  but  he  remarks  that  they  occur  in  the  "  Triompho  di  Fortuna  "  of 
Sigismondo  Fanti,  printed  at  Venice,  1526,  and  it  maybe  inferred  that  this  edition 
of  yEsop  was  from  a  Venetian  press,  about  the  same  date.  It  does  not  appear  to  be 
described  by  bibliographers.  Some  of  the  engravings  in  the  work  of  Fanti  bear  the 
initials  I.  M.  on  a  tablet.     See  Nagler,  vol.  iii..  No.  2809.

Let  Qvilibet  beware  of  Qvodlibet.    \_No  place,  printer's  name, 

or  date.'\     [1590.]    Sm.  8vo. 

Six  leaves,  including  the  title. 

A   Position  maintained  by  I.  B.  before  the  late   Earle  of 

Huntingdon  :  viz.  Priests  are  executed  not  for  Religion,  but  for 
treason.    Nczvly  imprinted.    1600.    Sm.  8vo. 

Eight  leaves,  the  first  blank. 

These  tracts  are  bound  together  in  one  volume.  They  are  in  the  original  limp 
vellum  binding,  and  on  the  fly-leaf  is  inscribed  :  "Samuel  Bamfordes  booke  of  his 
fathers  gifte." 

The  third  piece  in  the  volume  is  addressed  by  James  Bamford  "  To  his  welbe- 
lovcd  in  Christ,  the  Parishioners  of  Saint  Olaves  in  Southwarke,"  and  the  initials 
in  the  title  of  the  seventh  tract  appear  to  indicate  the  same  author.  It  therefore 
seems  e.\tremely  probable  that  Bamford  was  the  author  of  the  whole  of  the  pieces, 
and  presented  the  volume  to  his  son  as  a  collection  of  his  works.  That  he  did  not 
put  his  name  to  them  is  not  to  be  wondered  at,  as  they  are  written  on  the  Puritan 
side.  The  tract  in  defence  of  Darell  is  especially  curious.  Being  in  opposition  to 
the  works  published  on  the  same  subject  by  Archbishop  Harsnet  and  Bishop 
Barlow,  the  author  evidently  thought  it  safer  to  put  neither  his  own  name  nor  even 
that  of  a  publisher  to  it.  The  amount  of  credulity  it  displays  in  regard  to  witches 
and  witchcraft  is  surprising,  even  for  those  days.  With  the  exception  of  the 
"  Short  Dialogue  concerning  the  plagues  infection,"  all  these  tracts  appear  to  be 
undescribed  by  bibliographers.

At  the  end  of  the  play  occurs  this  curious  note  by  Brome  :  "  Courteous  Reader, 
You  shal  finde  in  this  Booke  more  then  was  presented  upon  the  Stage,  and  left  out 
of  the  Presentation,  for  superfluous  length  (as  some  of  the  Players  pretended).  I 
thoght  good  it  should  be  inserted  according  to  the  allowed  Original  ;  and  as  it  was, 
at  first,  intended  for  the  Cock-pit  Stage,  in  the  right  of  my  most  deserving  Friend 
Mr.  William  Beeston,  unto  whom  it  properly  appertained  .  .  ."

CARMARDEN,  Richard.  A  Caveat  for  the  Qucne  wherin  is 
declared  dyvers  Deceypts  used  towards  hur  Highnesse  by  hur  Majesties 
Officers  appoynted  for  the  receypts  of  hur  Customes  in  the  Poorte  of 
London  to  the  greate  hinderaunce  of  hur  Ma""  and  spoyling  of  hur 
Subiects  And  therwithal  is  nooted  how  little  to  hur  benefitt  the  lettinge 
of  the  same  to  ferme  doeth  serue.  compiled  by  Richad  Carmarden. 
Where  no  hedge  is,  there  the  possession  is  spoyled.  Eccle.  cap  39.  In 
London  the  12""  day  of  Septembre  A".  1572. 

Sixty  pages  of  manuscript  on  vellum,  in  the  original  vellum  binding,  with  the 
arms  of  Queen  Elizabeth,  and  her  cipher  on  the  sides.  The  date  has  been  altered, 
probably  by  the  author,  from  1572  to  1582. 

A  secret  information  to  the  Queen,  which  the  author  begs  her  to  communicate  to 
no  one  else,  lest  his  life  should  fall  in  danger.  It  is  probably  the  only  extant  copy 
of  a  document  important  for  the  history  of  English  finance  and  polity  in  EUzabeth's 
time  ;  and  moreover  interesting  as  a  memorial  of  that  Queen's  library. 

A  final  memorandum,  added  at  the  end  to  excuse  the  staleness  of  the  date,  gives 
the  author's  address  as  "  St.  Mary  Hill  Lane  by  Billingsgate." 

CARRASCO,  Joan  Fernandez.  Relacion  verdadera  de  vn  caso 
que  sucedioen  el  mes  de  lunio  deste  aiio  de  1608.  en  Aldeanuaua  dela 
Vera  con  vna  muger,  a  quien  engano  el  demonio  que  renegasse  de  la 
Virgen  Maria,  y  como  despues  de  algunos  sucessos  se  entro  en  ella,  y 
la  atormento  hasta  que  en  S.  Hieronimo  el  Imperial  de  Yuste  vnos 
religiosos  del  mismo  monesterio  se  le  sacaron  dando  per  serial  de  su 
salida  vn  ochauo,  con  vna  letra  al  fin  de  ellas.  Compuestas  por  loan 
Fernandez  Carrasco  vezino  de  laraez.     Con  liceneia.    \S.  I.  n.  «.]    4to.

CITOYS,  F.  A  True  and  admirable  Historic,  of  a  Maydcn  of 
Confolens,  in  the  Prouince  of  Poitcicrs  :  that  for  the  space  of  three 
yeares  and  more  hath  lined,  and  yet  doth,  without  cither  meate  or 
drinke.  Of  whom,  his  Maiestie  in  person  hath  had  the  view,  and,  (by 
his  commaund)  his  best  and  chifest  Phisitians,  hauc  tryed  all  meancs, 
to  find,  whether  this  fast  &  abstinence  be  by  deceit  or  no.  In  this 
Historic  is  also  discoursed,  whether  a  man  may  fast  many  daycs, 
moneths  or  yceres,  without  receiuing  any  sustenance.  Published  by 
the  Kings  especiall  Priuilege.  At  London,  Printed  by  I.  Roberts,  and

This  volume  has  been  fully  described  by  Mr.  Corser  in  his  "  Collectanea,"  and 
the  whole  of  the  pveliminary  matter  is  reprinted  in  the  privately  printed  volume  of 
"  Prefaces,  &c."  It  is  extremely  curious  on  account  of  the  attack  it  contains  on 
Willobie's  "  Avisa." 

On  D  3  is  an  illustration  of  the  expression  in  Hamlet's  soliloquy  of  "  this  too  too 
solid  flesh.''     Colse  writes  : — 

"  The  virgins  state,  I  must  confess. 
Is  too  too  tedious  for  to  beare." 

From  Mr.  Corser's  collection,  and  doubtless  the  same  copy  which  appears  in  the 
Heber  Catalogue,  part  iv.,  as  that  is  described  as  in  red  morocco  and  with  the  title 
mounted.     It  is  believed  that  no  other  is  known.

[DICTES.  The  dictes  or  sayengis  of  the  philosophres.]  [On  the 
recto  of  folio  73  :]  Here  endeth  the  book  named  the  dictes  or  sayengis  of 
the  philosophres  enprinted  by  vie  ivilliam  Caxton  at  tuestmestre  the  y ere 
of  our  lord  M.  CCCC.Lxxvij.  WhieJie  book  is  late  translated  ont  of  Frenslie 
into  englyssh.  by  the  Noble  and  puissant  lord  Lord  Antone  Erie  of 
Ryuyers Folio,  Black  letter.     R. 

Without  pagination,  catchwords,  or  signatures,  75  leaves. 

Folios  7  and  64  are  inlaid  ;  and  folio  73  is  in  facsimile  ;  otherwise  the  present 
copy  is  remarkably  fine  and  very  tall.  This  copy  when  described  by  Mr.  Blades 
had  three  leaves  in  facsimile,  7,  64  (not  49  as  he  states),  and  73.  The  two  former 
have  since  been  supplied  from  the  copy  sold  in  Mr.  Corser's  sale,  which  was  in  fact 
the  copy  described  by  Mr.  Blades  as  No.  1 1  in  his  list. 

"  This  book  is  remarkable  as  being  the  first  which  bears  a  plain  statement  of  the 
place  and  time  of  its  execution.  It  is  thought  by  some  to  be  really  the  first  book 
printed  in  England.  A  few  of  the  quarto  pieces  may  perhaps  have  preceded  it,  but 
there  is  none  that  can  be  proved  of  earlier  workmanship,  and  if,  as  there  seems  good 
reason  for  supposing,  Caxton  did  not  settle  at  Westminster  before  1476,  he  would 
not  have  had  time  to  produce  much." — Blades.

which  are  inserted  the  Characters  of  a  Pedant,  A  Squire,  A  Beau,  A 
Vertuoso,  A  Poetaster,  A  City-Critick,  &c.  In  a  Letter  to  a  Lady.

In  the  title  of  this  rare  volume  is  a  curious  misprint  of"  Serenissimae  "  which 
has  been  corrected  at  the  time  by  a  small  slip  of  paper  with  I  on  it  being  pasted  over. 
On  the  verso  of  the  title  is  an  address  from  the  printer  to  the  reader,  in  which  he 
says  that  the  book  had  been  put  into  his  hands  to  print  by  Alfonsus  Fonseca,  Arch- 
bishop of  Toledo.  It  is  probable  that  this  treatise  against  the  divorce  had  as  much 
influence  on  the  fate  of  the  author  as  his  denial  of  the  supremacy.

and  in  the  lower  one  she  is  shown  lying  dead  on  a  couch,  the  natives  of  the  country

posite are  verses  in  Dutch,  headed  "  De  laaste  Woorden  van  Me-Joff''  Katharina

GERSON,  JOH.  [Opusculum  tripartitum  de  mandatis  Dei,  con- 
fessione  et  arte  moriendi.]  [Colophon.]  Explicit  opiisculu  Wiptitiim  de 
preceptis  dccalogi  Dc  confcssione  &•  de  arte  Dioricdi  p  cximih  sacre  theo- 
logic  pfcssorc  Magistr  iohancm  de  Jcrsona  aline  univcrsitatis  pisiens' 
Caiiccllarifi.     [Sine  nlld  notd.]     4to. 

Thirty-six  leaves  (the  first  and  last  blank)  with  twenty-seven  lines  on  each  full 
page,  the  text  ending  with  the  colophon  above  cited  on  the  obverse  of  the  thirty- 
fifth  leaf.  It  has  neither  pagination,  signatures,  nor  catchwords.  Panzer  notes 
only  three  editions  of  this  piece,  and  gives  no  exact  particulars  by  which  they  may 
be  distinguished.  Hain  enlarges  the  list  to  five  different  editions.  The  present 
volume  agrees  with  his  No.  7654  as  regards  the  number  of  printed  leaves  (thirty-four 
without  the  blanks)  and  in  having  twenty-seven  lines  to  the  page.  The  spelling  of  the 
colophon  also  accords  exactly,  but  the  division  of  the  lines  is  different.  It  is  proba- 
bly this  edition,  however,  that  he  intended  to  describe,  and  the  error  noted  is  the 
more  easily  understood  as  it  is  not  one  of  the  books  he  had  through  his  own  hands. 
He  gives  the  place  of  printing  as  "  In  valle  Maria;  Virginis  Rhingaviae;"  later  bib- 
liographers, however,  say  nothing  of  this  press.

into  EngHsh,  and  Methodized,  with  the  Addition  of  Notes.  The 
Figures  etched  upon  Copper,  by  Mr.  F.[rancis]  P.[lace.]  York.  Printed 
by  John  White,  for  M.\_artin'\L.\ister.'\     1682.     4to. 

A — T  2  in  fours,  title  on  A  2.     With  fourteen  folding  plates. 

One  hundred  and  fifty  copies  were  privately  printed  for  Dr.  Lister,  the  eminent 
entomologist.  A  copious  account  of  the  book,  the  circumstances  under  which  it  was 
produced,  and  of  Francis  Place  the  artist,  will  be  found  in  "  A  Memoir  of  the  York 
Press,"  by  Robert  Davies,  1S68,  pp.  10S-13.

Title  and  preliminaries,  12  leaves  ;  sigs.  B — li  2  in  fours. 

This  book  is  substantially  the  same  as  the  "  Historia  delle  cose  occorse  nel 
regno  d'Inghilterra,"  printed  at  Venice,  1558  ;  but  in  the  preface  the  author  com- 
plains that  the  Venice  edition  was  printed  without  his  knowledge  or  consent,  lie 
also  tells  us  that  he  came  to  England  in  the  suite  of  Philip  II.,  and  a  great  part  of 
his  relation  has  therefore  a  special  value  as  being  written  from  his  personal  know- 
ledge and  observation.

ANNE  OF  DENMARK,  Queen  of  James  I.  Married  1589, 
d.  1619.  L.  s.,  with  autograph  postscript  of  five  lines.  To  the  Arch- 
duchess of  Austria.    Dated  Lond.  July  29,  1605.    IVii/i  seal  and  silk. 

She  wishes  the  Archduchess  all  happiness  and  prosperity,  and  continues,  "  Et  de 
luy  pouvoir  souhaicter  le  mesme  bonheur  quelle  me  donne  de  mon  accouchement,  afin 
quelle  puisse  veoir  en  sa  lignee,  la  benediction  et  contentement  que  selon  son  voeu, 
jespereen  lamienne"  [she  had  thirteen  children].  Her  postscript  apologizes  for  not 
having  written  with  her  own  hand,  "  car  j'estois  par  le  temps  e  par  mes  affaires  fort 
pressde."

CAMPBELL,  Thomas,  b.  1777,  d.  1844.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to 
Francis  Freeling,  Esq. 

"  After  leaving  Liverpool,  I  have  engaged  to  stop  a  month  to  lecture  at  the  Royal 
Institution  of  Birmingham.  I  know  not  if  this  business  of  lecturing  be  the  most 
honourable  in  the  world.  Perhaps  an  itinerant  lecturer  conveys  no  great  idea  of 
respectability,  but  it  is  a  very  pleasant  employment." 

CANOVA,  Antonio,  b.  1757,  d.  1802.  A.  L.  s.  Dated  "  Rome 
29  Janvier,  1809,"  in  French. 

CASAS,  Bartolomeo  de  las.  Bishop  of  Chiapa.  b.  1474,  d.  1 566. 
A.  L.  s.  In  Spanish.  Addressed  to  the  President  and  Council  {oydores). 
Dated  22  Octob.  1545. 

Las  Casas  is  chiefly  known  for  the  efforts  he  made  in  favour  of  the  aborigines  of 
America,  and  for  their  protection  against  the  oppression  of  his  own  countrymen.  In 
this  long  and  most  interesting  letter,  he  prays — 

"  That  your  Lordships  will  effectually  remedy  the  oppressions  which  are  fallen 
upon  the  native  Indian  flock  throughout  my  bishopric,  especially  the  excessive 
tribute  demanded  by,  and  extortion  of,  the  Spaniards,  who  treat  the  people  as  wild 
beasts,  by  night  and  by  day,  and  keep  them,  though  they  are  free,  in  slavery,  and 
harass  them  in  divers  ways,  contrary  to  divine  law  and  natural  reason  ;  and  that 


i682  C  ATI  NAT— CHARLES  I. 

you  will  arrest  the  diminution  and  total  destruction  (acabamientd)  of  the  entire  body 
of  inhabitants,  who  are  perishing  not  only  in  body  but  in  soul,  because,  since  the 
Spaniards  keep  them  always  in  bondage,  the  Religious  cannot  see  them  in  order  to 
preach  to  them  the  law  of  God,  and  instruct  them.  And  to  this  end  it  is  necessary 
that  your  lordships  should  give  them  that  liberty  which  his  Majesty  has  conferred 
upon  them  by  his  late  ordinances  granted  to  them  as  to  his  free  subjects." 

There  is  also  in  the  collection  a  long  letter  of  Las  Casas,  in  Latin,  in  the  hand- 
writing of  a  secretar}^,  but  signed  by  himself,  which  likewise  relates  to  the  treat- 
ment of  the  Indians  by  the  Spaniards. 

CATHERINE  DE  MEDICIS,  Queen  of  Henri  II.  of  France. 
b.  1 5 19,  d.  1589.  A.  L.  s.  Superscription  to  the  King,  her  son 
(Henri  III.),  from  Souterane,  June  6,  1569. 

She  has  heard  of  the  death  of  AUuy^,  the  loss  of  whom  she  very  much  laments,  and 
earnestly  recommends  his  wife  to  the  King  ;  warning  him  that  a  battle  is  imminent, 
and  the  enemy  at  half  a  league  from  her  residence.

on  the  proceedings  of  the  Army,  addressed  to  the  King.  Dated  "  Au 
Camp  de  Marsaghe  ce  7  d'Octobre  1693." 

The  report  occupies  thirteen  pages,  nearly  eight  of  which  are  in  the  hand  of 
Catinat ;  the  remainder  in  that  of  a  secretary.

Dec,  1745."

Requesting  him  to  hasten  the  negotiations  of  a  marriage,  which  should  be  for 
him  a  very  pleasant  thing.    La  Mole  was  afterwards  beheaded.

"  I  have  nothing  of  that  secresie  at  this  tyme  to  wryte  unto  you  -vi^  does  much 
require  the  use  of  a  cyfer."  He  begs  him  to  assure  his  friends  that  he  shall  be  "as  faith-

you  of  w""  on  my  word  you  are  in  the  first  ranke."  A  small  part  is  in  cypher,  which

CHARLES  II.,  King  of  England,  b.  1630,  d.  1685.  Aut.  s.  to

CLARENDON,  Sir  Edward  Hyde,  Earl  of.  b.  1608,  d.  1674. 
A.  L.  s.    Dated  "  Hague,  this  27  Wensday  9  at  night." 

Concerning  the  Order  for  wearing  the  Standard  on  board  the  Admiral.  Prince 
Charles  informs  Lord  Ormond  what  arts  are  used  to  bring  the  fleet  to  nothing  ; 
acknowledges  all  that  is  left  of  it  to  be  due  to  Prince  Rupert's  industry  and  dexterity. 
Evil  affected  persons  labour  a  misunderstanding  between  Prince  Rupert  and  Lord 
Ormond,  &c. 

CLIVE,  Catherine.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to  David  Garrick. 
Endorsed  by  Garrick,  "  My  Pivy,  excellent !  "  Dated  Twickenham, 
Jan.  23,  1776. 

"  Is  it  really  true,  that  you  have  put  an  end  to  the  glory  of  Drury  Lane  Theatre ; 
if  it  is  so;  let  me  congratulate  dear  Mr.  and  Mrs.  Garrick  on  their  approaching 
happiness.  I  know  what  it  will  be  ;  you  cannot  yet  have  an  idea  what  it  will  be  ; 
but  if  you  should  still  be  so  wicked  not  to  be  satisfied  with  that  unbounded,  uncom- 
mon degree  of  fame  you  have  received  as  an  actor  ;  and  which  no  other  actor  ever 
did  receive — nor  no  other  actor  ever  can  receive  ;  I  say  if  you  should  still  long  to  be 
dipping  your  fingers  in  their  Theatrical  pudding  (now  without  plumbs)  you  will  be 
no  Garrick  for  the/ny.  In  the  height  of  the  publick  admiration  for  you,  when  you 
were  never  mentioned  with  any  other  appilation,  but  the  Garrick,  the  charming  man, 
the  fine  fellow  ;  the  delightful  creature ; — both  by  Men  and  Ladies.  When  they  were 
admiring  every  thing  you  did,  and  every  thing  you  scribbled  ;  at  the  very  time  /  the 
pivy,  was  aliveing  witness,  that  they  did  not  know,  nor  could  they  know,  nor  could 
they  be  sensible,  of  half  your  perfections.  I  have  seen  you  with  your  Magical 
hammer  in  your  hand,  endeverin<;  to  beat  your  ideas  into  the  heads  of  creatures 
who  had  none  of  their  own  ;  I  have  seen  you  with  lamb  like  patience  endevering 
to  make  them  comprehend  you  ;  and  I  have  seen  you  when  that  could  not  be  done, 
I  have  seen  your  lamb  turn  into  a  lion,  by  this  your  great  labour  and  pains  the  pub- 
lick  was  entertained,  they  thought  they  all  acted  very  fine,  they  did  not  see  you 
pull  the  wires."  &c.  &c. 

"  What  a  strange  jumble  of  people  they  have  put  in  the  papers,  as  the  purchasers 
of  the  patent ;  1  thought  I  should  have  died  with  laughing  when  I  saw  a  Man  Mid- 
wife amongst  them  ;  I  suppose  they  have  taken  him  in  to  prevent  miscarriages  ;  I 
have  some  opinion  of  Mr.  Sheridan  as  I  hear  every  body  say  he  is  very  sensible  ; 

then  he  has  a  divine  wife,  and  I  loved  his  mother  dearly ' '  Now  let  me  say  one 

word  about  my  poor  unfortunate  friend  Miss  Pope.  I  know  how  much  she  dis- 
obliged you — .  But  by  this  time  I  hope  you  have  forgot  your  resentment,  and 
will  look  upon  her  late  behaviour  as  having  been  taken  with  a  dreadful  fit  of 
vanity,  which  for  that  time  took  her  senses  from  her,  and  having  been  tutored  by 
an  affected  beast  who  helped  to  turn  her  head,  but  pray  recollect  her  in  the  other 
light,  a  faithful  creature  to  you,  on  whom  you  can  always  depend,  certainly  a  good 
actress,  amiable  in  her  character,  both  in  her  being  a  very  modest  woman,  and  very 
good  to  her  family.'  '  Now  my  dear  Mr.  Garrick  I  hope  it  is  not  yet  too  late  to  rein- 
state her  before  you  quit  your  affairs  there,  I  beg  it,  I  intreat  it.'"  &c.

He  accepts  a  generous  offer  that  has  been  made  by  some  one  unknown  to  him  to

Autograph  Sonnet,  "  To  a  lady,"  beginning,  "If  grief  dismiss 

me  not  to  them  that  rest."

Respecting  the  rumours  spread  about  concerning  the  warlike  preparations  by  sea 
and  land  of  the  Venetians,  who  think  of  nothing  else  e.xcept  the  defence  of  their

teresting letter  respecting  one  of  her  uncle's  dramas.  Dated  "  24 
Aoust."     [1755.] 

DIANA  OF  POICTIERS.  A  MS.  on  19  leaves  of  paper;  17  of 
which  are  occupied  with  the  daily  household  accounts  of  this  cele- 
brated bibliophile  for  the  month  of  August,  1565.  At  the  foot  of  each 
page  is  the  signature  of  her  steward,  and  on  the  last  leaf  is  the  rare 
autograph  signature,  "  Dianne  de  Poytiers."     Small  folio. 

Accompanying  the  docmnent  is  a  brilliant  impression  of  the  rare  private  plate 
of  the  portrait  of  Diana  from  the  original  picture  in  the  possession  of  Earl  Spencer.

DRYDEN,  John.  b.  163  i,  d.  1700.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to  Mrs. 
Stewart  at  Cotterstock.     Dated  "Candlemass-day,  1698." 

"  Old  men  are  not  so  insensible  of  beauty  as  it  may  be  you  young  Ladies  think ; 
for  my  own  part  I  must  needs  acknowledge  that  your  fair  eyes  had  made  me  your 

slave  before  I  received  your  fine  presents I  pass  my  time  sometimes  with 

Ovid,  and  sometimes  with  our  Old  English  Poet  Chaucer,  translateing  such  stories 
as  best  please  my  fancy."

Court,  2  Nov.,  1584. 

EDWARD  IV.,  King  of  England,  and  his  brother  Edmund. 
L.    on    paper,    signed    by   them.      i    page   4to.      Dated  "  Londonie,

i686  ELIZABETH. 

Die  X  Decembris,  1460."  Letter  of  "cr^ance"  for  Antony  de  la 
Tour,  whom  they  send  to  the  Duke  of  Milan  and  the  Pope  to  im- 
part to  them  their  intentions  and  hopes.  They  crave  the  interest  of 
the  Holy  Father  that  their  projects  may  succeed  (they  contemplate 
nothing  less  than  the  dethronement  of  Henry  VI.),  and  they  rely  upon 
the  Lord  and  the  goodness  of  their  cause.  This  valuable  letter,  be- 
yond its  excessive  rarity,  is  of  such  great  historical  importance  that  it 
is  given  entire. 

"  lUustrissime  Princeps  et  Excellentissime  Domine,  post  officiosas  salutem.  Ex 
relatione  spectabilis  et  generosi  viri  Domini  Antonii  de  Turri,  Regie  Majestatis 
nostre  nuntii  et  Armigeri  intelleximus  de  honorificentia  et  gratitudine  illi  exhibita 
pro  reuerentia  Sacre  Regie  Majestatis  et  etiam  respectu  nostro  :  ac  etiam  de  fauore 
sibi  prestito  in  Romana  curia  apud  Pontificem  maximum  pro  nostris  uotis  et 
honestis  desideriis  impetrandis,  Et  de  singular!  caritate  et  beneuolentia  quam  ad 
nos  et  Statum  nostrum  Excellentia  Vestra  gerit,  pro  quibus  rebus  prefate  Excel- 
lentie  Vestre  cumulatissime  regratiamur.  Et  quoniam  multa  sunt  nobis  cordi  que 
D.  V.  jam  cupimus  reuelari  ideo  eundem  Dominum  Antonium  duximus  remitten- 
dum  ad  Serenissimum  Dominum  Nostrum  Papam  et  Vestram  Excellentiam  de 
intentione  ac  desideriis  nostris  plenissime  informatum.  Quo  circa  rogamus  Excel- 
lentiam Vestram  Excellentiam  ut  ilium  more  suo  solito  gratiose  suscipiat  et  audiat 
ac  plenam  illi  fidem  adhibeat  et  per  eundem  respondere  dignetur.  Speramus  in 
Domino  et  in  virtute  Reverendissimi  Domini  legati  apostolici,  apud  nos  existentis, 
cujus  status  cum  nostris  fortunis  est  conjunctus  quod  rerum  nostrarum  successus 
erit  gloriosus  sicut  idem  Dominus  Antonius  lator  presentium  latius  explicebit. 
Valeat  Excellentia  Vestra  ad  cujus  bene  placita  parati  sumus  :  Datum  Londonie 
die  X  Decembris  MccccLx.  Excellentie  Vestre  amicissimi  et  consanguinei  Eduardus 
Marchie  et  Edmundus  Ruthulandie  comites,  filii  illustrissimi  Principis  Ricardi,  veri, 
justi  et  legitimi  heredis  Regnoioim  Anglie  et  Francie  ac  Dominii  Hybernie,  ducis 
Eboracii,  &c.  "  E.  March(ie).  "E.  Rutland(ie)." 

The  latter  was  murdered  by  Lord  Clifford  at  the  battle  of  Wakefield,  on  the 
24th  of  December,  1460,  when  thirteen  years  of  age,  and  fourteen  days  after  his 
having  signed  the  present  paper. 

ELIZABETH,  Queen  of  England,  b.  1533,  d.  1603.  A.  L.  s. 
In  French.  Addressed  to  Henry  IV.  of  France.  With  seals  and  silk. 
No  date. 

Thanks  him  for  the  portrait  he  has  sent,  but  says  that  the  friendship  of  which 
his  messenger  assures  her  is  even  more  valuable  to  her,  who  rejoices  in  seeing 
that  "  a  good  tree  continues  to  bear  good  fruit  ....  defies  the  insinuations  of 
some  third  party  ;  has  certain  witnesses  and  proofs  of  what  she  states,  but  will  be 
cautious  of  producing  them,  for  she  would  rather  be  silent  and  sin  in  the  good  than 
in  the  bad  cause;"  speaks  of  the  agent  of  this  mysterious  third  party  as  Vclasco, 
whom  she  is  indignant  at  having  to  receive  as  ambassador ;  ends  with  professions 
of  diligence  in  his  service.

dressed to  the  Earl  of  Arlington.     Dublin  Castle,  March  17,  1673-4. 

In  1683  the  Earl  was  committed  to  the  Tower  for  his  complicity  in  Monmouth's

ESTE,  Leonora  d'.     L.  .s.     Dated  Mar.  23,  1574. 

It  was  on  account  of  his  love  for  this  lady  that  Torquato  Tasso  was  imprisoned 
in  a  madhouse  for  seven  years. 

ESTREES,  Gabrielle  d'.  Mistress  of  Henri  IV.  b.  1571,  d. 
1599.  Deed  of  Sale,  signed  by  her  at  the  end,  and  three  times  in  the 
margins.     8  pages  folio.     1599. 

Relative  to  the  purchase  by  her  of  the  manor  and  lands  of  Saint  Jehan  des 
deux  Jumeaux  and  Montretout.  She  is  styled  in  the  deed  Duchesse  de  Beaufort, 
pairesse  de  France,  Marquise  de  Monceaux  et  dame  de  Crizy. 

ETTY,  William,  b.  1787,  d.  1849.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to  M. 
L.  Watson,  Esq.,  containing  a  copy  of  the  register  of  the  birth  of 
Flaxman,  the  sculptor.    Dated  June  17,  1847. 

EVELYN,  John.  b.  1620,  d.  1706.  A.  L.  s.  For  Mr.  Cooper. 
Dated  21  June,  i/oi. 

"  Hearing  Sir  that  you  are  causing  the  tables  of  V'eines,  Arterys  &c.  which  I 
some  yeares  since  brought  out  of  Italy,  to  be  accurately  delineated  &  ingraven  as 
more  correct  than  any  yet  extant  or  to  be  found  among  the  Figures  in  Books  of 
Anatomy;  and  desirous  to  understand  how  they  came  to  my  hands,  I  send  you 
this  little  History  of  it  for  your  satisfaction."  The  letter  describes  how  he  first  saw 
these  tables  and  purchased  them  at  Padua,  that  he  lent  them  to  Dr.  Scarbrogh  at 
the  Colledge  [of  Physicians]  for  a  little  while,  "who  read  in  them,"  and  concludes, 
"his  Ma""  Charles  the  ll"""  hearing  of  them  was  pleased  to  come  and  see  them 
himselfe  with  great  satisfaction.  The  R:  Society  for  the  promotion  of  experimental 
and  natural  knowledge  being  a  little  after  instituted  by  that  Prince  and  meeting  at 
Gresham-Colledge  ;  I  made  a  present  of  the  Tables  to  the  Repositary."

Attesting  that  his  ambassador  at  Madrid  has  given  full  satisfaction  to  the

FLAXMAN,  John.  b.  1754,  d.  1826.  A.  L.  s.  To  Mr.  Huttner. 
Dated  29  Oct.,  1804. 

A  long  and  interesting  letter  about  his  designs  for  Homer's  Iliad. 

FOOTE,  Samuel,  b.  1721,  d.  1777.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to 
Garrick.     Dated  Dublin,  Dec.  31st. 

Had  it  not  been  for  Jewel  he  would  last  night  have  been  reduced  to  ashes  by 
reading  in  bed.  "  The  bed  was  burnt,  and  poor  Jewel's  hands  most  miserably 
scorched  ;  so  you  see,  my  dear  Sir,  no  man  can  foresee  the  great  ends  for  which 
he  was  born  ;  Macklin,  though  a  blockhead  in  his  manhood  and  youth,  turns 
out  a  wit  and  an  orator  on  the  brink  of  the  grave,  and  Foote,  never  very  re- 
markable for  his  personal  graces,  in  the  decline  of  his  life  was  very  near  becoming 
a  Toast."     Giving  news  of  several  people  in  Dublin,  &c. 

FRANCIS  I.,  King  of  France,  b.  1494,  d.  1547.  L.  s.  With 
superscription  to  the  Duchess  Dowager  of  Savoy,  in  favour  of  his 
uncle,  the  bastard  of  Savoy.  Amboise,  January  10,  1 5 17,  counter- 
signed Deneufville. 

FRANCIS  II.,  King  of  France,  b.  1544,  d.  1560.  A.  L.  s.  Super- 
scription to  "Mon  Oncle  le  Roy  de  Navarre,"  with  seal. 

He  has  seen  by  the  letters  of  his  uncle  how  the  latter  was  displeased  with  the 
calumnies  coming  from  England.  He  is  determined  to  send  to  Scotland  sufficient 
forces  to  crush  the  rebellion.

FREDERIC  THE  GREAT,  King  of  Prussia,  b.  1712,  d.  1786.

GALILEO  DE'  GALILEL     b.  1564,  d.  1642.    A.  L.  s.    Dated 
from  Arcetri,  Feb.  21,  1653. 

An  extremely  interesting  letter  addressed  to  one  of  his  protectors,  in  which  he 
relates  the  persecutions  he  is  subjected  to  by  his  enemies,  who,  under  the  mask 
of  religion,  make  war  against  him,  besiege  him,  destroy  his  books,  and  forbid  him 
to  reprint  them. 

GARRICK,  David,    b.  1717,  d.  1779.   A.  L.s.   To  Mrs.  Abington, 
dated  Saturday,  9  o'clock. 

"  I  hope,  when  all  the  rest  have  agreed  to  do  their  parts,  that  you  will  not  be  want- 
ing this  evening.  I  beg  you,  for  your  own  sake,  my  sake  and  the  credit  of  the  house  : 
nay,  I'll  go  farther,  I  will  do  every  thing  1  can  for  you  in  my  power ;  do  be  in  a  good 
humour  and  meet  me  at  the  house  this  morning." 

GENLIS,  Madame  de.  b.  1746,  d.  1830.  A  tale,  entitled  "  Les 
Souvenirs  de  Felicie  L  .  .  .  .",  occupying  thirteen  pages. 

A.  L.    Addressed,  "  A  Monsieur  de  S'.  Lambert."    Without 


signature. 

GIBBON,  Edward,    b.  1737,  d.  1794.    A.  L.  s.    Dated  June  24, 

1793- 

Expresses  his  inability  to  serve  the  person  to  whom  the  letter  is  addressed 
[Gillies]  in  his  legitimate  claim  to  the  title  or  office  of  historiographer  of  his  native 
country,  and  strongly  declares  his  contempt  for  the  then  wild  and  mischievous 
system  of  democracy. 

GOETHE,  J.  W.  VON.  b.  1749,  d.  1832.  A.  L.  s.  To  Professor 
Oeser,  dated  "Frankfurt,  14  Feb.,  1769." 

A  letter  written  after  he  had  been  obliged,  through  serious  illness,  to  leave  the 
University  of  Leipsic,  and  had  gone  home  to  await  his  recovery.  Discusses  in  a 
playful  manner  the  chances  of  his  returning  to  Leipsic,  &c. 

Letters  written  by  Goethe  in  his  youth  are  excessively  scarce.  This  one  has 
never  been  printed. 

GOLDONI,  Carlo,  b.  1707,  d.  1793.  A.  L.  s.  Dated  10  October, 
1761. 

GOLDSMITH,  O.  b.  172S,  d.  1774.  A.  L.  s.  To  Sir  J.  Reynolds. 
Dated  Paris,  29  July. 

A  long  and  curious  letter,  giving  a  doleful  account  of  his  travels  on  the  conti- 


1690  GRAY— HENRY   VII. 

nent ;  he  is  anxious  to  return,  and  begs  for  news  from  home  ;  wishes  to  hear  from 
Sir  Joshua  himself,  Johnson,  Burke,  Dyer,  Chamier,  and  every  one  of  the  Club  ; 
also  about  Miss  Reynolds,  Bickerstaff,  &c. 

GRAY,  Thomas,  b.  1716,  d.  1771.  A.  L.  s.  To  Mr.  Brown,  Pre- 
sident of  Pembroke  Hall,  Cambridge.    Oct.  25,  1760. 

"  The  King  is  dead  ;  he  rose  this  morning  about  six  (his  usual  early  hour),  in 
perfect  health,  and  had  his  chocolate.  Between  seven  and  eight,  an  unaccountable 
noise  was  heard  in  his  chamber,  they  ran  in,  and  found  him  lying  on  the  floor.  He 
was  directly  blooded,  and  a  few  drops  came  from  him,  but  he  instantly  expired. 
This  event  happens  at  an  unlucky  time,  but,  I  should  think,  will  make  little  altera- 
tion in  publick  matters." 

With  Gray's  usual  signature,  "  Yours,  T.  G." 

GUIDO  RENI.  b.  1575,  d.  1642.  A.  L.  s.  Bologna,  Aug.  23, 
1625. 

Relative  to  a  picture  in  Rome  over  the  Shrine  of  the  Pilgrims'  Church  of  the 
Trinity,  at  Ponte  Sisto.

7  full  pages  folio.  To  Nicolas  de  Neufville,  Seigneur  de  Villeroy,

State  affairs. 

HENRY  IV.,  King  of  France,  b.  1553,  d.  1610.  A.  L.  s.  Ad- 
dressed to  "Mon  Cousyne,  Mens'',  le  Vycomte  de  Turenne." 

He  has  heard  from  Boyrnart  of  the  division  between  him  and  Marsolan.  It 
seems  to  him  it  would  be  better  to  put  Boyrnart  in  Sainte  Baseylle  with  thirty  men 
for  the  defence  of  this  place,  which  is  threatened,  and  in  danger  on  account  of  the 
disaffection  of  the  inhabitants.  The  company  of  De  Vivans  is  to  be  maintained 
with  the  contributions  of  the  Garonne. 

HENRIETTA  MARIA,  Queen  of  Charles  I.  b.  1609,  d.  1669.

due  d'Orleans."  Complimentary  on  his  marriage.    With  seals  and  floss

HOGARTH,  W.  b.  1697,  d.  1764.  A.L.s.  Addressed  "A  Monsieur 
Reiftstein,  Governeur  de  Pages."    London,  April  18,  1757. 

Answer  to  a  letter  from  J.  F.  Reiffstein,  accepting  an  invitation  to  become  a 
member  of  the  Imperial  Academy  of  Augsburg  for  the  study  and  improvement  of 
Arts  and  Letters.  Reiffstein's  letter  is  also  in  the  collection.  At  the  end  of  it  he 
says,  "  My  address  is  Governour  of  the  Pages." 

HUMBOLDT.  Alex.  von.  b.  1769,  d.  1S59.  A.  L.  s.  Dated 
Paris,  17  Sept.,  1838. 

HUME,  David,  b.  171 1,  d.  1776.  A.L.s.  London,  25  May, 
1767. 

A  long  and  interesting  letter  to  a  friend  in  Paris. 

"  Mr.  Turgot  expresses  his  surprise  that  I  should  have  consented  to  enter  into 
public  affairs,  while  he  wishes  to  retire  from  them.  It  will  never  be  in  my  purpose, 
and,  I  believe,  still  less  in  my  power,  to  enter  into  the  prejudices  entertained  by  the 
vulgar  part  of  my  countrymen,  and  to  feel  an  antipathy  against  the  French  nation. 
I  know  them  too  well  to  be  capable  of  such  a  sentiment.  Happily  both  nations  are 
at  present  seriously  disposed  to  cultivate  peace  with  each  other,"  &c.  &c.

JOHNSON,  Dr.  Samuel,  b.  1709,  d.  1784.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed 
to  David  Garrick,  requesting  places  to  be  reserved  for  "any  night  on 
which  you  intend  to  appear,  before  Friday." 

A.  L.  s.    To  the  Rev.  Doctor  Taylor  in  Ashbourne.    London, 

Dec.  22,  1774. 

Talks  about  Charles  Congreve,  "  the  greatest  pleasure  that  I  have  had  from  him 
is  to  find  him  firm  and  orthodox  ;  yet  he  consorts  with  Wesley." 

A.  L.  s.    To  Mr.  Ryland.    Dated  Sept.  21,  1776. 

"  I  have  procured  this  play  to  be  read  by  Mrs.  Thrale,  who  declares  that  no  play 
was  ever  more  nicely  pruned  from  the  objection  of  indelicacy.  If  it  can  be  got  upon 
the  stage,  it  will,  I  think,  succeed,"  &c. 

A.  L.  s.    Addressed  to  the  Rev.  Dr.  Taylor.    Dated  June  23, 

1784. 

Speaks  of  his  illness  and  various  matters.  "  Boswell  has  a  great  mind  to  draw 
me  to  Lichfield,  and  as  I  love  to  travel  with  him,  I  have  a  mind  to  be  drawn,"  and 
other  observations  of  interest.

ture to  a  note.    Dated,  "  Malmaison,  le  5  Mai,  18 13."
