"  humyl  tya  de  vuestra  magestad 

"  Katherina." 
Addressed: — "  Al  muy  alto  y  muy  poderoso  sefior 
el  Emperador  y  rey  my  sobr>'[no]." 

Endorsed: — "  A  su  magestad 

De  la  reyna  de  Inglaterra,  viij  de 
Ebrero,  1534." 

Translation. 

"  Most  high  and  most  powerful  Lord :  it  is  but  a  little  time  since  I  wrote  to  your 
Majesty  stating  my  opinion  upon  the  articles  which  the  ambassador  of  the  king,  my 
Lord  and  husband,  presented  to  Your  Majesty;  after  which  the  ambassador,'  who 
resides  here,  showed  to  me  other  articles,  which  His  Holiness  sent,  by  his  nuncio, 
to  Your  Highness  upon  the  same  affair,  to  which  I  replied  as  well  as  appeared  to  me 
would  suit  the  good  of  this  [my]  cause,  avoiding  the  delay  which  the  said  articles 
necessarily  bring  with  them,  as  I  believe  the  said  ambassador  will  [by  this  time]  have 
notified  to  Your  Majesty.  And,  of  a  truth,  considering  what  has,  and  is  taking  place 
respecting  the  said  delay,  and  perceiving  that  neither  the  daily  offences  made  to  God 
here — which  go  on  on  the  increase — nor  my  own  continual  complaints,  nor  the 
power  of  Your  Highness,  are  sufficient  to  induce  His  Holiness  to  do  [me]  justice — • 
for  I  ask  nothing  else  of  him — I  was  almost  determined  not  to  trouble  Your  Majesty 
any  more  with  my  letters,  but  entrust  the  declaration  of  the  truth  of  my  Justice  to 
God,  and  accept  this  [state  of  things]  as  a  remedy  for  my  troubles  and  a  consola- 
tion for  my  hfe.  Yet  it  seemed  to  me  as  if  in  so  doing  I  should  be  tempting  God, 
especially  when  I  see  that  the  sin,  wherein  the  king,  my  Lord,  lives,  brings  about 
other  sins  every  hour,  as  it  would  appear  from  the  attempt  they  have  lately  made, 
without  fear  of  God,  and  to  the  great  scandal  of  all  Christendom,  against  the  autho- 
rity of  the  Holy  See,  as  Your  Majesty  must  have  heard  at  length  from  other  persons. 
I  think  therefore  that  I  ought  to  return  to  my  own  importunities,  for  I  do  not  occupy 
myself  in  any  other  thing  except  in  saving  my  soul  in  this  business,  and  certifying 
Your  Highness  that  I  would  not,  for  any  thing  in  this  world,  have  failed  to  acquaint 
His  Holiness,  and  your  Majesty,  and  all  persons,  capable  of  applying  a  remedy  to 
the  evils,  which  I  am  witnessing,  and  which,  though  ignorant  in  these  matters,  I 
have  always  had  before  my  eyes.  Having,  however,  done  what  I  could,  my  con- 
science remains  with  some  consolation  in  spite  of  all  the  bad  example  [they  give], 
and  all  the  rest  that  they  do  and  signify  that  they  will  do.  Your  Majesty  must  know 
that  the  remedy  lies  with  HisHohnessin  putting  a  termination  and  end  to  this  affair, 
and  not  yielding  in  the  prosecution  of  it,  in  accordance  with  the  requirements  of 
Justice,  telling  those  who  converse  with  him  upon  the  matter  that  this  is  his  volun- 
tary determination.  Such  being  the  only  remedy,  for  the  love  of  the  Passion  of 
God  I  entreat  Your  Majesty  to  be  pleased  to  take  action  in  it,  for  all  Christendom 
awaits  it,  and  you  are  obliged  as  a  Christian  prince  and  protector  of  our  faith, 
to  take  the  matter  in  hand  without  any  other  consideration  but  that  of  God's 
service  and  the  tranquillity  of  all  Christendom.  All  other  considerations,  even  that 
of  my  own  person  and  my  daughter's,  ought  to  be  put  aside,  unless  it  were  for  the 
sake  of  your  accomplishing  such  an  act  of  mercy,  as  to  deliver  us  from  the  prison 
in  which  we  are,  like  the  the  most  miserable  creatures  in  the  world.  Indeed,  there 
is  no  need  for  my  relating  to  Your  Highness  the  sufferings  that  1  and  my  daughter 

'  Eustace  Chapuys,  Imperial  ambassador  in  London  since  1529. 


KATHERINE    OF   ARRAGON.  1695 

undergo,  as  well  in  the  treatment  of  our  lives,  ns  in  the  surprises  and  affronts  whirh 
every  day  the  King's  Council  puts  upon  us,  for  our  troubles  are  matters  of  universal 
notoriety;  and,  indeed,  if  we  did  not  fortify  ourselves  with  God's  help,  and  by  look- 
ing upon  our  sufferings  as  undergone  for  His  sake  ;  if  the  all  Merciful  did  not  help 
us  throughout,  the  burden  would  be  insupportable.  However,  as  I  consider  that  my 
purgatory  and  my  daughter's  is  in  this  world,  we  are  both  doing  what  we  can  in  de- 
fence of  our  rights.  As  to  me.  Your  Highness  may  be  sure  that  I  shall  not  fail  [in 
this  task]  till  death,  as  otherwise  I  should  imperil  my  soul,  and  I  hope  to  God  the 
Princess  will  do  the  same,  as  a  good  daughter  should  do.  Further  matters  "i'our 
Majesty  shall  know  from  your  ambassador,  and  yet  I  think  he  will  be  unable  to  ac- 
quaint you  with  the  whole  of  the  mischief  that  is  taking  place  [here].  Above  all  I 
beseech  of  Your  Highness  brevity  and  despatch  in  the  affair,  for  to  lose  a  day  at 
this  juncture  is  worse  than  to  have  lost  hitherto  a  year,  as  I  have  [already]  signified 
to  Your  Majesty  in  my  last  letter,  to  which  I  refer  myself.  Just  as  I  was  writing 
this  Your  Majesty's  ambassador  [Chapuys]  sent  to  tell  me  that  in  his  opinion  it  is 
not  advisable  to  send  any  one  to  this  parliament  ;  and  also  how  he  has  sent  a  special 
messenger  to  His  Holiness  to  beg  with  all  urgency  and  brevity  for  a  definitive  sen- 
tence. Of  the  first  measure  [proposed  by  the  ambassador]  I  fully  approve,  for  un- 
less the  messenger  could  return  with  the  determination  and  end  of  the  affair,  it  would 
be  of  very  little  use,  considering  the  present  state  of  things  here.  With  regard  to  the 
man  whom  Your  Majesty  has  sent  I  kiss  the  hands  of  Your  Majesty,  for  of  a  truth, 
besides  the  good  which  his  coming  is  likely  to  do,  it  is  high  time  for  Your  Highness 
to  show  yourself  in  other  bearing  than  you  have  hitherto  shown  yourself,  if  it  were 
only  to  stand  on  good  terms  with  the  inhabitants  of  this  kingdom,  who  think  that  the 
affair  has  come  to  this  pass  through  your  Majesty  not  having  shown  yourself  as  you 
should  have  done.  For  my  own  part  I  entertain  some  scruples  that  Your  Highness 
might  have  done  better,  especially  since  there  has  been  a  good  opportunity  towards 
persuading  His  Holiness  to  do  [me]  Justice,  wherewith  the  whole  might  have  been 
brought  to  an  end.  However,  with  regard  to  this  point  each  one's  conscience  shall 
be  his  Judge,  and  God,  who  knows  the  truth,  is  enough  for  me  who  have,  as  I  de- 
clare, fully  disburdened  mine.  I  humbly  beseech  your  Majesty,  if  the  charity  hitherto 
shown  to  me  and  to  my  daughter  has  been  somewhat  cold  that  it  may  be  the  warmer 
in  future,  for  even  were  we  not  both  united  in  blood  and  relationship  to  each  other, 
it  would  be  Your  Majesty's  duty  to  help  persons  so  much  oppressed  and  in  such 
necessity  [as  we  are].  Your  Majesty  indeed  is  bound  to  help  us  to  bear  the  cross 
which  we  have  upon  our  shoulders  for  the  sake  of  truth,  otherwise  there  is  no  hope 
in  this  world,  for  the  perpetration  of  such  grave  and  multifarious  sins  is  sure  to  bring 
a  very  severe  punishment,  from  which  I  trust  God  will  protect  Your  Majesty,  and 
give  you  such  a  life  and  increase  of  estate  as  1  desire  you  should  have,  beseeching 
your  Majesty  to  forgive  my  scribbling,  for  as  my  heart  is  troubled  so  is  my  hand  stiff, 
and  1  have  not  written  [this]  my  letter  as  I  ought  to  have  done.  At  Buxton  (?)  the 
viij  of  February. 

"  Your  Majesty's  humble  aunt 

"  Katherin.\." 

Addressed :—"  To  the  most  High  and  powerful  lord,  the  Emperor  and  King  my 
nephew." 

Endorsed:—"  To  His  Majesty.    From  the  Queen  of  England,  8  Feb.,  1534-"

1698        MARIE    DE    MEDICIS—MARY    TUDOR. 

MARIE  DE  MEDICIS,  Queen  of  France,  second  wife  of  Henri 
IV.  b.  1573,  d.  1642.  A.  L.  s.  Superscription,  "Au  Roy  Mon- 
sieur men  fils."    With  seals  and  silk  attached. 

An  affectionate  letter,  thanking  him  for  his  assurance  of  his  belief  in  her  inno- 
cence of  false  reports  made  against  her  as  to  practices  against  the  interests  of 
his  service — begging  him  to  clear  up  the  affair  by  some  demonstration  so  public 
as  to  manifest  the  advantage  the  truth  gives  her  to  all  the  world  as  much  as  to 
himself — complains  of  her  absence  from  him,  and  asks  earnestly  for  permission  to 
return. 

There  is  in  the  collection  another  letter,  the  signature  only  being  autograph, 
dated  "A  Compiegne  le  x<'  May  1624." 

MARLBOROUGH,  John,  Duke  of.  b.  1650,  d.  1716.  A.  L.  s. 
Dated  from  the  Camp  near  Maestricht. 

A  long  letter  on  military  exploits  and  battles  in  which  he  was  then  engaged,  in 
full  hope  of  success. 

MARLBOROUGH,  S.,  Duchess  OF.  b.  1660,  d.  1744.  L.s.  Ad- 
dressed to  Mr.  Cooke,  April  6,  1742. 

Speaks  of  having  vindicated  herself  by  incontestable  proof  from  the  vile  asper- 
sions thrown  upon  her  by  the  rage  of  parties.  "  This  I  have  done  very  clearly,  and 
I  don't  care  what  Fools  or  Mad  People  say  of  me,  which  will  always  be  a  great 
majority.  And  I  am  as  little  concerned  at  what  Kings  may  write — For  I  am  very 
sure  that  I  shall  never  more  have  any  thing  to  do  with  Kings  or  Ministers,  for  which 
I  thank  God."

"Guedy  25  de  Fev. 
"J'estois  senciblement  touchee  d'aprendre  la  mort  de  Madame  de  Landsdown 
sachant  come  bien  cella  vous  seroit  sencible  et  a  M.  d'Ouerkirke,  J'advoue  que  J'en 
estois  mesnie  surprise  ne  croyent  pas  qu'elle  fut  si  mall,  Je  prieres  dieu  de  vous 
donner  les  consolations  qui  vous  sont  nessessaire  et  qui  assurement  ne  peuvent 
venir  que  de  lui,  et  il  semble  qu'il  vous  a  fait  de  grace  de  considerer  les  choses  de 
ce  monde  comme  vous  le  devez  Je  souhaite  de  tous  mon  coeur  que  parcette  example 
adjoustee  a  tant  d'autre  que  nous  voyons  chaque  jour  nous  pouvions  estre  autent 
detachee  du  monde  et  soumise  a  la  volontee  de  dieu  que  nous  le  devions  afin  d'estre 
prepare  a  la  mort  qui  est  si  incertaine,  Je  croit  que  vostre  fille  I'estois  et  ne  dout 
point  qu'elle  ne  soit  heureux,  ce  n'est  que  ceux  qu'elle  a  laissee  en  ce  monde  qui 
sont  a  plaindre,  ce  que  je  fais  autent  qu'il  est  possible  et  n'a  pas  voulu  manquer  de 
vous  assurer  de  !a  part  que  je  prens  en  tous  ce  qui  vous  touche  estant  autent  vostre 
Amie  que  je  le  suis. — Marie  R."

MONTROSE,  James  Graham,  Marquis  of.  b.  161 3,  executed 
1649.    A.  L.  s.    I  page  4to.    Dated  24th  March,  1645. 

Desires  him  to  hasten  out  with  all  his  fencible  and  other  men,  excepting  such  as

MORLAND,  George,  b.  1764,  d.  1804.  A.  L.  s.  Dated  St. 
Omer's,  Oct.  30,  1785  ;  also  a  Fragment  signed. 

An  account  of  his  tour  abroad,  including  notices  of  pictures,  particularly  Rubens' 
Descent  from  the  Cross — alludes  to  the  relics,  one  of  which  he  says  is  the  soul  of 
St.  Omer  in  a  large  silver  box — ''  I  keep  the  best  of  company  here  Marquiss's  &c., 
and  if  I  had  colours  I  might  paint  like  smoke,"  &c. 

MORNAY,  Philippe  De.  Seigneur  du  Plessis  Marly,  b.  1549, 
d.  1623.  Letter  subscribed  and  signed.  Addressed  to  the  father  of 
Marshal  Turenne.    Dated  London,  July  3.    [Without  date  of  year.] 

An  interesting  political  letter,  detailing  movements  in  the  Low  Countries — he 
apprehends  danger  to  the  Churches,  and  has  no  hope  from  Flanders. 

MOZART,  W.  A.  b.  1756,  d.  1791.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to  "La 
Baronne  de  Waldstetten." 

A  letter  of  the  highest  interest,  relating  to  Constance  Weber,  his  future  wife,  who 
was  then  residing  with  Madame  de  Waldstetten.  Mozart  says  that  Constance's 
mother  threatens  to  send  the  police,  if  her  daughter  does  not  return  home  at  once. 

Autograph  Music,  i  page  folio.

NELSON,  Horatio  Lord.  b.  1756,  d.  1805.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed 
to  Lady  Hamilton.    Dated  "  Madeira,  Aug.  7,  1801." 

The  letter  concludes,  "  To  our  friends  say  everything  which  is  kind  and  to  our 
enemies  damn  them."

PENN,  William,    b.  16—,  d.  1718.    A.  L.  s. 

"  My  ould  Friend  I  have  been  four  times  at  the  widdow  Aubreys  tod.ay  and

sensible  of  your  merits,  all  the  world  is  so  I  believe  ;  but  pray  condescend  to  accept 
the  Tribute  of  one  Welchvvoman."

1702  PRIOR— RICHARDSON. 

my  great  duty  and  eternal  good  wishes.  If  any  foolish  Poet  had  made  Anagrams

RALEIGH,  Sir  Walter,  b.  1552,  beheaded  1618.  A.  L.  s.  To 
his  Nephew  Sir  John  Gilbert  Kniglit.  14th  of  July  (a  little  before  the 
sailing  of  the  Island  expedition  under  Essex,  on  Aug.  17,  1597). 
With  address  and  seal. 

A.  L.  s.    Addressed  "  To  my  very  worthy  frind  Sir  Walter 


Cope  Knight.    Oct.  5  "  [no  year].    A  long  and  most  remarkable  letter, 
of  touching  and  painful  interest. 

"  My  desire  is,  that  you  will  be  pleased  to  moue  my  Lord  Treasurer  in  my  be- 
half, that  by  his  grace  my  wife  might  agayne  be  made  a  prisoner  with  me,  as  she 
hath  bine  for  six  yeeres  last  past.  Shee  being  now  deuided  from  me,  and  therby 
to  my  great  impoverishing  I  am  driven  to  keip  to  houses.  A  miserable  sute  it  is, 
and  yet  great  to  me,  who,  in  this  wretched  estate  can  hope  for  no  other  thing,  than 
peacible  sorrow."  The  letter  closes  thus  :  "  S'',  the  matter  is  of  no  great  impor- 
tance (though  a  cruell  destinie  hath  made  it  so  to  me)  to  desire  that  my  wife  may 
live  with  mee,  in  this  unsauery  place,  If  by  your  mediation  I  may  obtayne  it  I 
will  acknowledg  in  the  highest  degree  of  thankfullnes,  and  rest  reddy  in  trew  fayth 
to  be  commanded  by  you — W.  Ralegh." 

RAPHAEL  SANZIO  Di  Urbino.    b.  1483,  d.  1520.    Autograph 
receipt  for  two  months'  pension  of  100  ducats.    Dated  June  i,  15 17. 
"Jo  raphaello  da  Urbino  dipintore  ho  receuto  li  ducati  ciento  doro  vt  supra." 

REYNOLDS,  Sir  Joshua,  b.  1723,  d.  1792.  A.L.s.  Addressed 
to  the  Rev.  Dr.  Percy.    Dated  Leicesterfields,  March  3. 

RICHARDSON,  Samuel,  b.  1689,  d.  1761.  A.  L.  s.  A  highly 
interesting  letter,  addressed  to  some  particular  friend.  Dated  12 
January,  174I-. 

"As  to  Mr.  Garrick,  give  me  leave  to  say  what  I  know;  which  is,  that  he  was 
actually  long  ago  engaged  in  Ircjie.  The  Author  [Dr.  Johnson]  was  his  tutor,  and 
it  was  expected  to  come  on  last  season.  Garrick  had  also  engaged  to  Mr.  Lyttleton 
that  no  new  play  should  be  acted  during  the  run  of  Coriolanus.  There  was  ill-will 
between  Mr.  Thomson  and  Mr.  Garrick  on  the  score  of  Mr.  Quin  and  of  that  play, 
and  Mr.  Garrick  was  glad  of  an  opportunity  to  regain  Mr.  Lyttleton's  opinion  by 
such  a  promise.  Mr.  Millar  got  me  one  evening  last  week  to  look  upon  some  scenes 
of  Merope  at  his  house,  I  took  no  notice  of  having  seen  it ;  but  read  some  parts  of 
it,  as  new  to  me,  and  that  before  his  wife  and  her  sister,  two  intelligent  women,"  &c. 
He  also  alludes  to  various  other  matters  of  literary  and  theatrical  interest. 

A.  L.  s.    Addres.scd  to  Mi.ss  Grainger,  dated  3  Dec,  1749. 

A  long  and  interesting  letter  in  which  he  discusses  the  characters  of  Clarissa 
.Tnd  Calista

A  letter  of  the  highest  interest.  Is  .inxious  to  get  to  Paris  to  correct  the  Dic- 
tionary, does  not  know  how  to  get  there,  and  fears  he  will  be  turned  out  if  his  pre-

RUBENS,  P.  P.  b.  IS77,  d.  1640.  A.  L.  s.  "A  Monsieur  dc  Pay 
a  Paris."    Dated  Antwerp,  17  Sept.  1626. 

An  interesting  letter  in  Italian. 

A.  L.  s.    In  Italian.    Dated  Madrid,  April  22,  1629. 

He  does  not  know  if  "  I'iniquita  della  staggion  corrente  "  will  allow  him  to  see 
his  correspondent  and  serve  him  in  Provence,  but  it  is  possible  that  he  may  first  go 
to  Paris.  He  thanks  him  for  having  sent  a  fine  Heroic  Inscription  which  he  has 
shown  to  the  Count  of  Olivares.  The  rest  of  this  extremely  interesting  letter  is 
filled  with  important  political  news.  In  a  postscript  he  wishes  to  be  remembered  to 
the  Abbate  di  St.  Ambrosio  and  M.  de  la  Motte. 

RUSKIN,  John.    A.  L.  s. 

SCHILLER,  F.  von.  b.  1759,  d.  1805.  A.  L.  s.  To  Goethe. 
Dated  Jena,  31  Aug.  1794. 

"  Our  tardy  acquaintance,  which  has  raised  so  many  hopes  within  me  is  another 
proof  how  much  better  it  is  to  allow  hazard  to  reign,  instead  of  interfering  by  pre- 
mature activity.  Do  not  expect  to  find  in  me  great  wealth  of  ideas  ;  this  is  what  I 
shall  find  in  you,"  &:c.,  &c.

I704  SHELLEY—STEELE. 

SHELLEY,  P.  B.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to  Joseph  Severn,  Esq. 
Dated  "Pisa,  29  Nov.  1821." 

"  I  send  you  the  Elegy  on  poor  Keats,  and  wish  it  was  better  worth  your  accep- 
tance. In  spite  of  his  transcendant  genius  Keats  never  was  nor  ever  will  be  a 
popular  poet,  and  the  total  neglect  and  obscurity  in  which  the  astonishing  remnants 
of  his  mind  still  lie,  was  hardly  to  be  dissipated  by  a  writer,  who,  however  he  may 
differ  from  Keats  in  many  important  qualities,  at  least  resembles  him  in  that  acci- 
dental one,  a  want  of  popularity." 

SHENSTONE,  William,  b.  1714,  d.  1763.  A.L.s.  Dated  "  The 
Lessowes,"  Nov.  20,  1762. 

Complains  that  dizziness  of  head  and  depression  of  spirits  are  at  best  no  trivial 
maladies,  and  great  discouragement  to  writing.  Had  not  yet  wTitten  to  Mr.  Graves, 
and,  in  reference  to  Percy's  Ballads,  hopes  they  still  have  merit  to  engage  the 
public,  but  would  have  rejected  all  such  ballads  as  had  no  plea  but  their  antiquity. 
A  long  and  interesting  letter  referring  to  the  Lessowes  and  other  matters.

STERNE— TURENNE.  1705 

perfection,  but  I  know  more  for  your  friends  than  yourself.  I  begg  the  favour  of 
you  to  obtain  of  Her  Royal  Highness  her  pleasure  herein,  and  you  will  infinitely 
oblige." 

STERNE,  Laurence,  b.  171 3,  d.  1768.  A.  L.  s.  Addressed  to 
Mr.  Mills,  Merchant,  Philpot  Lane,  London.  Dated  "  Montpellier, 
Nov.  24,  1763." 

Requests  the  loan  of  Fifty  Pounds,  has  been  to  Bagniers  in  the  Pyrenees,  where 
he  has  been  very  ill  ;  will  call  on  him  as  soon  as  he  returns  to  London ;  with  a 
thousand  thanks,  and  the  Interest  (at  least);  as  for  the  Capital,  the  whole  Shandean 
family  will  stand  bound  for  it. 

SWIFT,  Jonathan,  b.  1667,  d.  1745.  A.  L.  s.  For  John  Temple, 
Esq.     Dated  Dublin,  15  June,  1706. 

"  I  am  extremely  obliged  by  your  kind  invitation  to  More  Park,  which  no  time 
will  make  me  forget  or  love  less.  If  I  love  Ireland  better  than  I  did,  it  is  because 
we  are  nearer  related,  for  I  am  deeply  allyed  to  its  Poverty.  My  little  Revenue  is 
sunk  two  Parts  in  three,  and  the  third  is  in  Arrear.  Therefore  if  I  come  to  More 
Park,  it  must  be  on  foot,"  &c.  &c.

TAYLOR.jEREMY,  Bp.  of  Down.  b.  1613,  d.  1667.  A.L.  s.  Dated 
Nov.  15,  1656. 

Commences  thus  :  "  In  the  midst  of  all  the  discouragements  which  I  meet 
withall  in  an  ignorant  and  obstinate  age,  it  is  a  great  comfort  to  mee,  and  I  receive 
new  degrees  of  confidence  when  I  find  that  your  selfe  and  such  other  ingenious 
and  learned  persons  as  your  selfe,  are  not  only  patient  of  truth,  and  love  it  better 
than  prejudice  and  prepossession,  but  are  so  ingenuous  as  to  dare  to  owne  it  in 
despite  of  the  contradictory  voices  of  error  and  unjust  partiality."

WREN,  Sir  Christopher,  b.  1632,  d.  1723.  A.  L.  s.  Dated 
Whitehall,  21  June,  1699. 

Respecting  his  inability  to  complete  his  professional  engagements,  of  which  he 
assigns  the  cause.  The  letter  begins  :  "  The  ready  execution  of  his  M"'''.  comandes 
is  to  me  soe  sacred  a  thing,  that  if  I  am  unavoydably  hindered  in  it,  I  thinke  in  that 
case  it  is  my  duty  to  let  him  know  my  misfortune." 


ENGRAVINGS  ON  WOOD  AND  METAL. 

S^C.  XV.— XVIII. 


9  V

ENGRAVINGS. 

DOTTED  PLATES  OR  "SCHROTBLATTER." 

THE  MASS  OF  ST.  GREGORY. 
The  facsimile  which  is  given  opposite  obviates  the  necessity  for  any  description. 

THE  DEATH  OF  THE  VIRGIN. 

This  print,  like  "The  Mass  of  St.  Gregory,"  is  so  well  represented  by  the  fac- 
simile as  to  render  any  detailed  account  of  it  unnecessary. 

These  remarkable  specimens  are  both  unique.  They  were  found  pasted  in  the 
covers  of  a  Latin  Bible  printed  by  Koberger  at  Nuremberg  in  1478,  and  are 
mentioned  by  Dr.  Willshire  in  his  "Introduction  to  the  Study  and  Collection  of 
Ancient  Prints,"  as  the  largest  examples  he  had  seen  of  prints  of  this  description. 

ST.  OSZWALT,  ST.  NOPURG. 

The  two  figures  stand  upright,  facing  one  another:  St.  Nopurg  on  the  right 
hand,  St.  Oswald  on  the  left.  The  latter  holds,  in  his  right  hand,  a  bird ;  in  his 
left  a  sceptre.  St.  Nopurg  approaches  one  hand  to  a  serpent  which  stands  upright 
before  her ;  the  other  is  not  seen.  At  the  top  of  the  pillar,  on  her  right,  is  engraved 
a  shield,  with  a  hunting-horn.  On  the  opposite  pillar,  behind  St.  Oswald,  is  a 
shield,  intersected  by  wavy  lines.     Size,  6'g  by  4|. 

ST.  CRISTINA. 

A  companion  print  to  the  preceding.  The  saint  stands  upright,  holding  in  front 
of  her  a  mill-stone,  which  is  hung  by  a  chain  round  her  neck.  On  each  side  are 
pillars  twined  with  oak-leaves  and  acorns.  Beneath,  engraved  in  white  with  black 
ground,  the  legend,  "  O  Sancta  Cristina  ora  pro  nobis."     Size,  6i  by  4^'^. 

EARLY  GERMAN  ENGRAVINGS  ON  WOOD  OR 

METAL. 

A  XYLOGRAPHIC  INDULGENCE.  In  the  centre  is  a  half- 
length  figure  of  the  Virgin,  who  holds  the  infant  Christ  on  her  left  arm. 
Round  this  figure  is  a  legend  in  German,  which  runs  thus  :  "  Das  ist 
die  bildnus  d'  all'  saligste  Junkfrowe  male  I  de  klaidn  vn  det  scmik 
welche  sie  getziert  wz  an  de  hochzytliche  veste  so  si  halsucht  de  tepel 
zu  Jerusale  als  v6  ir  schrybt  d'  wirdig  beda  T  ain  omely  also  hat 
sy  abgemalt  lucas  Ewagel ist  welches  gmeid  vn  kunt'fey  ma  noch  zu  ro 
hat."     Beneath   the   cut   are   ten   xylographic  lines,  stating   the   in

dulgence:  "  Bapst  sixtus  der  vierde  hat  alien  den  die  disz  nach- 
geschriben  gebette  andachtigclichen  sprechen  xi  tusent  iar  wares 
ablasz  geben  vnnd  verlihen  als  offt  vnd  dicke  sie  das  sprechen  Wel- 
liches  gebetter  in  ainer  kranckhait  zu  lob  vnd  ere  der  Junckfrowen

maria  ain  mut  gottes  ain  kingin  der  himel  ain  port  des  paradys  vn  ain

Amen."    Size,  lof  by  ']\. 

It  is  most  probable  that  this  remarkable  broadside  is  unique. 

CHRIST'S  SUMMONS  TO  MAN.  An  early  German  Wood 
Engraving  of  very  remarkable  character.  On  the  right  side  is  a 
full-length  figure  of  Christ,  naked  except  a  cloth  round  the  waist, 
and  bound  by  a  rope  to  a  pillar,  and  a  large  cross.  In  the  left 
hand  he  holds  a  scourge  of  twigs,  and  in  the  right  a  rope  which 
encircles  the  waist  of  a  man  with  shaven  crown,  and  also  naked 
but  for  a  cloth  round  the  loins,  who  holds  a  heart  in  his  two 
hands.  Above  the  Christ  is  a  scroll  with  xylographic  inscription, 
"  Sun  gib  mir  din  licrtz  den  ich  licb  liab.  Deni  lass  ich  straff  nit 
ab."  In  front  of  the  other  figure  is  a  scroll  with  legend,  "  O  lierr  das 
zvill  ich  das  bcger  ich  darnnibc  so  soltu  ziclic  mich."  On  the  left  is  a  tree, 
on  which  is  hung  a  shield,  the  lower  half  white,  the  upper  half  black  ;  this 
is  probably  the  artist's  mark.  The  piece  is  of  great  merit  as  a  work  of 
art,  the  figures  being  drawn  with  much  feeling,  and  admirably  engraved. 
This  very  valuable  example  of  early  German  art  has  been  printed  with  a  rubber, 
not  at  a  printing  press,  as  the  back  of  it  plainly  shows.     It  measures  5-j^  by  7J5. 

ST.  VERONICA  HOLDING  THE  SUDARIUM,  with  a  large 
head  of  the  Saviour  upon  it.  Size,  y\  by  5.  A  very  beautiful  specimen 
of  early  German  art  of  the  best  school,  finely  and  delicately  executed. 
St.  Veronica  stands  erect,  with  outstretched  arms,  holding  the  Sudarium 
in  front  of  her.  She  wears  a  turban,  and  the  nimbus  above  her  head 
touches  the  black  line  which  surrounds  the  cut.  A  glory  radiates 
from  the  head  of  Christ  thus — eleven  rays  on  the  right  side,  thirteen 
on  the  left,  and  seven  from  the  top.  It  is  printed  in  a  pale  blackish 
ink.  There  is  an  inscription  in  MS.  at  the  foot,  with  the  date  of  1473; 
but  the  engraving  is  probably  earlier. 

A  SAINT,  kneeling,  with  a  crown  on  his  head,  and  holding  between 
his  hands  a  double  cross.  He  is  clad  in  a  goatskin,  and  has  a  girdle  of 
vine-leaves  round  his  waist.  An  angel  appears  in  the  clouds,  holding  a 
consecrated  wafer  with  outstretched  hand.  Printed  in  a  pale  ink.  It 
measures,  exclusive  of  the  border,  5|  by  41. 


ENGRAVINGS.  1713 

Early  German  Engravings — continued. 

ST.  CATHERINE.  A  full-length  figure,  turned  nearly  full  face  to 
the  right,  with  a  crown  on  her  head  ;  an  open  book  in  the  right  hand, 
and  a  sword  in  the  left.  Beneath  her  feet  is  a  prostrate  figure  of  a 
king  ;  behind  her,  the  wheel ;  with  a  town  in  the  background,  and  a 
castle  on  a  hill.    Size,  6f  by  4|-. 

THE  MARTYRDOM  OF  ST.  URSULA  and  her  Companions. 
St.  Ursula,  crowned,  sits  about  the  middle  of  the  ship,  but  rather 
towards  the  left.  At  one  end  is  a  bishop  who  receives  a  thrust  in  the 
eye  from  the  lance  of  a  soldier.  At  the  other  end  of  the  ship  is  a  seated 
pope,  and  in  the  centre,  by  the  side  of  St.  Ursula,  a  cardinal.  A  female, 
the  back  of  whose  head  is  pierced  by  an  arrow,  hangs  over  the  side, 
with  her  hair  in  the  water,  in  which  two  women  and  one  man  are 
struggling  separately.  At  the  top  of  the  picture  on  the  right, 
Cologne  Cathedral  is  introduced,  with  the  crane  on  the  top.  The 
composition  is  enclosed  by  two  black  lines.  Measuring  from  the  inner 
one,  the  size  is  <^\  by  7. 

ST.  JEROME  AND  THE  LION.  Size,  /i  by  5f  St.  Jerome 
is  seated,  and  wears  the  cardinal's  hat,  the  brim  of  which  intersects  two 
of  the  three  pillars  of  the  cloister  behind  him.  His  left  hand  rests  on 
the  lion's  paw,  and  with  the  right  he  places  his  stile  between  the  animal's 
claws.  On  the  right  side  of  the  cut  is  a  desk,  with  an  open  book  on  it. 

THE  MARTYRDOM  OF  ST.  SEBASTIAN.  Size,  n  by  5- 
The  saint  is  tied  to  a  tree,  while  two  men  and  two  boys  on  each  side  of 
him  shoot  at  him  with  cross-bows.  In  the  foreground  a  third  boy  ties 
his  feet  with  a  cord.  Beneath  the  cut  are  fifteen  lines  of  text,  con- 
taining an  invocation  to  the  saint  against  the  plague,  entirely  engraved 
on  wood. 

THE  HOLY  ROOD.  Christ  extended  on  the  Cross  while  angels 
hold  chalices  for  the  blood  from  his  wounds.  The  emblem  of  the 
Evangelists  within  circles,  one  at  each  extremity  of  the  Cross.  On 
either  side  are  St.  John  and  the  Blessed  Virgin.  Above  the  Cross  the 
sun  and  the  moon.  Beneath,  a  xylographic  inscription  of  the  beginning 
of  St.  John's  Gospel — "In  de  afag  wz  dz  vodt,"  &c. — eleven  lines. 
On  the  left  is  the  name  of  the  engraver,  "  Caspar."  This  is  evidently 
printed  from  metal.  Size,  \2\  by  7]-,  exclusive  of  a  portion  of  an 
ornamental  border  which  remains  on  the  left  side,  and  which  no  doubt 
originally  surrounded  the  plate. 

THE  DEATH  OF  THE  BLESSED  VIRGIN.  Size,  I2i  by  8. 
A  very  fine  and  remarkable  early  woodcut,  containing  fifteen  figures.

Early  German  'Esckkw^g?,— continued. 

The  Virgin  is  extended  on  a  bed.  St.  Peter  holds  a  candle  and  book, 
and  another  saint  has  in  his  hand  a  chalice.  A  third  holds  the  released 
spirit  of  the  Virgin  in  his  arms.  On  the  front  side  of  the  bed  are  two 
reclining  figures  of  saints;  one  appears  to  sleep,  the  other  reads.  In 
the  centre  are  two  angels  holding  flambeaux. 

A  CRUCIFIXION.  Of  very  early  and  remarkable  design.  Size, 
lOi  by  7^.  On  the  right  hand  stands  St.  John,  on  the  left  the  Blessed 
Virgin. 

THE  BLESSED  VIRGIN  showing  the  Infant  Christ  to  St. 
Bridget.  The  saint  kneels  on  the  right :  the  Virgin  a  little  higher  on 
the  left,  with  the  infant  Christ  before  her.  In  front,  a  shield  with  a  lion 
rampant.  Beneath  is  engraved  the  legend,  "  Da  zeigt  mala  S.  birgita 
wie  sie  Jhs  geboren  hat."    Size,  4  by  2\. 

CHRIST  BEING  STRIPPED  FOR  SCOURGING.  A  soldier 
drags  the  clothes  from  our  Saviour,  while  a  second,  standing  behind 
him,  holds  them.  On  the  left  is  Caiaphas,  with  his  staff  of  office  ;  and 
behind  him  a  third  soldier  with  a  halberd.  Beneath  are  three  lines  of 
xylographic  inscription, "  O  her  ihu  xpe  duech  die  abziehung  vnd  menig 
diner  wunden  hilf  vns  christen  menschen  zii  alien  stunden."  Size,  5|- 
by4i. 

ST.  ANDREW  HOLDING  HIS  CROSS.     Size,  3  by  2i. 

THREE  NAKED  INFANTS,  each  with  a  nimbus,  gathering 
roses,  which  a  female  saint  collects  in  a  basket.     Size,  3^  by  2i. 

THE  DEATH  OF  THE  TEN  THOUSAND  MARTYRS, 
symbolized  by  four  saints  thrown  from  a  rock.     Size,  3  by  2^. 

ST.  JOHN  BAPTIST  preaching  in  the  Wilderness.  The  lamb 
in  his  arms.    Size,  3  by  2^. 

ST.  JOHN  THE  EVANGELIST,  with  the  Chalice.  Size,  3  by  2^. 

THE  ANGELIC  ANNOUNCEMENT  of  Christ's  Birth.  An 
angel  appears  from  the  clouds  on  the  left  to  a  shepherd  on  the  right : 
four  sheep  are  in  front  of  the  shepherd  and  one  behind  him.  Size, 
3  by  2i. 

ST.  CATHERINE.  On  the  right  stands  the  executioner,  the 
saint  in  the  centre,  the  wheel  on  the  left.  Over  it  appears  an  angel

Early  German  Engravings — continued. 

THE  SHIP  WITH  ST.  URSULA  and  five  of  her  Companions  : 
on  the  shore  two  soldiers ;  one  shoots  with  a  cross-bow,  the  other 
strikes  a  woman  with  his  sword.    Size,  3^  by  2\. 

ST.  MEYNRATH.  In  his  right  hand  he  holds  a  church  ;  a  bird 
is  perched  on  his  shoulder.     Size,  3  by  2. 

THE  HOLY  ROOD.  Christon  the  Cross,  with  St.  John  and  the 
Virgin  beneath.     Size,  31  by  2|. 

THE  MAN  OF  SORROWS.  Christ,  standing  upright,  displays 
his  wounds.  A  rich  tapestry  background,  behind  which  are  two  angels. 
Size,  3  by  2\. 

THE  SHIP  OF  ST.  URSULA.  The  saint  with  three  com- 
panions :  her  forehead  is  pierced  by  an  arrow.  On  the  shore  a  soldier. 
Size,  3^  by  2^.    Very  delicately  engraved,  and  printed  in  a  pale  ink. 

THE  OFFERINGS  OF  THE  MAGI.  On  the  right  the  Blessed 
Virgin  with  the  infant  Christ  in  her  arms,  St.  Joseph  behind  her.  On 
the  left  kneels  one  of  the  kings,  the  two  others  behind  bring  gifts. 
Surrounded  by  a  border  of  oak  leaves.     Size,  3^  by  4^. 

ST.  ERMERAN  AND  ST.  ALBAN.  St.  Ermeran  holds  his  head 
in  his  hand  ;  St.  Alban,  to  the  left,  holds  a  crozier  in  his  right  hand 
and  an  executioner's  sword  in  the  left.     Size,  74-  by  5. 

ST.  LAWRENCE  AND  ST.  STEPHEN.  St.  Lawrence,  on 
the  right,  holds  in  one  hand  the  gridiron,  in  the  other  a  book.  St. 
Stephen  carries  in  his  right  hand  a  stone,  in  his  left  a  palm-branch. 
Size,  -ji^  by  5. 

SOULS  IN  PURGATORY,  to  whom  an  Angel  from  above 
ministers.    Size,  3  by  2i. 

THE  LAST  SUPPER.  Christ  and  His  disciples  round  a  table. 
The  Saviour  in  the  centre  opposite,  St.  John  lying  on  His  breast. 
Size,  3  by  2\. 

ST.  CHRISTOPHER  crossing  the  River.  A  diminutive  and  curious 
xylograph.    Size,  i  ;^  by  l\. 

THE  HOLY  ROOD.  A  very  early  example.  Christ  hangs  on 
the  cross.  St.  John  to  the  right ;  the  Blessed  Virgin  to  the  left.  Size, 
3^-by2|. 

CHRIST  DISPLAYING  HIS  WOUNDS.  He  stands  upright  in 
front  of  the  tomb  :  behind,  the  instruments  of  the  Passion  are  suspended 
on  the  cross.    Size,  5^  by  3^.

ENGRA  VINGS. 


Early  German  Engravings — continued. 

THE  VINE  OF  ST.  DOMINIC.  An  extraordinary  and  very 
fine  xylograph,  of  most  unusual  dimensions.  Size,  1 5^  by  io|-.  With 
xylographic  inscriptions  at  top  and  bottom  of  two  h'nes  each.  At  the 
foot  lies  St.  Dominic,  and  from  his  breast  springs  forth  a  vine,  among 
the  branches  of  which  are  represented  twenty-two  men  and  women 
saints,  the  great  lights  of  the  order,  beneath  each  of  whom  is  a  xylo- 
graphic  inscription  in  Latin  on  a  riband.  On  the  top  in  the  centre  is  a 
full-length  figure  of  the  Blessed  Virgin,  who  hands  her  mantle  to  Pope 
Innocent.    Dated  1473.    Collectio  Weigeliana,  vol.  i.  p.  278. 


ENGRAVINGS   ON    COPPER. 
JOHN  OF  COLOGNE. 

A  series  of  forty-seven  engravings  on  copper,  representing  the  Life 
and  Passion  of  Christ,  unknown  to  Bartsch,  but  described  by  Passa- 
vant,  vol.  ii.  pp.  180-184.  The  complete  set  consists  of  fifty-two  prints, 
but  only  two  other  series  besides  the  present  are  known,  one  of  which 
consists  of  fifty  pieces,  and  the  other  of  forty-nine.  The  series  of  fifty 
was  formerly  in  the  possession  of  Mr.  T.  O.  Weigel,  of  Leipzig,  and  is 
described  in  the  catalogue  of  his  collection,  vol.  ii.  p.  356.  At  his  sale 
in  1 87 1  it  was  purchased  for  the  Dresden  Museum  for  the  sum  of 
.^200.  When  bought  by  Mr.  Weigel  it  was  coloured,  but  under  his 
direction  the  paint  was  removed  from  forty-eight  out  of  the  fifty  prints. 
The  series  of  forty-nine  is  in  the  British  Museum. 

The  series  here  described  is  in  fine  condition  and  free  from  colour. 
It  consists  of  the  following  pieces,  the  numbers  being  taken  from 
Passavant : — 

41.  The  Last  Supper.     (This  is  not 
in  the  British  Museum  series.)

Feet.

48.  Christ  before  Pilate. 

49.  Christ     brought     again     before 
Herod.

before  Pihite.

JOB  AND  HIS  FRIENDS.  An  undescribed  piece  by  the  Master 
of  the  monogram  "S."  See  Passavant,  vol.  iii.  p.  47.  Job  is  seated  on 
the  left,  looking  towards  the  right.  He  is  naked,  except  for  a  cloth 
thrown  across  his  lap.  Behind  stands  his  wife,  and  a  little  to  the 
right  stands  the  Devil,  who  prepares  to  hit  him  with  a  large  club. 
Two  of  his  friends  stand  before  him  at  the  right  of  the  picture,  and 
two  faces  are  seen  behind  them.  In  the  background  a  town  is  repre- 
sented in  flames. 

SAINT  PAUL.  An  undescribed  print  with  monogram  "  fGI  ". 
The  saint  stands  in  the  centre,  turned  towards  the  left,  leaning  on  a 
large  sword;  over  his  head  is  an  ornamental  design  on  which  are 
seated  two  little  angels.  The  finials  of  the  ornament  are  cherub  heads. 
Size  2\  by  i ;. 

9  z 


are  ascribed  to  the  Master  of  the  Gothic 
letters,  "E.  S.,"  1466;  but  Passavant 
thinks  they  are  not  by  him.  No  com- 
plete series  of  them  is  known,  and  it  is 
uncertain  of  how  many  it  should  consist. 
Of  the  prints  described  above,  not  more 
than  two  or  three  examples  exist  in  any 
collections. 


i/iS  ENGRAVINGS. 

Anonymous  Engravings  on  Copper — continued. 

ST.  SERVATIUS.  By  the  Master  "  M."  See  Nagler,  "  Mono- 
grammisten  Lexicon,"  vol.  iv.  No.  1463,  and  Passavant,  vol.  iii.  p.  89, 
No.  6. 

AN  ORNAMENTAL  PIECE  OF  FOLIAGE  WITH  SIX 
BIRDS.  Engraved  by  a  Master  of  the  latter  end  of  the  fifteenth  cen- 
tury; of  very  great  power  and  beauty,  and,  so  far  as  can  be  ascertained, 
quite  undescribed.    Size  4|-  by  2^^. 

CHRIST  CROWNED  WITH  THORNS.  Engraved  on  copper 
and  believed  to  belong  to  an  undescribed  series  of  the  Passion  by 
a  Dutch  engraver.  Christ  is  seated  in  the  centre,  at  the  foot  of  a 
column  ;  on  each  side  a  soldier,  who  presses  on  the  crown  with  a  stick  ; 
the  heads  of  two  soldiers  are  seen  behind.  The  engraving  is  coloured 
like  a  miniature,  and  is  taken  from  a  Dutch  MS.  book  of  prayers. 
Size  3  by  2. 

ST.  MICHAEL.  A  very  early  and  undescribed  Engraving  by 
a  goldsmith-engraver,  of  very  fine  design  and  execution.  It  repre- 
sents St.  Michael  the  Archangel,  standing  upright,  enveloped  in  an 
ample  robe.  His  flowing  hair  is  surmounted  by  a  chaplet  of  flowers, 
and  behind  this  is  an  aureole  of  rich  design.  The  one  hand  holds  a 
small  shield,  which  may  be  intended  for  the  sun,  at  the  sight  of  which 
a  host  of  demons  flee  all  around.  The  other  hand,  uplifted,  holds  a 
sword.  From  the  fact  that  the  sword  appears  in  the  engraving  to  be  in 
the  left  hand,  it  is  probable  that  it  was  not  intended  to  take  impressions 
from  the  plate.  It  is  most  likely  an  unique  impression,  and  is  of  great 
beauty  and  value.    Executed  about  1450-60. 

THE  PASSION  OF  CHRIST.  A  .series  of  twenty-six  Copper- 
plate Engravings  of  the  Flemish  School,  representing  the  passion  and 
death  of  Christ.  The  series  begins  with  "  The  Entry  into  Jerusalem," 
and  ends  with  "The  Last  Judgment."  It  was  probably  executed  from 
1470  to  1480,  if  not  earlier,  and  so  far  as  can  be  ascertained  it  is  quite 
undescribed.  Each  plate  is  surrounded  by  a  border  formed  of  three 
or  four  straight  lines.  The  size,  counting  from  the  innermost  of  these, 
is  2|-  by  li. 

THE  PASSION  OF  CHRIST.  A  scries  of  forty-five  Copper- 
plate Engravings,  representing  the  birth,  passion  and  sufferings  of 
Christ.  Probably  designed  and  engraved  by  Alart  Claaszen,  or  one 
of  his  scholars,  at  Utrecht,  about  1540.    Sm.  8vo. 

This  set  of  plates  appears  to  be  almost  unknown  to  print  collectors.  It  is  in  a 
Stamped  calf  binding  of  the  sixteenth  century,  witli  a  number  of  blank  leaves,  which 
the  original  possessor  has  filled  with  MS.  devotions. 


ENGRAVINGS.  17 19 

ISRAEL  VAN  MECKENEN. 

A  Monkey  tied  to  a  Stake  :  in  his  hand  he  holds  a  plant  which 
he  has  pulled  up  by  the  root.  This  fragment  in  all  probability  is  cut 
from  one  of  the  series  of  plates,  of  which  two  arc  described  by  Passavant, 
vol.  ii.  p.  197,  Nos.  253,  254.  But  the  plate  from  which  this  is  taken  is 
undescribed.  At  Dresden  are  a  number  of  similar  prints  representing 
animals,  said  to  be  intended  for  playing-cards.

the  first  edition.  Not  more  than  two  or  three  complete  sets  are  known  to  e.xist. 
There  are  in  all  ninety-two  separate  woodcuts.

Heller,  2169.  Passavant,  iii.  p.  225,  No.  343.  Both  Heller  and  Passa- 
vant  point  out  that  Bartsch  is  mistaken  in  calling  this  portrait  "  Guil- 
laume,  Electeur  de  Saxe,"  and  remark  that  he  had  seen  only  the 
impressions  printed  in  purple  and  reddish-brown  chiaro-scuro,  and 
was  unacquainted  with  this  impression  in  black  and  white,  which  has 
a  German  poem  at  the  side  headed  "  Friderich  der  drit,  Churfurst 
vnd  Hertzog  zu  Sachssen,"  &c.,  but  has  not  the  monogram  of  Durer  or 
the  date  1519,  both  of  which  appear  on  the  chiaro-scuro  impressions. 


1/28  ENGRAVINGS. 

ALBRECHT    T)\}^Y.Vi—coHtinmd. 

Neither  of  these  authorities,  however,  remark  that  the  chiaro-scuro 
impressions  are,  in  fact,  copies  from  the  print  with  the  poem  at  the 
side,  instead  of  being,  as  they  would  imply,  from  the  same  block.

HANS  BURGKMAIR. 

Proof  impressions  of  thirty-four  of  the  wood  engravings,  illustrating 
the  History  of  Tevvrdannkh. 

The  Battle  of  Saint  Ulrich,  in  the  year  958.  Pass.  iii.  p.  278, 
No.  109.  Bartsch,  vii.  p.  286,  No.  74,  attributes  this  woodcut  to  Lucas 
Cranach.  At  the  top  is  the  inscription  "  S.  Ulrichs  Schlacmt, 
1520  ;"  beneath  is  an  inscription  of  seventeen  lines,  which  is  not  noticed 
by  the  above-named  authorities,  nor  is  it  alluded  to  in  Schuchardt's 
"  Lucas  Cranach  des  Aeltern  Leben  und  Werke."  It  reads  as  follows  : — 

Als  man  zehlt  955.  Jahr/  da  seynd  die  Ungern  mit  4.  Fiirsten  oder  Haupt- 
leuten/  grausamer  dann  kein  Tiirck/  niit  unzalbarer  menge  in  Teutschland  gezogen/ 
vnd  da  verhergt  vnd  verwlistet  das  gantze  Bayrland/  vnd  Schwaben/  bisz  an  die 
Thonaw  vnnd  Yler/  da  keiner  Wiirdigkeit  oder  Geschlecht/  Alter  vnd  Jugendt 
verschont/  sonder  alles  mit  grimm  vnnd  zorn  erwiirgt  vnd  verfolgt  haben  bisz  in 
Todt.  Die  Statt  Augspurg  beliigert/  daselben  S.  Afra  Gottshausz  vnnd  Jacober 
Vorstatt/  ausserhalb  der  Statt  gelegen/  verbriinnt.  Da  isl  S.  Ulrich/ zu  rettung  der 
Statt  mit  seinein  Kriegsvolck  vnnd  andachtigem  Gebet  sitzend  auff  eincm  Rosz  ohn 
Harnisch/  in  Alb  vnnd  Stol/  den  Ungern  enlgegen  zogen/  da  auch  zu  ihm  koTnen 
Keyser  Otto  der  erst/  mit  Heerskraft't/  mit  vielen  Fiirsten  vnnd  Herren/  Daselb  in 
gar  grossem  Fechten  vnd  .Streit/  hat  S.  Ulrich  vmb  Gott  erworben  den  Sig  wider 
die  Feind/  Und  zu  einem  VVahrreichen  ist  ihm  sichtbarlich  darbraclit  von  eim 
Engel  Gottes  vom  Himmel  ein  Creutz/  welches  in  seinem  Gottshausz  noch  vor- 
handen.  Nach  solchem  seynd  die  Ungern  sampt  einem  der  Hauptleut  zu  Todt 
geschlagen/  vil  in  der  flucht  im  Lech  vnnd  Thonaw  ertruncken/  auch  seynd  da 
gefangen  worden  die  andere  drey  Fiirsten  vnnd  Oberste  Hauptleut  mit  vil  andern 
Ungern  gen  Regenspurg  gefuhrt/  vnd  an  den  Galge  gehengckt  worden.  In  disem 
Streit  ist  Hertzog  Conradt  von  Worms  erschlossen  worden  mit  eim  pfeyl/  der  desz 
Keysers  Otto  Tochterman  war/  Nachmals  S.  Ulrichs  Bruder  Diebaldus/  ein  Graue 
zu  Kyburg/  vnd  Reginbaldus  ein  Herzog  zu  Schwaben/  S.  Ulrichs  Schwester  Sohn 
erschlagen  worden/  mit  vil  anderm  Christlichem  Volck.  Die  Schlacht  ist  geschehen 
dem  10.     August!/  an  S.  Lorentzen  tag   im  obgeschritenen  Jahr/  &c.

1734  ENGRA  VINGS. 

MATTHIAS  GERON  VON  LAUINGEN. 

A  series  of  forty-eight  most  rare  and  beautiful  wood-engravings,  by 
this  little-known  artist,  the  greater  number  of  them  entirely  undescribed. 

This  remarkable  collection  is  in  a  folio  volume  in  the  original  black 
morocco  binding,  with  the  date  of  1637  impressed  on  the  side. 

Bartsch  did  not  even  know  the  name  of  the  artist,  but  places  him 
among  "les  maitres  inconnus,"  and  gives  a  list  of  only  five  pieces  from 
the  Apocalypse,  saying,  "  Nous  ignorons  le  nombre  veritable  de  cette 
suite  puisque  nous  n'en  avons  vu  que  ces  cinq  pieces." 

Nagler,  in  his  "  Monogrammisten,"  says  that  Passavant  mentions 
a  series  of  fifty-four  pieces  as  existing  at  Wolft'enbuttel,  but  he  gives 
no  list  of  them,  and  says  that  he  himself  knew  but  seven.  It  is  worthy 
of  remark  that  one  of  the  prints  described  below  is  four  years  earlier 
than  any  date  given  by  Nagler. 

1.  Christ  driving  the  Buyers  and  Sellers  out  of  the  Temple.  No  date  or  mono- 
gram. 

2.  An  Allegory.  A  man  bestriding  a  brook,  with  flames  issuing  from  his  mouth. 
Monogram  in  the  right  bottnm  corner. 

3.  A  Satire  on  the  Church  of  Rome.  To  the  right,  a  nude  figure  of  a  winged 
woman  holding  forth  a  crown  and  jev.els.     Monogram  on  the  left  at  the  foot. 

4.  On  a  raised  dais  sit  four  rulers  or  kings  ;  above  them  five  figures  of  children. 
Monogram  in  right  bottom  corner,  with  date  1536.  (The  earliest  mentioned  by 
Nagler  is  1540.) 

5.  An  Allegoi"y  or  Satire.  In  the  foreground,  a  man  on  horseback,  followed  by 
a  noble  on  a  mule.  In  the  background,  Christ  sending  forth  His  disciples.  Mono- 
gram at  the  bottom  right  corner. 

6.  The  Pope,  Bishops,  and  Emperor,  in  stomi-tossed  Ships.  Monogram  in  the 
right  top  corner ;  date  1545,  in  the  left  top  corner. 

7.  Bishops,  Cardinals,  Ladies,  Rulers,  &c.,  seated  round  a  Table,  playing  at 
Cards  and  Backgammon.     Monogram  in  the  right  corner,  at  the  foot ;  date  1546. 

8.  The  Emperor,  on  horseback,  pierces  the  prostrate  Pope  with  a  spear.  Mono- 
gram to  the  left,  date  1548. 

9.  The  Sullan  and  Ecclesiastics  prostrated  by  an  Old  Man,  over  whose  head 
two  Angels  hold  a  Crown.     Monogram  in  the  left  corner,  at  the  foot  ;  no  date. 

10.  A  Bear  wearing  a  Turban,  and  a  Lion  Crowned,  attack  some  Country  Folk. 
No  date  or  monogram. 

11.  The  Pope  and  Sultan  in  a  Car,  which  is  dragged  different  ways  by  a  Bear, 
a  Lion,  and  Griffins.    Monogram  in  the  left  corner,  at  the  foot. 

12.  In  the  foreground,  a  Turk  holds  a  Woman  by  the  hair  as  he  strikes  her 
with  a  sword  ;  behind,  the  Pope  and  another  drive  a  crowd  with  spear  and  sword. 
No  date  or  monogram. 

13.  A  Church  shattered  to  pieces  by  a  Book  of  the  Gospels,  which  falls  upon 
it.     Monogram  on  the  left  at  the  foot. 

14.  The  Pope  and  other  Ecclesiastics  in  a  Caldron  ;  two  monsters  and  another 
figure,  which  has  the  papal  tiara  slung  over  its  shoulder,  blow  and  feed  the  fire. 
Monogram  in  the  left  bottom  corner  ;  date  1546. 

15.  A  Ship,  in  which  sits  Christ  preaching  to  His  Disciples  ;  above,  are  smaller 
ships  containing  the  Pope  and  the  Sultan.     Monogram  above  ;  date  1 548. 


ENGRA  VTNGS.  1735 

MATTHIAS  GERON  VON  hK\5mGEii— continued. 

16.  The  Descent  of  the  Holy  Spirit  on  the  Apostles  and  the  Virfjin  Mary. 
Devils  drive  oft"  the  Pope  and  clergy.  Monogram  and  date,  1546,  in  the  right 
corner  at  the  foot. 

17.  At  the  foot,  the  Pope,  &c.,  in  Hell ;  over  them  Moses  and  other  holy  men  ; 
at  the  top,  the  Trinity.     No  monogram  or  date.

foot,  in  the  left  corner  ;  date  1546,  at  the  top,  in  the  opposite  corner. 

19.  The  Pope  and  Ecclesiastics  in  Hell;  the  Devil  tearing  to  bits  their  Indul- 
gences.    Monogram  at  the  top,  to  the  right. 

20.  The  Pope  and  Sultan  advance  against  one  another  ;  above,  Christ  preaches 
the  Gospel  to  a  crowd  of  people.     No  monogram  or  date. 

21.  Two  Devils  on  Thrones  ;  two  Devils  winged  hold  over  their  heads  the  papal 
tiara  and  the  Sultan's  turban.     No  date  or  monogram. 

22.  The  Holy  Trinity  in  a  circular  compartment  ;  above,  below,  and  at  the 
sides,  are  represented  the  Apostles  and  Prophets. 

23.  St.  John's  Vision  of  the  Seven  Golden  Candlesticks.  Monogram  and  date, 
1544,  in  the  bottom  corner,  to  the  right. 

24.  St.  John  sees  the  Almighty  on  His  Throne,  surrounded  by  seven  Angels  with 
trumpets,  while  the  eighth  pours  forth  a  vial  on  the  earth.     No  monogram  or  date. 

25.  The  Vision  of  the  Sealed  Book  and  the  Beast  full  of  Eyes  [Rev.  iv.].  Mono- 
gram in  the  bottom  corner,  to  the  right  ;  date  1 546. 

26.  The  Vision  of  the  P'our  Horsemen.  Monogram  in  the  bottom  corner,  to 
the  right  ;  date  •r5'4"6',  in  the  opposite  corner. 

27.  The  Opening  of  the  Fifth  Seal.     Monogram  at  foot,  in  centre  ;  date  1546. 

28.  The  Wicked  calling  on  the  Rocks  and  Trees  to  fall  upon  them.  No  mono- 
gram or  date. 

29.  The  Angels  holding  back  the  Winds.  Monogram  in  centre  ;  date  1546,  in 
the  left  bottom  corner. 

30.  The  Angel  proclaiming  Woe  upon  the  Earth.  Monogram  on  the  vault,  in 
the  left  corner  ;  no  date. 

31.  The  First  Angel  blows  the  Trumpet.     No  date  or  monogram. 

32.  The  Third  Angel  blows  the  Trumpet.  Date  1547,  and  monogram,  at  the 
top,  in  the  left  corner. 

33.  The  Fifth  Angel  blows  his  Trumpet.  Date  1 547,  and  monogram,  in  the 
left  corner,  at  the  foot. 

34.  The  Loosing  of  the  Four  Angels.  Date  1553,  on  a  cloud  towards  the  top  ; 
monogram  near  the  bottom  on  the  right. 

35.  St.  John  eating  the  Book.  Date  •r5'4'6-,  towards  the  foot,  on  the  right ; 
monogram  in  the  left  corner,  at  foot.  - 

36.  Murder  of  the  Two  Witnesses  by  the  Beast.     No  date  or  monogram. 

37.  The  Woman  standing  on  the  Moon.  Monogram  in  the  left  corner,  at  the 
foot;  date  1547. 

38.  The  Beast  with  many  Heads.     Monogram  towards  the  left,  at  the  foot. 

39.  The  Lamb  on  Mount  Zion.     No  date  or  monogram. 

40.  The  Angels  with  the  Sickles.     No  date  or  monogram. 

41.  The  Opening  of  the  Seven  Vials.  Date  1547  ;  monogram  in  the  left  corner, 
at  the  foot. 

42.  The  Great  Whore  of  Babylon.    Monogram  at  the  foot. 

43.  Lamentation  of  the  Merchants  at  the  Destruction  of  Babylon.  Monogram 
at  the  top,  in  the  right  comer ;  date  1547,  in  the  opposite  corner. 

44.  Destruction  of  the  Seven-headed  Beast.     No  date  or  monogram. 


1736  ENGRAVINGS. 

MATTHIAS  GERON  VON  LAUINGEN— f^;///«;/^rt'. 

45.  The  Destruction  of  the  Whore  of  Babylon  and  the  Dragon.  No  date  or 
monogram. 

46.  The  Devil  Chained.    Date  r5'4'6  ;  and  monogram  in  the  bottom  left  corner. 

47.  Destruction  of  the  False  Prophet.    No  date  or  monogram. 

48.  The  New  Jerusalem.  Date  1546  ;  the  figures  placed  wide  apart  at  the  foot ; 
monogram  in  the  left  corner  at  the  top. 

Both  Nagler  and  Passavant  assign  to  this  artist  the  designs  in  the  sumptuous 
edition  of  the  Augsburg  Missal,  printed  at  the  expense  of  the  Cardinal  Archbishop 
by  Sebald  Mayer,  at  Dilingen  in  1555  ;  and  this  would  seem  to  explain  the  extreme 
rarity  of  these  engravings  satirizing  the  Church  of  Rome,  as  the  artist,  if  he  had 
become  a  convert  to  the  Roman  Catholic  faith,  would,  no  doubt,  have  used  all  means 
in  his  power  to  suppress  them.  Certain  it  is,  that  with  the  exception  of  the  collec- 
tion at  Wolffenbiittel,  there  appears  to  be  no  other  series  known  so  extensive  as  this. 
The  possessor  of  the  volume,  who  had  it  bound  in  1637,  supposed  the  designs  to  be 
by  Mathias  Griinewald;  but  Nagler  says  that  they  could  not  be  by  him,  as  he  died 
in  1530.

This  list  comprises  the  seventy  old 
Ballads  of  which  the  collection  con- 
sists. "The  Merchant's  Daughter  of 
Bristow  "  and  the  "  Widow  of  Watling 
Street,"  each  being  in  two  parts.  In 
the  reprint  made  in  1867  the  collec- 
tion is  described  as  "  79  Black-letter 
Ballads  ; "  the  extra  nine  consisting  of 
three  which  are  printed  on  the  same 
broadside  as  B .  Garter's  "Appelles  and 
Pigmalion,"  one  which  is  on  the  same 
broadside  as  C.  Wilson's  "  Almighty

God,  &c.,"  and  five  pieces  of  MS. 
which  are  inserted  at  the  end  of  the 
collection.
